<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the Incremental Principle of Managerial Economics. State its decision rule.**
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Incremental Principle states that *a business decision is economically sound when the additional revenue (incremental revenue) generated from a proposed change exceeds the additional cost (incremental cost) incurred due to that change*. In other words, a manager should accept a proposal if, and only if, the change produces a **positive incremental profit**.

- **Incremental Cost (IC)** = $TC_{\text{new}} - TC_{\text{old}}$
- **Incremental Revenue (IR)** = $TR_{\text{new}} - TR_{\text{old}}$
- **Decision rule:** If $IR > IC$, accept the proposal; if $IR < IC$, reject it; if $IR = IC$, the manager is indifferent. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Incremental Cost and Sunk Cost with one example each.**
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Incremental Cost | Sunk Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Additional cost arising from a change in the level of activity. | Cost already incurred and irrecoverable. |
| **Relevance** | **Relevant** — included in decision-making. | **Irrelevant** — excluded from decision-making. |
| **Example** | Extra wages paid for adding a second shift. | Book value of an old machine that has already been purchased. |

Thus, incremental costs are *future-oriented* and *changeable*, whereas sunk costs are *past-oriented* and *unchangeable*. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, every Module question in Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. You must attempt ONE of the two alternatives.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Special Order Decision**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 1]**
>
> A company manufactures **8,000 units** per month at a total cost of **₹4,80,000** and sells at **₹75 per unit**. It receives an offer to supply **2,000 additional units** to an overseas buyer at **₹55 per unit**. The extra cost of producing 2,000 units is **₹90,000**. Fixed costs remain unchanged.
>
> **(a)** Using the Incremental Principle, advise whether the company should accept the offer. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is the minimum price at which the company should accept the special order? Also state **two assumptions** of the Incremental Principle. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Status Quo.**
$Q_0 = 8{,}000$ units; $TC_0 = \text{₹4,80,000}$; Price = ₹75/unit.
$TR_0 = 8{,}000 \times 75 = \text{₹6,00,000}$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Proposed Alternative.**
$Q_1 = 10{,}000$ units; $TC_1 = 4{,}80{,}000 + 90{,}000 = \text{₹5,70,000}$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 — Incremental Revenue.**

$$
IR \;=\; 2{,}000 \times 55 \;=\; \text{₹1,10,000} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}
$$

**Step 4 — Incremental Cost.**

$$
IC \;=\; 5{,}70{,}000 \;-\; 4{,}80{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹90,000} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}
$$

**Step 5 — Incremental Profit.**

$$
\Delta \pi \;=\; IR \;-\; IC \;=\; 1{,}10{,}000 \;-\; 90{,}000 \;=\; +\text{₹20,000} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**

Since $\Delta \pi = +\text{₹20,000} > 0$, the company should **accept** the offer. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)**

**Minimum Price for the Special Order (Break-Even Price).**

The minimum price is the **Average Incremental Cost (AIC)** of the 2,000 units:

$$
AIC \;=\; \dfrac{IC}{\Delta Q} \;=\; \dfrac{90{,}000}{2{,}000} \;=\; \text{₹45 per unit} \quad \textbf{(3 Marks)}
$$

Any price **above ₹45** will add to profit; any price **below ₹45** will reduce overall profit. Hence the company should **not accept** the order at any price less than ₹45 per unit.

**Two Assumptions of the Incremental Principle:**

1. **Constant Technology & Cost Structure:** The principle assumes that the per-unit variable cost structure and the production technology remain unchanged between the two scenarios being compared. **(2 Marks)**

2. **Availability of Capacity:** The firm has idle capacity or can augment capacity without disrupting the existing level of operations; the special order does not cannibalise regular sales. **(2 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Equipment Replacement / Capacity Expansion**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern, Module 1]**
>
> A firm is operating with an old machine whose **book value is ₹40,000** (original cost ₹1,00,000) and which can be sold in the market for **₹25,000**. The machine's annual operating cost is **₹90,000**. A new machine costs **₹1,60,000**, has a useful life of **5 years**, negligible salvage value, and an annual operating cost of **₹55,000**.
>
> **(a)** Apply the Incremental Principle to decide whether the old machine should be replaced. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Distinguish between the **Incremental Principle** and the **Marginal Principle** in managerial decision-making. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify and Exclude Sunk Cost.**

The book value of the old machine (₹40,000) is a **sunk cost** and must be **ignored**. The only relevant *recoverable* value is its market sale value of ₹25,000. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Status Quo (Keep Old Machine).**

Annual operating cost, $TC_0 = \text{₹90,000}$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 — Proposed Alternative (Buy New Machine).**

Net incremental investment = $1{,}60{,}000 - 25{,}000$ = **₹1,35,000**. **(1 Mark)**

Annualised capital charge over 5 years:

$$
\text{Annualised} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}35{,}000}{5} \;=\; \text{₹27,000 per year} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}
$$

Annual total cost of new machine, $TC_1$:

$$
TC_1 \;=\; \text{Operating cost} \;+\; \text{Annualised capital charge} \;=\; 55{,}000 \;+\; 27{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹82,000 per year} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}
$$

**Step 4 — Incremental Cost.**

$$
IC \;=\; TC_1 \;-\; TC_0 \;=\; 82{,}000 \;-\; 90{,}000 \;=\; -\text{₹8,000 per year} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}
$$

The negative sign indicates a **cost saving** of ₹8,000 per year by switching to the new machine.

**Step 5 — Decision.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Replace the old machine. Annual saving} = \text{₹8,000 (Total saving over 5 years} = \text{₹40,000).}} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)**

| Dimension | Incremental Principle | Marginal Principle | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit of analysis** | Compares **two distinct alternatives** (e.g., old vs. new, in-house vs. outsourced) | Analyses the effect of producing **one additional unit** | **2** |
| **Mathematical form** | $\Delta \pi = IR - IC$ for the *change in plan* | $MR = MC$ for the *next unit* | **2** |
| **Used for** | Special orders, make-or-buy, replacement, capacity expansion | Determining the profit-maximising level of output | **1** |
| **Time horizon** | Short-to-medium term; discrete decisions | Continuous, very short term | **1** |
| **Key difference** | "Compare Plan A with Plan B in totality" | "What happens if I produce just one more unit?" | **1** |

**Conclusion:** The Incremental Principle is best suited for **discrete, lumpy business decisions** (accept/reject), while the Marginal Principle is best suited for **continuous, fine-tuning decisions** (how much to produce). **(1 Mark — for concluding sentence)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Incremental Principle questions — KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Including sunk costs (book value, past R&D, past salaries):** This is the #1 reason for losing 3–4 marks. *Always* state explicitly which costs you are *excluding* and *why*.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute IR separately:** Many students compute new total profit and old total profit, then subtract — that works, but the *cleaner* KTU-rewarded method is to show $IR$ and $IC$ *individually* and then take the difference.
> 3. **Confusing Incremental with Marginal:** Examiners often place a "compare and contrast" 7-mark sub-question. Use the matrix above.
> 4. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly:** End every numerical with a clear sentence: *"Since $IR > IC$, the proposal is accepted."* Examiners award the final mark for this concluding line.
> 5. **Ignoring capacity assumptions:** When a special order question appears, always add a one-line assumption: *"The firm has sufficient idle capacity; the special order does not affect regular sales."*

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Core idea:** Compare **only the changes** — incremental cost vs. incremental revenue. Ignore the past.
- [x] **Decision rule:** Accept if $IR > IC$; Reject if $IR < IC$; Indifferent if $IR = IC$.
- [x] **Incremental Profit:** $\Delta \pi = IR - IC = (TR_1 - TR_0) - (TC_1 - TC_0)$.
- [x] **Average Incremental Cost (AIC):** $AIC = IC / \Delta Q$ — used to find the minimum acceptable price for a special order.
- [x] **Sunk Cost — Always Excluded:** Book value of old assets, past salaries, past R&D, non-refundable deposits. *Never* enter these into incremental analysis.
- [x] **Real-world uses:** Special orders, make-or-buy, equipment replacement, capacity expansion (second shift), new product launch, process automation.
- [x] **Incremental vs. Marginal:** Incremental = blocks of change; Marginal = one additional unit.
- [x] **Key Assumptions:** Constant technology, availability of capacity, no displacement of regular sales, costs and revenues are measurable in monetary terms.
- [x] **Always end with a one-line decision statement** in the exam.
- [x] **Visual aids earn bonus marks:** Redraw the Mermaid flowchart neatly in your answer booklet when explaining the principle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Managerial Economics**, the **Concept of Cost** refers to the monetary expenditure, measured in real terms, incurred by a firm in the process of producing a good or service. It represents the sacrifice of resources (inputs) made to achieve a specific business objective. From a decision-making perspective, cost is any forgone alternative, measured in monetary terms, that a manager must account for while allocating scarce resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (High-Yield)**
> *Cost is the value of the alternative foregone (opportunity cost) plus the actual monetary outlay (explicit cost) incurred in the production process.* It is the fundamental unit of measurement used by managers for pricing, output, and investment decisions.

Cost in managerial economics is broader than the accountant's cost because it incorporates **implicit costs** and **opportunity costs**, which do not appear in the firm's financial ledgers but are critical for rational economic decision-making.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you (a student) decide to open a small tea stall near your college campus.

- The money you pay for milk, sugar, tea powder, and the rent of the stall is your **Explicit Cost** (out-of-pocket).
- The salary you *could have earned* if you had taken a part-time job at a cafe (say ₹8,000/month) is your **Opportunity Cost**.
- The value of your own labor and the small shed your father gave you for free are your **Implicit Costs**.
- The ₹20,000 you spent last year on a special blender that cannot be resold is a **Sunk Cost** (irrelevant to current decisions).
- The extra cost of producing the *101st cup* of tea is your **Marginal Cost**.

**The total sacrifice to run the stall** = Explicit + Implicit + Opportunity costs. A managerial economist calls this the **Economic Cost**, while a traditional accountant only records the explicit portion (the **Accounting Cost**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the difference between **Accounting Cost** (historical, record-based) and **Economic Cost** (forward-looking, decision-based). Always justify decisions using Economic Cost, not Accounting Cost.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Cost (One-Line Definitions)

- **Explicit Cost**: Actual out-of-pocket monetary payments to outsiders (wages, rent, materials).
- **Implicit Cost**: Imputed value of self-owned resources and owner's time.
- **Opportunity Cost**: Return from the next best alternative foregone.
- **Accounting Cost**: Explicit cost recorded in books of accounts.
- **Economic Cost**: Accounting cost + Implicit cost (Total opportunity cost).
- **Sunk Cost**: Past, unrecoverable expenditure, irrelevant for current decisions.
- **Incremental Cost**: Additional cost of producing an extra batch or unit.
- **Fixed Cost (FC)**: Cost that does not vary with output in the short run.
- **Variable Cost (VC)**: Cost that varies directly with output.
- **Total Cost (TC)**: Sum of Fixed Cost and Variable Cost, $TC = FC + VC$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework (Why and How)

### A. Classification by Nature of Payment
1. **Explicit (Out-of-Pocket) Costs** — paid to external factors of production. *Why?* Directly verifiable, used in tax filings and P&L statements.
2. **Implicit Costs** — value of self-supplied factors. *How?* Calculated using market prices of similar resources (e.g., imputed rent of own building = market rent of equivalent space).
3. **Sunk Costs** — historical, irrecoverable. *Why important?* They should be **ignored** in marginal decision-making, as they remain the same regardless of the action chosen.

### B. Classification by Behavior with Output
- **Total Fixed Cost (TFC)**: Constant in the short run; e.g., rent, insurance, salaries of permanent staff.
- **Total Variable Cost (TVC)**: Increases with output; e.g., raw materials, hourly wages, electricity.
- **Total Cost (TC)**: $TC = TFC + TVC$
- **Average Fixed Cost (AFC)**: $AFC = \dfrac{TFC}{Q}$ — continuously falls as Q rises.
- **Average Variable Cost (AVC)**: $AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q}$ — typically U-shaped.
- **Average Cost (AC) / Average Total Cost (ATC)**: $AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = AFC + AVC$.
- **Marginal Cost (MC)**: Additional cost of producing one more unit.
$$MC_n = TC_n - TC_{n-1} = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$$

### C. Short-Run vs Long-Run Cost
- **Short Run**: At least one factor (usually capital) is fixed. TFC > 0.
- **Long Run**: All factors are variable. TFC = 0; hence AC = AVC.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Name** | **Units / Property** |
|------------|-------------|----------|----------------------|
| $TC$ | $TFC + TVC$ | Total Cost | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $AC$ | $TC / Q$ | Average Cost | ₹ per unit |
| $MC$ | $\Delta TC / \Delta Q$ | Marginal Cost | ₹ per unit |
| $AFC$ | $TFC / Q$ | Average Fixed Cost | Falls as Q rises |
| $AVC$ | $TVC / Q$ | Average Variable Cost | U-shaped |
| $TVC$ | $\int MC \, dQ$ | Total Variable Cost | Area under MC curve |
| $TFC$ | Constant (Short Run) | Total Fixed Cost | Horizontal line |
| $LAC$ | Envelope of $SAC$ | Long-Run Average Cost | U-shaped, flatter |
| $LMC$ | $dLTC / dQ$ | Long-Run Marginal Cost | U-shaped |
| $AC$ at minimum | $MC = AC$ | Optimal Output Point | ₹ per unit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Relationship (Examination Favorite):**
> The MC curve always intersects the AC curve and the AVC curve at their **minimum points**. When $MC < AC$, AC is falling. When $MC > AC$, AC is rising. When $MC = AC$, AC is at its minimum.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering economics and project management, the concept of cost directly drives:
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions** (using incremental vs sunk cost logic).
- **Break-Even Analysis** (separation of fixed and variable components).
- **Depreciation & Replacement Decisions** (sunk cost principle).
- **Capacity Planning** (long-run average cost minimization).
- **Pricing of Industrial Output** (MC-based marginal cost pricing in public utilities).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship between Total Cost, Marginal Cost, and Average Cost

**Given:**
$$TC = TFC + TVC$$

**Step 1:** Take the first derivative of TC with respect to Q to obtain MC.

$$MC = \dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{d(TVC)}{dQ}$$

*Explanation:* TFC is constant, so its derivative is zero. Hence, the change in total cost comes entirely from variable cost.

**Step 2:** Express AC as a function of Q.

$$AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = \dfrac{TFC + TVC}{Q}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate AC with respect to Q to find the slope of the AC curve.

$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} - TC \cdot 1}{Q^2} = \dfrac{Q \cdot MC - TC}{Q^2}$$

**Step 4:** Set the derivative equal to zero to find the minimum of AC.

$$\dfrac{Q \cdot MC - TC}{Q^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \cdot MC = TC \quad \Rightarrow \quad MC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = AC$$

*Conclusion:* The AC curve reaches its minimum exactly when **MC = AC**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Falling and Rising AC

**Case A — AC is falling:**

$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \cdot MC - TC < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad MC < AC$$

**Case B — AC is rising:**

$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \cdot MC - TC > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad MC > AC$$

*Conclusion:* The MC curve cuts the AC curve from below at its lowest point. The same logic applies to the AVC curve.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derivation of the U-Shape of the AVC

Assume a simple cubic short-run variable cost function:

$$TVC = aQ - bQ^2 + cQ^3$$

where $a, b, c > 0$.

**Step 1:** Compute MC.

$$MC = \dfrac{d(TVC)}{dQ} = a - 2bQ + 3cQ^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute AVC.

$$AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q} = a - bQ + cQ^2$$

**Step 3:** Find the minimum of AVC.

$$\dfrac{d(AVC)}{dQ} = -b + 2cQ = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^* = \dfrac{b}{2c}$$

**Step 4:** Verify it is a minimum.

$$\dfrac{d^2(AVC)}{dQ^2} = 2c > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $Q^* = b/2c$ is a minimum. The AVC curve is, therefore, **U-shaped**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Short-Run Cost Computation

Consider a firm with the following schedule. Derive the complete cost table.

| Output $Q$ | TFC | TVC | TC | AFC | AVC | AC | MC |
|------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 0 | 100 | 0 | 100 | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | 100 | 40 | 140 | 100.00 | 40.00 | 140.00 | 40 |
| 2 | 100 | 70 | 170 | 50.00 | 35.00 | 85.00 | 30 |
| 3 | 100 | 90 | 190 | 33.33 | 30.00 | 63.33 | 20 |
| 4 | 100 | 120 | 220 | 25.00 | 30.00 | 55.00 | 30 |
| 5 | 100 | 160 | 260 | 20.00 | 32.00 | 52.00 | 40 |
| 6 | 100 | 210 | 310 | 16.67 | 35.00 | 51.67 | 50 |

**Observations from the matrix:**
- AFC keeps falling continuously.
- AVC and AC are U-shaped, reaching minima at Q = 3 and Q = 6 respectively.
- MC is rising and lies below AC at Q = 3 (where AVC is minimum) and equals AC at Q = 6.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COST] --> B[By Nature of Payment]
    A --> C[By Behavior with Output]
    A --> D[By Decision Relevance]
    A --> E[By Time Horizon]

    B --> B1[Explicit Cost]
    B --> B2[Implicit Cost]
    B --> B3[Opportunity Cost]

    C --> C1[Fixed Cost TFC]
    C --> C2[Variable Cost TVC]
    C1 --> C1a[Rent Insurance Salaries]
    C2 --> C2a[Raw Materials Wages Power]

    D --> D1[Sunk Cost Ignore]
    D --> D2[Incremental Cost Use]
    D --> D3[Marginal Cost Use]

    E --> E1[Short Run TFC greater than zero]
    E --> E2[Long Run All factors variable]

    B1 --> F1[Accounting Cost]
    B2 --> F2[Economic Cost]
    F1 --> G[Total Economic Cost equals AC plus Implicit]
    F2 --> G
```

## 4.2 Short-Run Cost Curve Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SR[SHORT RUN COST STRUCTURE]
        TFC[Total Fixed Cost TFC Horizontal Line]
        TVC[Total Variable Cost TVC Increasing Curve]
        TC[Total Cost TC equals TFC plus TVC]
        AFC[Average Fixed Cost AFC Hyperbola Falling]
        AVC[Average Variable Cost AVC U Shape]
        AC[Average Cost AC equals AFC plus AVC U Shape]
        MC[Marginal Cost MC U Shape Steeper]
    end

    TVC --> TC
    TFC --> TC
    TC --> AC
    TFC --> AFC
    TVC --> AVC
    TC --> AC
    MC --> AC
    MC --> AVC

    M1[MC intersects AVC at its minimum]
    M2[MC intersects AC at its minimum]
    M1 --> AVC
    M2 --> AC
```

## 4.3 Long-Run vs Short-run Envelope Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LR[Long Run Average Cost LAC] --> EN[Envelope of all Short Run AC curves SAC1, SAC2, SAC3]
    EN --> P1[Each SAC is tangent to LAC]
    EN --> P2[LAC is flatter than SAC]
    EN --> P3[Minimum of LAC corresponds to optimal plant size]
    P1 --> Q[Engineering Takeaway Capacity Planning Tool]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Accounting Cost and Economic Cost with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Accounting Cost (1 mark):** The actual monetary expenditure recorded in the books of accounts. It is historical, verifiable, and consists only of **explicit costs**. Example: Wages paid to workers, rent paid to landlord, raw material purchases.
- **Economic Cost (1 mark):** The sum of accounting cost plus **implicit costs** (value of owner's resources and opportunity cost). Example: Rent of own building, salary the owner could have earned elsewhere.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Accounting cost is used for financial reporting and tax purposes; Economic cost is used for managerial decision-making as it reflects the true sacrifice of resources.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of Sunk Cost. Why is it considered irrelevant in decision-making?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Sunk cost is a past, irrecoverable expenditure that has already been incurred and cannot be recovered regardless of any future course of action. Example: A non-refundable deposit of ₹50,000 paid for a cancelled project.
- **Reason for Irrelevance — Part A (1 mark):** Sunk costs cannot be altered by current or future decisions; they remain constant irrespective of whether the project is continued or abandoned.
- **Reason for Irrelevance — Part B (1 mark):** Rational decision-making should be based on **incremental (future) costs and benefits**, not on past irrecoverable expenses. Including sunk costs leads to the *Sunk Cost Fallacy* and suboptimal choices.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the various concepts of cost used in managerial economics. (7 marks)
**(b)** The total cost function of a firm is given by $TC = 100 + 50Q - 10Q^2 + Q^3$. Find the output at which AC is minimum and the minimum AC. Also verify the relationship between MC and AC. (7 marks)

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks
1. **Explicit and Implicit Cost (2 marks):** Explicit costs are out-of-pocket payments; implicit costs are imputed values of self-owned factors.
2. **Opportunity Cost (1 mark):** The return from the next best alternative foregone. Foundation of economic cost.
3. **Fixed and Variable Cost (1 mark):** FC remains constant; VC varies with output.
4. **Total, Average, and Marginal Cost (2 marks):** TC = FC + VC; AC = TC/Q; MC = ΔTC/ΔQ.
5. **Sunk and Incremental Cost (1 mark):** Sunk is past and irrelevant; incremental is additional and relevant for decisions.

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks
**Step 1:** Express AC.

$$AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = \dfrac{100}{Q} + 50 - 10Q + Q^2$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate AC with respect to Q and set to zero.

$$\dfrac{d(AC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{100}{Q^2} - 10 + 2Q = 0$$

$$2Q^3 - 10Q^2 - 100 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^3 - 5Q^2 - 50 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Test integer values. For $Q = 5$:

$$125 - 125 - 50 = -50 \quad (\text{not zero})$$

For $Q = 6$:

$$216 - 180 - 50 = -14 \quad (\text{not zero})$$

For $Q = 7$:

$$343 - 245 - 50 = 48 \quad (\text{not zero})$$

Re-check the algebra. Multiply the original equation $2Q^3 - 10Q^2 - 100 = 0$ by 1/2:

$$Q^3 - 5Q^2 - 50 = 0$$

Try $Q = 5.6$:

$$5.6^3 = 175.616; \quad 5 \times 5.6^2 = 156.8; \quad 175.616 - 156.8 - 50 = -31.184$$

Try $Q = 6.5$:

$$6.5^3 = 274.625; \quad 5 \times 6.5^2 = 211.25; \quad 274.625 - 211.25 - 50 = 13.375$$

Linear interpolation between $Q = 6$ and $Q = 6.5$:

$$Q^* \approx 6 + 0.5 \times \dfrac{14}{14 + 13.375} \approx 6.26$$

**Step 4:** Compute MC and AC at the optimal point to verify the relationship.

$$MC = 50 - 20Q + 3Q^2$$

At $Q = 6.26$:

$$MC = 50 - 20(6.26) + 3(6.26)^2 = 50 - 125.2 + 117.58 = 42.38$$

$$AC = \dfrac{100}{6.26} + 50 - 10(6.26) + (6.26)^2 = 15.97 + 50 - 62.6 + 39.19 = 42.56$$

**Step 5:** Verification.

$$MC \approx AC \approx 42.5$$

The small difference is due to approximation. **Verified: At the minimum of AC, MC = AC.** (1 mark)

**Minimum AC = ₹42.56 per unit (approx).** (1 mark)

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Differentiate between fixed cost, variable cost, total cost, average cost, and marginal cost. Draw a neatly labeled diagram of the short-run cost curves. (7 marks)
**(b)** A firm has the following cost data. Complete the cost table and find the level of output where AC is minimum. (7 marks)

| Output Q | TFC | TVC |
|----------|-----|-----|
| 1 | 60 | 30 |
| 2 | 60 | 50 |
| 3 | 60 | 60 |
| 4 | 60 | 75 |
| 5 | 60 | 100 |

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks
1. **Definitions (4 marks):**
   - **FC:** Cost independent of output; e.g., rent.
   - **VC:** Cost varying with output; e.g., raw materials.
   - **TC:** $TC = FC + VC$.
   - **AC:** $AC = TC / Q$.
   - **MC:** $\Delta TC / \Delta Q$.
2. **Diagram Description (2 marks):** MC and AC are U-shaped. AFC is a rectangular hyperbola falling continuously. AC = AFC + AVC. MC intersects AC and AVC at their minimum points.
3. **One-line takeaway (1 mark):** MC is the prime mover of AC; whenever MC < AC, AC falls, and vice versa.

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks
**Step 1:** Compute TC, AFC, AVC, AC, and MC for each output level.

| Q | TFC | TVC | TC | AFC | AVC | AC | MC |
|---|-----|-----|----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 1 | 60 | 30 | 90 | 60.00 | 30.00 | 90.00 | 30 |
| 2 | 60 | 50 | 110 | 30.00 | 25.00 | 55.00 | 20 |
| 3 | 60 | 60 | 120 | 20.00 | 20.00 | 40.00 | 10 |
| 4 | 60 | 75 | 135 | 15.00 | 18.75 | 33.75 | 15 |
| 5 | 60 | 100 | 160 | 12.00 | 20.00 | 32.00 | 25 |

**Step 2:** Identify the level of output where AC is minimum.

From the table, AC values are: 90, 55, 40, 33.75, 32.

**AC is minimum at Q = 5, where AC = ₹32 per unit.** (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Verify using the MC = AC principle.

- At Q = 4: MC = 15, AC = 33.75. Since MC < AC, AC is still falling.
- At Q = 5: MC = 25, AC = 32. Since MC < AC (closer now), AC is still falling marginally.

The minimum AC is achieved at the output level where MC is about to cross AC. Given the discrete data, the **minimum AC in the table is ₹32 at Q = 5**. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **Total Cost** with **Total Variable Cost**. Always state $TC = TFC + TVC$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Computing MC incorrectly as $TC/Q$ instead of $\Delta TC / \Delta Q$. MC is the *change* in TC per additional unit.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $AC$ minimum where $AC$ is **maximum**. Always re-check by reading the table values in sequence.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to verify that at the minimum AC, **MC = AC**. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for this verification step.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Using the wrong units in the final answer. Always specify ₹ per unit for AC and MC.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cost** = Monetary sacrifice for production; broader than accounting cost.
- **Accounting Cost** = Explicit cost only (record-based).
- **Economic Cost** = Accounting Cost + Implicit Cost (decision-based).
- **Opportunity Cost** = Return from the next best alternative foregone; the foundation of economic cost.
- **Sunk Cost** = Past, unrecoverable; **always ignore** in decisions.
- **Incremental Cost** = Additional cost of a batch; use it for batch decisions.
- **TC = TFC + TVC**; **AC = TC/Q = AFC + AVC**; **MC = ΔTC/ΔQ**.
- AFC always falls as Q rises (hyperbolic curve).
- AVC and AC are U-shaped.
- **MC cuts AC and AVC at their minimum points.**
- When $MC < AC$, AC is falling; when $MC > AC$, AC is rising.
- In the **Long Run**, all costs are variable, so TFC = 0 and LAC is the envelope of SAC curves.
- Always verify the $MC = AC$ condition at the optimal output for full marks.
- Use **marginal cost** for short-run output decisions and **average cost** for long-run survival analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discounting Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discounting Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discounting Principle** is the financial technique of converting a future cash flow (or a series of future cash flows) into its equivalent value **today**, by applying a rate of return (called the *discount rate*) that reflects the **Time Value of Money (TVM)**.

Mathematically, the principle is expressed as:

$$PV = \frac{FV_n}{(1 + r)^n}$$

Where:
- $PV$ = Present Value (value in *Year 0*, i.e., today)
- $FV_n$ = Future Value receivable in *n* years
- $r$ = Discount (or required) rate of return per period, expressed as a decimal
- $n$ = Number of periods (usually in years)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU managerial economics, the *discounting principle* is the analytical backbone of **capital budgeting** decisions — firms use it to evaluate whether long-term projects (plant installation, R&D, machinery) generate value above the firm's cost of capital.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza, The Friend, and The Bank"

Imagine your friend promises you **₹1,000 one year from now**. Would you happily wait?

- **Reason 1 (Time Preference):** You'd rather have *something* today — say, a pizza — than wait. So ₹1,000 *next year* feels psychologically smaller than ₹1,000 *right now*.
- **Reason 2 (Inflation):** Next year's ₹1,000 may buy only what ₹940 buys today because prices rise.
- **Reason 3 (Opportunity Cost):** If you had the money today, you could deposit it in a bank earning, say, **8% interest**, and grow it to ₹1,080 in a year. So you'd need compensation for the *lost* interest.

Therefore, the *true* value of ₹1,000 receivable after one year is **less than ₹1,000 today**. *Discounting* is the math that quantifies *how much less*.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Managerial Economics:** *"A rupee in hand today is worth more than a rupee promised tomorrow."* — This is the soul of the Discounting Principle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value Used in KTU Problems |
|---|---|
| **Symbol for Money** | $PV$, $FV$, $CF$ |
| **Standard Discount Rate Ranges** | **5% to 20%** for corporate projects |
| **Risk-free benchmark (India)** | 10-Year G-Sec yield ≈ **7.0% – 7.5%** |
| **Project life assumption** | Usually **3 to 10 years** in numericals |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars Behind the Discounting Principle

A KTU examiner expects you to know *why* we discount — not just *how*. There are three economic reasons:

1. **Productivity of Capital (Investment Opportunity):**
   Money invested today produces more money tomorrow. Hence, future money is "diluted" in productive terms.

2. **Time Preference for Consumption:**
   Human beings are *impatient*. We value present satisfaction over future satisfaction. This is captured by **Fisher's Time Preference Theory**.

3. **Risk and Uncertainty:**
   The further into the future a cash flow lies, the higher the probability that something may go wrong (default, recession, policy change). A rational investor demands compensation for this **uncertainty premium**.

### 2.2 The Logical Flow of Discounting (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

Let $C_0$ = cash flow today, $C_1$ = cash flow at end of year 1, $C_2$ = cash flow at end of year 2, and so on.

- **Step 1:** Identify the cash flow that occurs at the *end* of each period.
- **Step 2:** Determine the appropriate **discount rate $r$** — usually the firm's *cost of capital* (WACC) or *required rate of return*.
- **Step 3:** Apply the discount factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ to each future cash flow.
- **Step 4:** **Sum** all discounted values to obtain the **Present Value**.
- **Step 5:** Subtract the initial investment $C_0$ to get the **Net Present Value (NPV)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — 70% of Managerial Economics numericals in KTU are built from these five equations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single Cash Flow PV** | $PV = \dfrac{FV_n}{(1+r)^n}$ | Discount a *single* future lump sum |
| 2 | **Single Cash Flow FV** | $FV_n = PV \cdot (1+r)^n$ | Compound a present sum forward |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Sum of all discounted cash flows |
| 4 | **Ordinary Annuity PV** | $PV = C \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | $C$ = equal yearly cash flow; $n$ = years |
| 5 | **Perpetuity PV** | $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | Infinite stream of equal cash flows |
| 6 | **Discount Factor** | $DF_n = (1+r)^{-n}$ | Multiplier used to convert $FV$ to $PV$ |
| 7 | **Effective Annual Rate** | $EAR = (1 + r/m)^m - 1$ | When compounded $m$ times per year |
| 8 | **Doubling Time (Rule of 72)** | $t \approx \dfrac{72}{r\%}$ | Years to double the principal at rate $r$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notational Warning:** When writing $PV$ of an annuity, **never** confuse it with the *Present Value of a Lump Sum*. They are different formulas. The annuity formula uses $\left[1-(1+r)^{-n}\right]/r$, while the lump sum uses just $(1+r)^{-n}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- **Capital Budgeting in Manufacturing Firms:** Tata Steel, BHEL, and L&T use discounting to decide whether to invest ₹500 crores in a new plant.
- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** A factory manager must choose between repairing an old CNC machine (low cost now) versus buying a new one (high cost now, but lower operating cost over 10 years).
- **R&D Project Evaluation:** Pharmaceutical and tech firms discount the *expected* future cash flows from patents to decide on R&D spend.
- **Government Projects:** NHAI evaluates highway projects using discounted cash flow analysis over 20–30 year concession periods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1: Present Value of a Single Future Cash Flow

**Premise:** If ₹1 today grows to $(1+r)$ after one year at rate $r$, then ₹1 received after one year is worth *less* than ₹1 today.

- **Year 0 (today):** Principal = $PV$
- **Year 1 (end):** Principal grows to $PV \cdot (1+r)$
- So: $FV_1 = PV \cdot (1+r)$

Rearranging for $PV$:

$$PV = \frac{FV_1}{1+r}$$

- **Year 2:** $FV_2 = PV \cdot (1+r)^2$

$$\boxed{PV = \frac{FV_n}{(1+r)^n}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation #2: Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity

An **ordinary annuity** is a series of *equal* end-of-period cash flows $C$ for $n$ years.

The total PV is the sum of individually discounted cash flows:

$$PV = \frac{C}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^3} + \dots + \frac{C}{(1+r)^n}$$

This is a **geometric progression** with first term $a = C/(1+r)$ and common ratio $q = 1/(1+r)$.

Sum of geometric series: $S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - q^n}{1 - q}$

Substituting:

$$PV = \frac{C}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+r}\right)^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r}}$$

Simplifying the denominator $1 - \dfrac{1}{1+r} = \dfrac{r}{1+r}$:

$$PV = \frac{C}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{\dfrac{r}{1+r}}$$

$$\boxed{PV_{\text{annuity}} = C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Model Solved Problems (KTU Board Style)

---

#### **Model Problem 1 — Single Cash Flow PV (Warm-up)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]** A firm is promised ₹50,000 three years from now. If the discount rate is **10% per annum**, what is the present value?

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
$FV_3 = ₹50,000$, $r = 10\% = 0.10$, $n = 3$ years.

**Step 2 — Apply the discount formula:**

$$PV = \frac{50{,}000}{(1.10)^3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the power:**

$$(1.10)^3 = 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 = 1.331$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$PV = \frac{50{,}000}{1.331} = ₹37{,}565.74$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for valuation answer:** The firm should not pay more than **₹37,565.74 today** for an asset promising ₹50,000 in three years, assuming 10% is the required return.

---

#### **Model Problem 2 — NPV of a Multi-Year Project**

> A company is evaluating a project with the following cash flows. Initial Investment = **₹10,00,000**. Year 1 cash inflow = **₹3,00,000**, Year 2 = **₹4,00,000**, Year 3 = **₹5,00,000**, Year 4 = **₹4,00,000**. Discount rate = **12%**. Should the project be accepted?

**Step 1 — Tabulate the cash flows:**

| Year ($t$) | Cash Flow $CF_t$ (₹) | Discount Factor $(1.12)^{-t}$ | Discounted CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-10,00,000$ | $1.0000$ | $-10,00,000.00$ |
| 1 | $+3,00,000$ | $0.8929$ | $+2,67,857.14$ |
| 2 | $+4,00,000$ | $0.7972$ | $+3,18,877.55$ |
| 3 | $+5,00,000$ | $0.7118$ | $+3,55,889.45$ |
| 4 | $+4,00,000$ | $0.6355$ | $+2,54,206.75$ |

**Step 2 — Compute each discount factor** (shown to 4 decimal places):

$$(1.12)^{-1} = 0.8929$$
$$(1.12)^{-2} = 0.7972$$
$$(1.12)^{-3} = 0.7118$$
$$(1.12)^{-4} = 0.6355$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to obtain discounted cash flows:**

$$DF_1 \cdot CF_1 = 0.8929 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}67{,}857.14$$
$$DF_2 \cdot CF_2 = 0.7972 \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}18{,}877.55$$
$$DF_3 \cdot CF_3 = 0.7118 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}55{,}889.45$$
$$DF_4 \cdot CF_4 = 0.6355 \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}54{,}206.75$$

**Step 4 — Sum to compute NPV:**

$$NPV = -10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}67{,}857.14 + 3{,}18{,}877.55 + 3{,}55{,}889.45 + 2{,}54{,}206.75$$

$$\boxed{NPV = +₹1{,}96{,}830.89}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (Memorize for KTU):** If $NPV > 0$ → **Accept** the project. If $NPV < 0$ → **Reject**. If $NPV = 0$ → Firm is **indifferent**.
>
> **Conclusion:** Since $NPV = +₹1,96,830.89 > 0$, **the project is financially viable** and should be accepted.

---

#### **Model Problem 3 — Annuity Application (Loan EMI Equivalent)**

> A bond pays **₹50,000 per year for 5 years**. Discount rate is **8%**. Find the present value of these payments.

**Step 1 — Apply annuity formula directly:**

$$PV = 50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-5}}{0.08}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the discount factor on ₹1 in year 5:**

$$(1.08)^5 = 1.4693$$
$$(1.08)^{-5} = 0.6806$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$PV = 50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.6806}{0.08}$$
$$PV = 50{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.3194}{0.08}$$
$$PV = 50{,}000 \cdot 3.9927$$

$$\boxed{PV = ₹1{,}99{,}636.50}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world link:** This is exactly how banks price **personal loan EMIs** and how companies price **bonds** — by discounting a known stream of future coupon payments back to the present.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart: The Discounting Decision Process

> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end decision flow** that a finance manager follows when applying the Discounting Principle. The node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels are kept raw for safe rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["STEP 1: Identify Future Cash Flows C1 to Cn"] --> node2{"STEP 2: Is the Cash Flow Series Equal Each Year?"}
    node2 -- Yes --> node3["Use Annuity Formula: PV = C times bracket 1 minus 1 plus r to power n divided by r"]
    node2 -- No --> node4["Use General NPV Formula: NPV = sum CFt divided by 1 plus r to power t"]
    node3 --> node5["STEP 3: Determine Discount Rate r from Cost of Capital"]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6["STEP 4: Compute Present Value of Each Cash Flow"]
    node6 --> node7["STEP 5: Sum Discounted Values"]
    node7 --> node8{"STEP 6: Compare NPV to Zero"}
    node8 -- NPV greater than 0 --> node9["Decision: ACCEPT the Project"]
    node8 -- NPV less than 0 --> node10["Decision: REJECT the Project"]
    node8 -- NPV equals 0 --> node11["Decision: INDIFFERENT Break Even Point"]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Components of the Discount Rate

> This diagram explains *how* the discount rate $r$ is constructed in real corporate settings — useful for 3-mark and 7-mark KTU theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rateA["Base Rate: Risk Free Government Security Yield"] --> rateB["Add Inflation Premium"]
    rateB --> rateC["Add Default Risk Premium"]
    rateC --> rateD["Add Liquidity Premium"]
    rateD --> rateE["Add Maturity Risk Premium"]
    rateE --> rateF["Final Discount Rate r equals Cost of Capital WACC"]
    rateF --> rateG["Used to Discount Future Cash Flows to Present Value"]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Compounding vs. Discounting

| Aspect | Compounding | Discounting |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Present → Future | Future → Present |
| **Mathematical Operation** | Multiply by $(1+r)^n$ | Divide by $(1+r)^n$ |
| **Used For** | Computing future savings growth | Evaluating investment proposals |
| **KTU Question Type** | 3-mark direct | 7/14-mark problem |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define the Discounting Principle. State any two reasons why a rupee received in the future is worth less than a rupee received today.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1½ marks per part):**

The **Discounting Principle** is the method of determining the present value of a future cash flow by applying a discount rate that reflects the time value of money. The formula is:

$$PV = \frac{FV_n}{(1+r)^n}$$

**Two reasons a future rupee is worth less:**

1. **Time Preference for Consumption:** Individuals prefer present consumption over future consumption due to psychological impatience — hence a future rupee is valued less.

2. **Inflation:** The general price level rises over time, eroding the purchasing power of money. ₹100 next year buys fewer goods than ₹100 today.

*(Acceptable 3rd reason: Opportunity cost of capital — money tied up in a future promise could have earned interest if received today.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Distinguish between Present Value (PV) and Net Present Value (NPV). Why is NPV considered superior to simple Payback Period in capital budgeting?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Present Value (PV) | Net Present Value (NPV) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Today's equivalent of a single future sum | Sum of PVs of *all* cash flows minus initial investment |
| Formula | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ |
| Output | Single rupee figure | Single rupee figure (signed) |
| Use Case | Pricing of bonds/loans | Project acceptance decisions |

**Why NPV is superior to Payback Period (1 mark):**
Payback ignores the **time value of money** and disregards cash flows occurring *after* the payback period. NPV discounts all cash flows, considers the project's entire life, and gives a direct rupee measure of value creation — making it the theoretically correct method per KTU syllabus.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** A manufacturing firm is evaluating a new assembly line. The project requires an initial investment of **₹25,00,000** and is expected to generate cash inflows of **₹8,00,000**, **₹10,00,000**, **₹9,00,000**, and **₹7,00,000** at the end of years 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively. The firm's cost of capital is **11%**.
> Calculate the NPV of the project. Should the project be accepted? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is the concept of *Time Value of Money*? Explain the role of the discount rate in determining present value. Briefly describe any three methods of evaluating capital budgeting decisions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Tabulate cash flows and discount factors:**

| Year $t$ | $CF_t$ (₹) | $(1.11)^{-t}$ | Discounted CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-25,00,000$ | $1.0000$ | $-25,00,000$ |
| 1 | $+8,00,000$ | $0.9009$ | $+7,20,720$ |
| 2 | $+10,00,000$ | $0.8116$ | $+8,11,620$ |
| 3 | $+9,00,000$ | $0.7312$ | $+6,58,080$ |
| 4 | $+7,00,000$ | $0.6587$ | $+4,61,090$ |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Show discount factor calculations:**

$$(1.11)^{-1} = 0.9009$$
$$(1.11)^{-2} = 0.8116$$
$$(1.11)^{-3} = 0.7312$$
$$(1.11)^{-4} = 0.6587$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Multiply CF by DF:**

$$0.9009 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}20{,}720$$
$$0.8116 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}11{,}620$$
$$0.7312 \times 9{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}58{,}080$$
$$0.6587 \times 7{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}61{,}090$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Sum to get NPV and conclude:**

$$NPV = -25{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}20{,}720 + 8{,}11{,}620 + 6{,}58{,}080 + 4{,}61{,}090$$

$$\boxed{NPV = +₹1{,}51{,}510}$$

**Decision (1 mark):** Since $NPV = +₹1,51,510 > 0$, the project **SHOULD BE ACCEPTED** as it adds value to the firm.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks**

**Time Value of Money (2 marks):**
The Time Value of Money (TVM) is the economic principle that *a rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee in the future* because of the earning potential of money and the negative effects of inflation and uncertainty. It is the conceptual foundation of the discounting principle.

**Role of the Discount Rate (2 marks):**
The discount rate $r$ is the *opportunity cost* of capital — the minimum return a firm must earn to satisfy its investors. A **higher discount rate** reduces the present value of future cash flows (making long-term projects less attractive); a **lower discount rate** increases PV (encouraging long-gestation projects). It is used as the denominator in $(1+r)^n$ to convert future rupees into today's value.

**Three Capital Budgeting Methods (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Net Present Value (NPV):** Discounts all future cash flows to the present and subtracts initial investment. Accept if NPV > 0.
2. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** The discount rate at which NPV = 0. Accept if IRR > cost of capital.
3. **Payback Period Method:** Time required to recover the initial investment from project cash flows. Accept if payback < target period.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **(a)** A machine costing **₹6,00,000** is expected to generate a uniform annual cash inflow of **₹2,00,000** for **5 years**. The discount rate is **10%**.
> Calculate the present value of cash inflows using the annuity formula. Advise whether the machine should be purchased. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Perpetuity**. A charitable trust wishes to fund a scholarship of **₹1,50,000 per year forever**. If the fund earns **9% per annum**, what amount must be deposited today? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify variables:**
$C = ₹2,00,000$, $n = 5$, $r = 10\% = 0.10$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute the annuity factor:**

$$AF = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Evaluate $(1.10)^5$:**

$$(1.10)^5 = 1.6105$$
$$(1.10)^{-5} = 0.6209$$

$$AF = \frac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} = \frac{0.3791}{0.10} = 3.7908$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Multiply to get PV of inflows:**

$$PV_{\text{inflows}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908 = ₹7{,}58{,}160$$

**Step 5 (1 mark — Decision):**

$$NPV = 7{,}58{,}160 - 6{,}00{,}000 = +₹1{,}58{,}160 > 0$$

**Conclusion:** The machine **SHOULD BE PURCHASED** as it yields a positive NPV.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks**

**Perpetuity Concept (3 marks):**
A **perpetuity** is a financial instrument that pays a fixed cash flow $C$ at the end of every period *forever* (i.e., $n \to \infty$). Its present value formula is derived by taking the limit of the annuity formula as $n \to \infty$:

$$PV_{\text{perpetuity}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

As $n \to \infty$, $(1+r)^{-n} \to 0$, so:

$$\boxed{PV_{\text{perpetuity}} = \frac{C}{r}}$$

**Numerical Application (4 marks):**

Given: $C = ₹1,50,000$ per year, $r = 9\% = 0.09$.

$$PV = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{0.09} = ₹16{,}66{,}666.67$$

**Conclusion:** The trust must deposit **₹16,66,666.67** today to perpetually fund the ₹1,50,000 annual scholarship.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $n$ with cash flow year:** The discount factor $(1+r)^{-t}$ uses the *period index*, not the year number directly. If a cash flow occurs at the *beginning* of year 2, it should be discounted as period 1, not 2.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the initial investment:** Many students compute the *sum* of discounted cash flows but forget to subtract $C_0$. That gives them PV of inflows, not NPV — both will be marked, but NPV is what answers "accept/reject."
>
> 3. **Using percentage as a decimal:** Always convert **10% → 0.10**. Writing $r = 10$ in the formula is a guaranteed zero in the calculation step.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Annuity vs. Perpetuity:** Annuity is for *finite* periods; Perpetuity is for *infinite* periods. The perpetuity formula is *simpler* ($C/r$), but the derivation is different.
>
> 5. **Annual vs. Mid-year convention:** KTU problems usually assume *end-of-year* cash flows. If the problem says "beginning of the year," multiply the annuity PV by $(1+r)$ to get the annuity-due value.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before the KTU exam — go through this list twice.**

- **Core Idea:** Future money is worth less than present money — discount it!
- **Master Formula:** $PV = \dfrac{FV_n}{(1+r)^n}$ — the *single most important equation* in this module.
- **Compounding = $(1+r)^n$**; **Discounting = $(1+r)^{-n}$** — they are mirror operations.
- **NPV Decision Rule:** $NPV > 0$ → Accept; $NPV < 0$ → Reject; $NPV = 0$ → Indifferent.
- **Annuity PV:** Use when cash flows are *equal* every year for a *finite* number of years.
- **Perpetuity PV:** $PV = C/r$ — used for *infinite* cash flows (scholarships, preference shares).
- **Discount Rate:** Usually the firm's *cost of capital* or *required rate of return*. Higher $r$ → lower PV.
- **Rule of 72:** To find the doubling time, divide 72 by the interest rate (in %).
- **Always convert percentages to decimals** before plugging into the formula.
- **Show discount factor calculations explicitly** in KTU answer sheets — examiners award step marks.
- **Round off to 2 decimal places** in the final answer unless otherwise specified.
- **Real-world link:** EMI pricing, bond valuation, project feasibility, IPO pricing — all depend on the discounting principle.
- **Common KTU traps:** Mixing annuity vs. perpetuity, forgetting $C_0$, wrong decimal conversion, using future value formula when present value is asked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Time Concept in Managerial Economics:**
> The **Time Concept** refers to the classification and analysis of economic decisions, production adjustments, and market equilibria across different **temporal horizons**. In managerial economics, time is treated as a critical dimension that determines the degree of flexibility a firm possesses in adjusting its **inputs, output levels, and production capacity** in response to changes in market demand or cost conditions.

According to **Alfred Marshall** (the father of modern managerial economics), the time horizon fundamentally governs the behavior of supply, the mobility of factors of production, and the ability of a firm to enter or exit a market. Time is therefore not merely a chronological measure but a **strategic economic resource** that shapes managerial decision-making.

## 1.2 Classification of Time Periods (Marshallian Framework)

Marshall classified economic time into **four distinct periods**, each differing in the degree of input adjustability:

| Period | Adjustability of Supply | Output Level | Firm's Strategic Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Period (Very Short Run)** | Completely fixed (Zero) | Cannot be changed | Maximum pricing power (Pure price setter) |
| **Short Run** | Fixed and Variable inputs exist | Limited adjustment possible | Moderate — can vary output by changing variable inputs |
| **Long Run** | All inputs are variable | Full capacity adjustment possible | Full strategic flexibility |
| **Very Long Run (Secular Period)** | Technology, population, and capital stock all change | Structural transformation | Industry-wide evolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Syllabus Highlight:**
> The 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the distinction between **short run** and **long run** in the context of **production functions, cost curves, and demand-supply dynamics**. Students must be able to identify which time horizon applies to a given managerial scenario.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **restaurant owner** responding to a sudden surge in dinner demand:

- **Market Period (Very Short Run):** The kitchen is already running, ingredients are pre-purchased, and the chef cannot cook faster than physics allows. The owner can only **raise prices** — supply is frozen. This is a *price taker's nightmare* turned into a *price setter's paradise*.

- **Short Run:** The owner can **hire more cooks** (variable input) and buy more ingredients, but the **building size, ovens, and seating capacity** remain fixed (fixed inputs). Output can be increased, but only within physical constraints.

- **Long Run:** The owner can **renovate the kitchen, install larger ovens, expand seating, and even open a second branch** (all inputs become variable). Supply fully adjusts to demand.

- **Very Long Run:** Dining trends change — perhaps **cloud kitchens** or **AI-driven restaurants** emerge. The entire industry restructures itself.

This analogy vividly captures why **time dictates managerial flexibility** — a core insight of the Time Concept.

## 1.4 Why the Time Concept Matters in Managerial Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** *The same economic problem — say, a 20% demand increase — will lead to radically different managerial responses depending on the time horizon under consideration.*

For instance, a 20% demand surge in the **market period** triggers **price rationing**, in the **short run** triggers **output expansion using existing capacity**, in the **long run** triggers **capacity expansion**, and in the **very long run** triggers **technological innovation or industry exit**.

This cascading logic is what makes the Time Concept foundational to all subsequent modules in PECBT525, including demand analysis, production theory, cost analysis, and pricing strategies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Time Periods — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Market Period (Very Short Run)

- **Definition:** A period so brief that **no output adjustment is possible**. All factors of production are completely fixed.
- **Supply Elasticity:** Supply is **perfectly inelastic** with respect to price. The supply curve is a **vertical line** parallel to the Y-axis.
- **Managerial Implication:** The firm has no recourse but to **adjust price** to clear whatever stock is available. This is the domain of **auction pricing, distress sales, and perishables** (e.g., fish market at the dock, airline seats minutes before departure).
- **Graphical Characteristic:**

$$
\frac{dQ_s}{dP} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_s = 0
$$

where $E_s$ denotes the price elasticity of supply.

### 2.1.2 Short Run

- **Definition:** A period long enough for the firm to **vary its output by changing variable inputs** (like labor and raw materials) but **not long enough to change fixed inputs** (like plant size, machinery, or land).
- **Supply Elasticity:** Supply is **somewhat elastic**. The supply curve slopes **upward** from left to right.
- **Managerial Implication:** Firms can respond to demand changes through **overtime work, additional shifts, increased raw material procurement**, and **inventory adjustments**.
- **Key Constraint:** At least one input remains fixed, leading to the **Law of Diminishing Returns** in production.

### 2.1.3 Long Run

- **Definition:** A period sufficient for the firm to **vary all inputs**, including plant size, equipment, and organizational structure.
- **Supply Elasticity:** Supply becomes **more elastic** or even **perfectly elastic** in competitive markets. The supply curve approaches a **horizontal line**.
- **Managerial Implication:** Firms can achieve **economies of scale, optimal plant size**, and **full cost minimization**. New firms can **enter the industry**; existing firms can **exit**.
- **Key Characteristic:** The **Law of Returns to Scale** (increasing, constant, or decreasing) governs production behavior.

### 2.1.4 Very Long Run (Secular Period)

- **Definition:** A period long enough for **technology, population, capital stock, and institutional frameworks** to undergo fundamental change.
- **Managerial Implication:** Firms must engage in **strategic foresight, R&D investment, innovation management**, and **adaptation to structural change**. Examples include the shift from typewriter to computer industries, or from fossil fuels to renewable energy.
- **Key Concept:** **Creative destruction** (Schumpeter's terminology) — the engine of long-run economic evolution.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value and conditional notations use $\vert$ (LaTeX) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| S.No. | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Interpretation | Time Period |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supply Elasticity in Market Period | $E_s = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P} = 0$ | Supply is perfectly inelastic | Market Period |
| 2 | Supply Elasticity in Short Run | $E_s = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P} > 0$ and $< \infty$ | Supply is moderately elastic | Short Run |
| 3 | Supply Elasticity in Long Run | $E_s \to \infty$ (in perfect competition) | Supply is perfectly elastic | Long Run |
| 4 | Production Function (Short Run) | $Q = f(L, \bar{K})$ | Output depends on labor $L$; capital $K$ is fixed | Short Run |
| 5 | Production Function (Long Run) | $Q = f(L, K)$ | All factors are variable | Long Run |
| 6 | Total Cost (Short Run) | $TC = TFC + TVC$ | Fixed cost + Variable cost | Short Run |
| 7 | Total Cost (Long Run) | $TC = TVC$ only (no fixed cost) | All costs are variable | Long Run |
| 8 | Break-even Condition | $TR = TC$ | Total revenue equals total cost | Any period |
| 9 | Profit Maximization | $\pi = TR - TC$ (maximize) | Maximize the difference | Any period |
| 10 | Time Preference / Discount Factor | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | Present value of future cash flow | Cross-period |

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Time Period

### Why does supply elasticity increase with time?

- **Market Period:** No time to produce more goods → supply is **vertical**.
- **Short Run:** Firms can **intensify production** using existing capacity (more labor, more shifts) → supply becomes **upward sloping**.
- **Long Run:** New firms can **enter**, old firms can **expand plants**, and **new technology** can be adopted → supply becomes **more elastic**, approaching horizontal in perfectly competitive markets.
- **Very Long Run:** **Technological revolutions, demographic shifts, and institutional changes** fundamentally alter the production possibility frontier.

### How does this affect managerial decision-making?

- **Pricing Strategy:** A firm with perishable inventory in the market period will **discount heavily** rather than hold stock. In the long run, the same firm can plan **dynamic pricing** based on capacity utilization.
- **Investment Decisions:** Capital investment decisions (plant expansion) are **long-run** by nature and require **time value of money** analysis.
- **Inventory Management:** Inventory decisions bridge short-run fluctuations and long-run supply planning.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

- **Manufacturing Firms:** Engineers and managers use the Time Concept to decide **batch production vs. continuous production**, **make-or-buy decisions**, and **capacity planning**.
- **IT Industry:** Short-run decisions involve **server load balancing**; long-run decisions involve **data center construction**.
- **Construction:** Short-run = daily project management; long-run = architectural design and contract planning over years.
- **Automotive Industry:** Market period = showroom pricing; short run = overtime shifts; long run = new model launch; very long run = transition to electric vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Connection:** The Time Concept directly mirrors **engineering project life cycles** — conceptual design (very long run), detailed design (long run), construction (short run), and operation (market period). This interdisciplinary resonance makes it essential for KTU 2024 engineering graduates entering management roles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Supply Elasticity Across Time Periods

We derive how supply elasticity evolves as the time horizon expands, using a standard linear supply function.

### Step 1: Define the Linear Supply Function

We begin with a general linear supply relationship between quantity supplied $Q_s$ and price $P$:

$$
Q_s = a + bP
$$

**Explanation:** Here, $a$ is the autonomous (intercept) supply component and $b > 0$ is the slope coefficient representing how sensitively producers respond to price changes.

### Step 2: Compute the Price Elasticity of Supply

The price elasticity of supply $E_s$ is defined as the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price:

$$
E_s = \frac{dQ_s}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q_s}
$$

**Explanation:** This ratio captures the *responsiveness* of supply to price — a dimensionless measure that allows comparison across markets.

### Step 3: Differentiate the Supply Function

Taking the derivative of the supply function with respect to price:

$$
\frac{dQ_s}{dP} = b
$$

**Explanation:** The slope $b$ is constant for a linear supply curve, representing the marginal response of quantity to a unit change in price.

### Step 4: Substitute Into the Elasticity Formula

Plugging the derivative into the elasticity expression:

$$
E_s = b \cdot \frac{P}{Q_s} = \frac{bP}{a + bP}
$$

**Explanation:** This expression shows that elasticity is not constant along a linear supply curve — it increases as $P$ rises and approaches unity as $P \to \infty$.

### Step 5: Apply the Time-Period Constraint

**Market Period:** Output cannot be adjusted at all, so $b = 0$:

$$
E_s^{\text{market}} = \frac{0 \cdot P}{a + 0 \cdot P} = 0
$$

**Explanation:** Zero elasticity — a vertical supply curve. The firm sells whatever it has at whatever price the market allows.

**Short Run:** Some adjustment is possible, so $0 < b < \infty$:

$$
E_s^{\text{short}} = \frac{bP}{a + bP} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 < b < \infty
$$

**Explanation:** Moderate elasticity. The supply curve slopes upward, and producers can partially respond to price changes.

**Long Run:** Full adjustment is possible, and in perfect competition, the long-run supply curve is horizontal ($b \to \infty$):

$$
E_s^{\text{long}} = \lim_{b \to \infty} \frac{bP}{a + bP} = \lim_{b \to \infty} \frac{P}{\frac{a}{b} + P} = \frac{P}{0 + P} = 1
$$

**Explanation:** Elasticity approaches 1 in the long run for competitive markets, reflecting the full flexibility of supply.

**Very Long Run:** With technological change, the entire supply function shifts, and elasticity can exceed 1 as innovation amplifies response:

$$
E_s^{\text{secular}} > 1
$$

**Explanation:** Supply becomes hyper-elastic as technology, new entrants, and structural change drive production capabilities beyond historical norms.

### Step 6: Summarize the Elasticity Cascade

$$
E_s^{\text{market}} = 0 \;<\; E_s^{\text{short}} \;<\; E_s^{\text{long}} \;\leq\; E_s^{\text{secular}}
$$

**Explanation:** This monotonic cascade is the **central theorem of the Time Concept in Managerial Economics** — elasticity of supply is a strictly increasing function of the time horizon available for adjustment.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Time Periods Across Key Dimensions

| Dimension | Market Period | Short Run | Long Run | Very Long Run |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Duration (Typical)** | Hours to days | Weeks to 1 year | 1 to 10 years | Decades |
| **Fixed Inputs** | All inputs | At least one input | No fixed inputs | All inputs + technology change |
| **Variable Inputs** | None | Some | All | All + new technologies |
| **Supply Curve Shape** | Vertical | Upward sloping | Horizontal (PC) / gently sloping | Shifts outward (technological progress) |
| **Entry/Exit of Firms** | Impossible | Not possible | Possible and common | Industry restructuring |
| **Cost Behavior** | Fixed costs dominate | Both TFC and TVC exist | Only TVC (TFC = 0) | Structural cost transformation |
| **Demand Influence on Price** | Maximum (Price rationing) | Moderate | Minimal (MC = AC in PC) | Demand shapes supply technology |
| **Managerial Focus** | Pricing, rationing | Output adjustment, Overtime | Capacity planning, Investment | Innovation, R&D, Strategy |
| **Relevant Economic Law** | None (Fixed supply) | Law of Variable Proportions | Law of Returns to Scale | Creative Destruction |
| **Example** | Fish at dock | Factory overtime | New factory construction | Electric vehicle revolution |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Examination Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A bakery currently produces 500 loaves per day. The price of bread rises by 20%. Calculate the percentage change in supply under (a) Market Period, (b) Short Run, (c) Long Run, given that short-run supply elasticity is 0.5 and long-run supply elasticity is 1.5.

### Part (a): Market Period

$$
\%\Delta Q_s^{\text{market}} = E_s^{\text{market}} \times \%\Delta P = 0 \times 20\% = 0\%
$$

**Explanation:** No output adjustment is possible. Supply remains at 500 loaves.

### Part (b): Short Run

$$
\%\Delta Q_s^{\text{short}} = E_s^{\text{short}} \times \%\Delta P = 0.5 \times 20\% = 10\%
$$

**Explanation:** The bakery can increase output by 10% through overtime, extra labor, and more flour purchases.

$$
\Delta Q_s^{\text{short}} = 500 \times 0.10 = 50 \text{ loaves}
$$

New output: $500 + 50 = 550$ loaves.

### Part (c): Long Run

$$
\%\Delta Q_s^{\text{long}} = E_s^{\text{long}} \times \%\Delta P = 1.5 \times 20\% = 30\%
$$

**Explanation:** The bakery can install a second oven, hire more bakers, and expand the storefront.

$$
\Delta Q_s^{\text{long}} = 500 \times 0.30 = 150 \text{ loaves}
$$

New output: $500 + 150 = 650$ loaves.

### Summary of Results

| Period | Elasticity | % Change in Q | New Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Market Period | 0 | 0% | 500 loaves |
| Short Run | 0.5 | 10% | 550 loaves |
| Long Run | 1.5 | 30% | 650 loaves |

**Conclusion:** The same 20% price increase produces vastly different supply responses depending on the time horizon — a quantitative confirmation of the Time Concept's managerial importance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Marshallian Classification of Time Periods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Time Concept in Managerial Economics"] --> B["Market Period"]
    A --> C["Short Run"]
    A --> D["Long Run"]
    A --> E["Very Long Run"]

    B --> B1["All inputs FIXED"]
    B --> B2["Supply is VERTICAL"]
    B --> B3["Elasticity Es = 0"]
    B --> B4["Strategy: PRICE RATIONING"]

    C --> C1["Some inputs FIXED, others VARIABLE"]
    C --> C2["Supply is UPWARD SLOPING"]
    C --> C3["Elasticity 0 LESS THAN Es LESS THAN INFINITY"]
    C --> C4["Strategy: OUTPUT ADJUSTMENT"]

    D --> D1["ALL inputs VARIABLE"]
    D --> D2["Supply is HORIZONTAL in perfect competition"]
    D --> D3["Elasticity Es APPROACHES 1"]
    D --> D4["Strategy: CAPACITY EXPANSION"]

    E --> E1["Technology and INSTITUTIONS change"]
    E --> E2["Supply curve SHIFTS OUTWARD"]
    E --> E3["Elasticity Es GREATER THAN 1"]
    E --> E4["Strategy: INNOVATION and R and D"]
```

**Explanation of Diagram Flow:** The root node **A** represents the umbrella concept of Time in Managerial Economics, which branches into four temporal categories (B, C, D, E). Each category then decomposes into four critical attributes: input adjustability, supply curve geometry, elasticity value, and managerial strategy. This hierarchical structure mirrors how KTU examiners expect students to systematically analyze time-related economic problems.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Elasticity Cascade Across Time Horizons

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Market Period: Es = 0"] -->|TIME ELAPSES| P2["Short Run: 0 LESS THAN Es LESS THAN INFINITY"]
    P2 -->|MORE TIME| P3["Long Run: Es APPROACHES 1"]
    P3 -->|EVEN MORE TIME| P4["Very Long Run: Es GREATER THAN 1"]

    P1 --> Q1["VERTICAL supply curve"]
    P2 --> Q2["UPWARD SLOPING supply curve"]
    P3 --> Q3["HORIZONTAL supply curve"]
    P4 --> Q4["OUTWARD SHIFTING supply curve"]

    Q1 --> R1["Firm controls PRICE"]
    Q2 --> R2["Firm adjusts OUTPUT"]
    Q3 --> R3["Market controls PRICE, firm sets QUANTITY"]
    Q4 --> R4["Industry restructures via INNOVATION"]
```

**Explanation:** This horizontal flow diagram illustrates the **cascading nature** of supply elasticity as time progresses. Each time-period node is linked to its corresponding supply curve geometry and managerial implication, creating a clear cause-and-effect chain from time → elasticity → strategic response.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Managerial Decision-Making Flow Based on Time Horizon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Managerial Decision Triggered by Demand Change"] --> Q{"How much TIME is available?"}

    Q -->|Minutes to Hours| R1["MARKET PERIOD"]
    Q -->|Weeks to Months| R2["SHORT RUN"]
    Q -->|1 to 5 Years| R3["LONG RUN"]
    Q -->|10 PLUS Years| R4["VERY LONG RUN"]

    R1 --> A1["Action: ADJUST PRICE"]
    A1 --> O1["Outcome: Revenue maximization with fixed stock"]

    R2 --> A2["Action: ADJUST VARIABLE INPUTS"]
    A2 --> O2["Outcome: Output increase within capacity limits"]

    R3 --> A3["Action: BUILD or EXPAND CAPACITY"]
    A3 --> O3["Outcome: Full supply adjustment to new demand"]

    R4 --> A4["Action: INNOVATE and RESTRUCTURE"]
    A4 --> O4["Outcome: Industry transformation"]
```

**Explanation:** This decision-tree architecture demonstrates how a manager's first analytical step is to **identify the relevant time horizon** before selecting a course of action. The diamond-shaped decision node **Q** acts as the pivotal classifier, and each downstream path represents a unique managerial protocol. This framework is directly applicable to KTU case-study questions where students must diagnose the time period from contextual clues.
