- **Rahul**, an average middle-class earner, increases his spending on *rice, milk, and bus tickets* only marginally (say 5%) even though his income went up by 10%. His spending barely responds to his income change. → **Low Income Elasticity** (necessity goods).

- **Anjali**, on the other hand, books a **luxury vacation**, upgrades her **smartphone**, and signs up for a **gym membership** — her spending on these items jumps by 25% for the same 10% income rise. → **High Income Elasticity** (luxury/superior goods).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition in one line:** Income Elasticity simply asks — *"When my income changes by 1%, by what percentage does my demand for this particular good change?"*

A positive, negative, or zero answer tells us whether the good is **normal, inferior, or income-neutral**, respectively.

---

### 1.3 Notation, Symbols & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|:-------|:--------|:------------------|
| $E_y$ | Income Elasticity of Demand | Subscript *y* from *yield/income* |
| $\Delta Q_d$ | Change in quantity demanded | Absolute change in demand |
| $\Delta Y$ | Change in consumer income | Absolute change in income |
| $Q_d$ | Original quantity demanded | Base quantity |
| $Y$ | Original consumer income | Base income |
| $\eta_y$ | Greek letter "eta" | Sometimes used as alternative symbol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention:** Since income elasticity can be positive, negative, or zero, it is **NOT** typically expressed in absolute value form (unlike price elasticity in some contexts). The sign itself carries economic meaning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Equation — Decomposed

The master formula for Income Elasticity of Demand is:

$$
E_y = \frac{\text{Percentage change in Quantity Demanded}}{\text{Percentage change in Income}}
$$

Expanding the percentage changes into their underlying ratios:

$$
E_y = \frac{\dfrac{\Delta Q_d}{Q_d} \times 100}{\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} \times 100}
$$

The factor of 100 cancels out (since both numerator and denominator are percentages), simplifying the working formula to:

$$
E_y = \frac{\Delta Q_d / Q_d}{\Delta Y / Y}
$$

This can be cross-multiplied into a more computationally useful form:

$$
E_y = \frac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta Y} \times \frac{Y}{Q_d}
$$

Where:
- $\frac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta Y}$ = the slope-like ratio of absolute changes (a **marginal effect** measure)
- $\frac{Y}{Q_d}$ = the **inverse of the average propensity to consume** (a base-level correction factor)

---

### 2.2 Classification of Goods Based on Income Elasticity

The numerical value and sign of $E_y$ classify a commodity into one of five economically distinct categories:

| Category | Condition on $E_y$ | Economic Meaning | Real-World Example |
|:---------|:-------------------|:-----------------|:-------------------|
| **Superior / Luxury Good** | $E_y > 1$ | Demand rises *faster* than income; consumers buy disproportionately more as they grow richer | Designer clothing, foreign holidays, premium cars, gym memberships |
| **Necessity (Unitary-Normal)** | $E_y = 1$ | Demand rises in the *same proportion* as income | Often a theoretical benchmark; staple food in some models |
| **Normal Necessity** | $0 < E_y < 1$ | Demand rises, but *slower* than income; essential but with spending cap | Salt, basic groceries, public transport, milk, rice |
| **Inferior Good** | $E_y < 0$ | Demand actually *falls* as income rises (Engel's Law in action) | Coarse grains (ragi, jowar), second-hand clothing, tap water, instant noodles |
| **Income-Neutral Good** | $E_y = 0$ | Demand is completely unaffected by income changes | Salt, matchsticks, basic medicines (after a certain threshold) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A good can be a *normal good* with either $0 < E_y < 1$ (necessity) or $E_y > 1$ (luxury). The boundary $E_y = 1$ is **unitary income elasticity** — demand grows *proportionately* with income.

---

### 2.3 The Two Economists Whose Work Made This Concept Famous

**1. Ernst Engel (1821–1896)** — German statistician
- Formulated **Engel's Law**: *"As income increases, the proportion of income spent on food falls, even though the absolute expenditure on food may rise."*
- This implicitly means food has $0 < E_y < 1$ in the long run.
- Also gave us the **Engel Curve** — a plot of quantity demanded against income, holding price constant.

**2. Alfred Marshall (1842–1924)** — British economist
- Formalized elasticity concepts (price, income, cross) in his *Principles of Economics (1890)*.
- Provided the rigorous mathematical apparatus used today.

---

### 2.4 The Three Methods of Computing Income Elasticity

| Method | When Used | Formula | Key Feature |
|:-------|:----------|:--------|:------------|
| **Percentage / Proportionate Method** | Infinitesimally small changes; pure theoretical analysis | $E_y = \frac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta Y} \times \frac{Y}{Q_d}$ | Most commonly used in KTU numericals |
| **Geometric / Point Method** | Specific point on the Engel curve | $E_y = \frac{dQ_d}{dY} \times \frac{Y}{Q_d}$ | Uses calculus (derivatives); used when demand is a function of income |
| **Arc Method (Mid-Point Formula)** | Discrete, finite changes between two points | $E_y = \dfrac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta Q_d} \div \dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y}$ where averages are used: $E_y = \dfrac{(Q_2 - Q_1) / (Q_2 + Q_1)}{(Y_2 - Y_1) / (Y_2 + Y_1)}$ | Symmetric — direction of change doesn't affect the absolute value |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** In the **Arc Method**, the sign of $\Delta Q_d$ and $\Delta Y$ depends on whether you move from point 1 to point 2 or vice-versa. However, the *absolute value* remains the same because both numerator and denominator flip sign. The answer is therefore the **same in both directions** — a property the simple percentage method does NOT possess.

---

### 2.5 Income Elasticity of Demand vs. Price Elasticity of Demand

| Feature | Price Elasticity ($E_p$) | Income Elasticity ($E_y$) |
|:--------|:------------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Numerator variable** | Quantity demanded | Quantity demanded |
| **Denominator variable** | Own price ($P$) | Consumer income ($Y$) |
| **Curve used** | Demand curve ($Q$ vs. $P$) | Engel curve ($Q$ vs. $Y$) |
| **Direction of curve** | Typically downward sloping (law of demand) | Typically upward sloping for normal goods |
| **Sign** | Usually negative (negative sign often dropped) | Can be positive, zero, or negative |
| **Classification** | Elastic, inelastic, unitary | Normal, inferior, luxury, necessity |

---

### 2.6 Utility in Engineering & Managerial Decision-Making

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an engineering student must study this:** Although the topic belongs to Managerial Economics, it is foundational for **operations planning, market segmentation, product-line decisions, and long-term business strategy** — all critical skills for B.Tech graduates entering management, consulting, or entrepreneurial roles.

Real-world engineering applications include:
- **Automotive industry:** Forecasting car sales during GDP growth (luxury cars vs. budget cars).
- **Telecom:** Planning 4G vs. 5G subscription growth as disposable incomes rise.
- **Consumer electronics:** Deciding when to launch premium SKUs in emerging markets.
- **Agricultural engineering:** Understanding shifts in food demand (inferior to superior grains) with rising rural income.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Formula from First Principles

**Step 1 — Start with the basic definition:**
By definition, elasticity is the *responsiveness* of one variable to a unit change in another. For income elasticity, we are tracking how $Q_d$ responds when $Y$ changes.

$$
E_y = \lim_{\Delta Y \to 0} \frac{\text{Relative change in } Q_d}{\text{Relative change in } Y}
$$

**Step 2 — Express "relative change" as a ratio:**
A relative (percentage) change in $Q_d$ is the absolute change divided by the base value:

$$
\text{Relative change in } Q_d = \frac{\Delta Q_d}{Q_d}
$$

$$
\text{Relative change in } Y = \frac{\Delta Y}{Y}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**
Substituting these into the definition:

$$
E_y = \frac{\Delta Q_d / Q_d}{\Delta Y / Y}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply for computational simplicity:**
Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal:

$$
E_y = \frac{\Delta Q_d}{Q_d} \times \frac{Y}{\Delta Y}
$$

Reordering for clarity:

$$
E_y = \frac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta Y} \times \frac{Y}{Q_d}
$$

**Step 5 — Confirm dimensions cancel out:**
$\Delta Q_d$ has units of *units* (e.g., kg), $Q_d$ has units of *kg* — they cancel.
$\Delta Y$ has units of *rupees*, $Y$ has units of *rupees* — they cancel.
The result is **dimensionless**, confirming that income elasticity is a pure number.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Geometric (Point) Method from the Engel Curve

**Step 1 — Recognize that demand is a function of income:**
The quantity demanded, holding all prices constant, is a function of income alone:

$$
Q_d = f(Y)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the total differential:**
If $Y$ changes by an infinitesimal amount $dY$, then $Q_d$ changes by:

$$
dQ_d = f'(Y) \cdot dY = \frac{dQ_d}{dY} \cdot dY
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to relative (percentage) changes:**
Divide both sides by $Q_d$ on the left and $Y$ on the right:

$$
\frac{dQ_d}{Q_d} = \frac{dQ_d}{dY} \cdot \frac{Y}{Q_d} \cdot \frac{dY}{Y}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the elasticity ratio:**

$$
E_y = \frac{dQ_d / Q_d}{dY / Y} = \frac{dQ_d}{dY} \cdot \frac{Y}{Q_d}
$$

This is the **point elasticity formula** — the slope of the Engel curve at a point, scaled by the income-to-quantity ratio at that same point.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Arc (Mid-Point) Method

**Step 1 — Define two observation points:**
Let the initial state be $(Q_1, Y_1)$ and the final state be $(Q_2, Y_2)$.

**Step 2 — Use the average as the base:**
The simple percentage method gives different answers depending on whether we go $1 \to 2$ or $2 \to 1$, because the base value differs. To fix this, we use the **arithmetic mean** of the two values as the base:

$$
\text{Base for } Q_d = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Base for } Y = \frac{Y_1 + Y_2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the relative changes:**

$$
\frac{\Delta Q_d}{Q_d^{avg}} = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{(Q_1 + Q_2)/2}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta Y}{Y^{avg}} = \frac{Y_2 - Y_1}{(Y_1 + Y_2)/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio:**

$$
E_y = \frac{(Q_2 - Q_1) / \{(Q_1 + Q_2)/2\}}{(Y_2 - Y_1) / \{(Y_1 + Y_2)/2\}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2 (which cancels out):

$$
E_y = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_1 + Q_2} \times \frac{Y_1 + Y_2}{Y_2 - Y_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify symmetry:**
If we swap indices 1 and 2, both $(Q_2 - Q_1)$ and $(Y_2 - Y_1)$ flip sign, but $(Q_1 + Q_2)$ and $(Y_1 + Y_2)$ remain unchanged. The sign flips twice — the result is **identical** in both directions. The arc method is therefore **symmetric**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A consumer's income rises from ₹40,000 per month to ₹44,000 per month. As a result, her monthly demand for a particular brand of organic honey rises from 6 jars to 8 jars. Calculate the income elasticity of demand using (a) the Simple Percentage Method, and (b) the Arc Method. Classify the good.

**Given:**
- $Y_1 = 40{,}000$, $Y_2 = 44{,}000$
- $Q_1 = 6$, $Q_2 = 8$

**Part (a) — Simple Percentage Method:**

Step 1: Compute the change in income.
$$
\Delta Y = Y_2 - Y_1 = 44{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 4{,}000
$$

Step 2: Compute the percentage change in income (using $Y_1$ as base).
$$
\%\Delta Y = \frac{4{,}000}{40{,}000} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

Step 3: Compute the change in quantity demanded.
$$
\Delta Q_d = Q_2 - Q_1 = 8 - 6 = 2
$$

Step 4: Compute the percentage change in quantity demanded (using $Q_1$ as base).
$$
\%\Delta Q_d = \frac{2}{6} \times 100 = 33.33\%
$$

Step 5: Form the ratio.
$$
E_y = \frac{33.33\%}{10\%} = 3.33
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $E_y = 3.33 > 1$ → The good is a **luxury / superior good** in this range. A 1% rise in income causes a 3.33% rise in demand — demand is highly income-sensitive.

---

**Part (b) — Arc Method (Mid-Point Formula):**

Step 1: Compute the change in quantity demanded (numerator's numerator).
$$
\Delta Q_d = Q_2 - Q_1 = 8 - 6 = 2
$$

Step 2: Compute the average quantity demanded (numerator's denominator).
$$
Q_d^{avg} = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2} = \frac{6 + 8}{2} = 7
$$

Step 3: Compute the relative change in quantity demanded.
$$
\frac{\Delta Q_d}{Q_d^{avg}} = \frac{2}{7} \approx 0.2857
$$

Step 4: Compute the change in income.
$$
\Delta Y = Y_2 - Y_1 = 44{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 4{,}000
$$

Step 5: Compute the average income.
$$
Y^{avg} = \frac{Y_1 + Y_2}{2} = \frac{40{,}000 + 44{,}000}{2} = 42{,}000
$$

Step 6: Compute the relative change in income.
$$
\frac{\Delta Y}{Y^{avg}} = \frac{4{,}000}{42{,}000} \approx 0.0952
$$

Step 7: Form the ratio.
$$
E_y = \frac{0.2857}{0.0952} = 3.00
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $E_y = 3.00 > 1$ → Confirms the good is a **luxury good**. Note that the arc method gives a slightly different value (3.00 vs. 3.33) from the simple method because it uses a different base. This is expected and the classification is unchanged.

---

### 3.5 Decision Matrix — How Firms Use Income Elasticity

| Industry | Typical $E_y$ | Strategic Implication |
|:---------|:--------------|:-----------------------|
| Basic food grains | $0.2 \text{ to } 0.5$ | Income-inelastic; stable demand regardless of recession |
| Packaged FMCG | $0.6 \text{ to } 0.9$ | Mildly income-sensitive; volume plays matter |
| Two-wheeler vehicles | $1.0 \text{ to } 1.3$ | Highly income-sensitive; sales track GDP growth |
| Four-wheeler (economy) | $1.3 \text{ to } 1.8$ | Luxury-tendency; benefits from boom cycles |
| Four-wheeler (premium) | $2.0 \text{ to } 3.5$ | Pure luxury; collapses in downturns |
| International tourism | $2.5 \text{ to } 4.0$ | Ultra-luxury; first to be cut in recessions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Income Elasticity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Consumer Income Y rises by deltaY]) --> calcA[Compute percentage change in Y]
    startB([Consumer Income Y rises by deltaY]) --> calcB[Compute percentage change in Qd]
    calcA --> ratio[Form ratio deltaQd over Qd divided by deltaY over Y]
    calcB --> ratio
    ratio --> branch{Is Ey positive, zero, or negative?}
    branch --> positive[Ey greater than 0]
    branch --> zeroN[Ey equals 0]
    branch --> negative[Ey less than 0]
    positive --> pcheck{Is Ey greater than 1 or less than 1?}
    pcheck --> luxury[Ey greater than 1: LUXURY GOOD]
    pcheck --> necessity[0 less than Ey less than 1: NORMAL NECESSITY]
    zeroN --> neutral[INCOME NEUTRAL GOOD e.g. salt]
    negative --> inferior[INFERIOR GOOD e.g. coarse grains]
    luxury --> strat1[Firm strategy: Premium pricing, capacity expansion in boom cycles]
    necessity --> strat2[Firm strategy: Mass market, cost leadership, stable volumes]
    neutral --> strat3[Firm strategy: Static production planning]
    inferior --> strat4[Firm strategy: Exit or reposition as economy improves]
    strat1 --> outcome[Revenue and profit forecast adjustment]
    strat2 --> outcome
    strat3 --> outcome
    strat4 --> outcome
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Decision Flow in a Manufacturing Firm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Macro economist forecasts GDP growth] --> B[Estimate percentage rise in disposable income]
    B --> C[Look up Ey for each product line]
    C --> D{Compare Ey across portfolio}
    D --> E1[Luxury SKUs: Ey greater than 1]
    D --> E2[Staple SKUs: 0 less than Ey less than 1]
    D --> E3[Inferior SKUs: Ey less than 0]
    E1 --> F1[Increase production capacity, raise advertising budget]
    E2 --> F2[Hold production steady, optimize cost]
    E3 --> F3[Reduce production, consider product discontinuation]
    F1 --> G[Annual revenue and capacity plan finalized]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Relationships to Other Elasticity Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    parent[Elasticity of Demand family]
    parent --> priceP[Price Elasticity of Demand: Ep]
    parent --> crossX[Cross Elasticity of Demand: Ex]
    parent --> incomeY[Income Elasticity of Demand: Ey]
    incomeY --> n1[Normal good: Ey greater than 0]
    incomeY --> n2[Inferior good: Ey less than 0]
    incomeY --> n3[Neutral good: Ey equals 0]
    n1 --> n1a[Luxury: Ey greater than 1]
    n1 --> n1b[Necessity: 0 less than Ey less than 1]
    n1a --> engelA[Engel curve convex upward]
    n1b --> engelB[Engel curve concave]
    n2 --> engelC[Engel curve downward sloping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The third diagram shows how Income Elasticity is one of three members of the elasticity family and how the Engel curve's *shape* depends on the value of $E_y$. Convex Engel curves correspond to luxuries, concave curves to necessities, and downward-sloping curves to inferior goods.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define Income Elasticity of Demand. What does a negative value of $E_y$ indicate about a commodity?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Income Elasticity of Demand is the degree of responsiveness of quantity demanded of a commodity to a change in the consumer's income, *ceteris paribus*. It is mathematically defined as:

$$
E_y = \frac{\text{Percentage change in Quantity Demanded}}{\text{Percentage change in Income}}
$$

**Negative value interpretation (1 mark):** A negative value of $E_y$ means that as the consumer's income rises, the quantity demanded of the commodity *falls*. Such a commodity is classified as an **inferior good** (e.g., coarse grains, second-hand clothing, or instant noodles). The consumer shifts to higher-quality substitutes as their purchasing power improves.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
Distinguish between a **luxury good** and a **necessity** on the basis of Income Elasticity of Demand. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Luxury Good | Necessity |
|:------|:------------|:----------|
| Income Elasticity | $E_y > 1$ | $0 < E_y < 1$ |
| Behaviour | Demand rises *more than proportionately* with income | Demand rises *less than proportionately* with income |
| Example | Foreign vacation, premium sedan car | Rice, milk, public bus ticket |

(1 mark each for correct $E_y$ condition, 1 mark for behaviour distinction, 1 mark for examples.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Question A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **three methods** of measuring Income Elasticity of Demand with suitable formulas. State one limitation of the Simple Percentage Method.

**(b) [7 marks]** A consumer's monthly income rises from ₹50,000 to ₹56,000. Her demand for brand-A basmati rice increases from 10 kg to 12 kg per month. Calculate the income elasticity using (i) the Simple Percentage Method and (ii) the Arc Method. Classify the good.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The three methods are:

**1. Percentage / Proportionate Method (2 marks):**
This method uses the simple percentage changes of both quantity and income. The formula is:

$$
E_y = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta Y} = \frac{\Delta Q_d / Q_d}{\Delta Y / Y} = \frac{\Delta Q_d}{\Delta Y} \times \frac{Y}{Q_d}
$$

**2. Geometric / Point Method (2 marks):**
Used when the demand function $Q_d = f(Y)$ is given. It uses derivatives:

$$
E_y = \frac{dQ_d}{dY} \cdot \frac{Y}{Q_d}
$$

**3. Arc Method / Mid-Point Formula (2 marks):**
Used for discrete finite changes between two points. It uses the average of the two values as the base:

$$
E_y = \frac{(Q_2 - Q_1) / \{(Q_1 + Q_2)/2\}}{(Y_2 - Y_1) / \{(Y_1 + Y_2)/2\}}
$$

**Limitation of the Simple Percentage Method (1 mark):** It gives different values of $E_y$ when the direction of change is reversed (i.e., going from point 1 to point 2 vs. point 2 to point 1) because the base value differs in the two cases. The arc method overcomes this drawback by using the average as the base.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $Y_1 = 50{,}000$, $Y_2 = 56{,}000$, $Q_1 = 10$ kg, $Q_2 = 12$ kg.

**(i) Simple Percentage Method (3 marks):**

Step 1:
$$
\Delta Y = 56{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 6{,}000
$$

Step 2:
$$
\%\Delta Y = \frac{6{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 12\%
$$

Step 3:
$$
\Delta Q_d = 12 - 10 = 2
$$

Step 4:
$$
\%\Delta Q_d = \frac{2}{10} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

Step 5:
$$
E_y = \frac{20\%}{12\%} = 1.67
$$

**(ii) Arc Method (3 marks):**

Step 1:
$$
Q_d^{avg} = \frac{10 + 12}{2} = 11
$$

Step 2:
$$
Y^{avg} = \frac{50{,}000 + 56{,}000}{2} = 53{,}000
$$

Step 3:
$$
E_y = \frac{(12 - 10) / 11}{(56{,}000 - 50{,}000) / 53{,}000} = \frac{2/11}{6{,}000/53{,}000} = \frac{0.1818}{0.1132} = 1.606
$$

**Classification (1 mark):** In both methods, $E_y > 1$, therefore **brand-A basmati rice is a luxury (superior) good** in this income range.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Question B — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** What is an **Engel Curve**? Explain the shape of the Engel curve for (i) a normal necessity, (ii) a luxury good, and (iii) an inferior good. How is it different from a demand curve?

**(b) [7 marks]** A firm observes the following data for two goods X and Y. Income rises from ₹30,000 to ₹36,000. Demand for X rises from 20 units to 28 units, and demand for Y falls from 50 units to 45 units. Compute $E_y$ for both goods (using the Simple Percentage Method), classify them, and advise the firm on its production strategy.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Engel Curve Definition (2 marks):** An Engel Curve, named after Ernst Engel, is a graphical representation of the relationship between a consumer's income (Y) and the quantity demanded (Q) of a good, *holding the price of the good and all other factors constant*. It is plotted with income on the X-axis and quantity demanded on the Y-axis.

**Shape for different goods (4 marks):**

| Good Type | Income Elasticity | Engel Curve Shape |
|:----------|:------------------|:------------------|
| Normal necessity | $0 < E_y < 1$ | Upward-sloping and **concave** (rises but at a decreasing rate) |
| Luxury / superior good | $E_y > 1$ | Upward-sloping and **convex** (rises at an increasing rate) |
| Inferior good | $E_y < 0$ | **Downward-sloping** (demand falls as income rises) |

**Difference from a demand curve (1 mark):** A demand curve plots quantity demanded against the *price* of the good (typically downward-sloping due to the law of demand), whereas an Engel curve plots quantity demanded against *consumer income* (typically upward-sloping for normal goods). The demand curve reflects the price-consumption relationship; the Engel curve reflects the income-consumption relationship.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $Y_1 = 30{,}000$, $Y_2 = 36{,}000$.

**For Good X:** $Q_1 = 20$, $Q_2 = 28$.

Step 1:
$$
\%\Delta Y = \frac{36{,}000 - 30{,}000}{30{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{6{,}000}{30{,}000} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

Step 2:
$$
\%\Delta Q_X = \frac{28 - 20}{20} \times 100 = \frac{8}{20} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

Step 3:
$$
E_y^X = \frac{40\%}{20\%} = 2.0
$$

**Classification (1 mark):** $E_y^X = 2.0 > 1$ → Good X is a **luxury good**.

**For Good Y:** $Q_1 = 50$, $Q_2 = 45$.

Step 4:
$$
\%\Delta Q_Y = \frac{45 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \frac{-5}{50} \times 100 = -10\%
$$

Step 5:
$$
E_y^Y = \frac{-10\%}{20\%} = -0.5
$$

**Classification (1 mark):** $E_y^Y = -0.5 < 0$ → Good Y is an **inferior good**.

**Production Strategy Advice (1 mark):**
- For **Good X (luxury)**: The firm should *expand capacity and increase marketing spend* during income upturns, since demand will grow more than proportionately. However, it should also build a buffer for downturns, as luxury demand collapses faster than income.
- For **Good Y (inferior)**: The firm should *gradually reduce production* and consider *repositioning the product* (e.g., a budget variant) or *divesting* as consumer incomes continue to rise. Long-term demand is structurally falling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention is sacred:** Never report a negative sign for a normal good or a positive sign for an inferior good. The sign *is* the classification.
> 2. **Don't confuse "normal" with "luxury":** All luxury goods are normal, but not all normal goods are luxuries. The KTU answer key distinguishes carefully between $0 < E_y < 1$ (necessity) and $E_y > 1$ (luxury).
> 3. **Always state units of measurement in the final answer** for quantity (kg, units, litres) — losing 0.5 mark here is a common error.
> 4. **Don't forget to classify the good** in the final step of every numerical — a correct number with no classification loses 1 full mark.
> 5. **In the Arc Method**, do NOT use the simple base ($Q_1$ or $Y_1$). Use the *average* — the most common mistake students make.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**

- **Definition:** $E_y = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta Y}$ — the responsiveness of demand to income changes, *ceteris paribus*.
- **It is a pure number** (dimensionless) — units cancel out.
- **Sign carries meaning:** Positive = normal good; Negative = inferior good; Zero = income-neutral.
- **Magnitude carries classification:**
  - $E_y > 1$ → **Luxury / Superior good** (demand grows *faster* than income)
  - $E_y = 1$ → **Unitary income elasticity** (demand grows *in proportion* to income)
  - $0 < E_y < 1$ → **Normal necessity** (demand grows *slower* than income)
  - $E_y < 0$ → **Inferior good** (demand *falls* as income rises)
- **Three methods of calculation:** Simple Percentage, Geometric (Point), and Arc (Mid-Point) — the Arc method is **symmetric** and **direction-independent**.
- **Arc Method formula to memorize:**
$$
E_y = \frac{(Q_2 - Q_1)}{(Q_1 + Q_2)} \times \frac{(Y_1 + Y_2)}{(Y_2 - Y_1)}
$$
- **Engel Curve** plots $Q_d$ vs. $Y$: **convex** for luxuries, **concave** for necessities, **downward-sloping** for inferior goods.
- **Engel's Law:** As income rises, the *proportion* of income spent on food falls (food is income-inelastic in the long run).
- **Real-world classification cheats:**
  - Salt, matchsticks → $E_y \approx 0$
  - Rice, milk, bus tickets → $0 < E_y < 1$
  - Branded clothing, dining out → $1 < E_y < 2$
  - Luxury cars, foreign holidays → $E_y > 2$
- **Managerial use:** Demand forecasting, capacity planning, product-line decisions, business cycle strategy.
- **Always classify the good** at the end of every KTU numerical — partial marks are heavily penalized without the final classification line.
- **Cross-check** your $E_y$ value with real-world intuition (e.g., a negative $E_y$ for a smartphone would be wrong) — a sanity check can save you from silly sign errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cross Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross Elasticity of Demand (E_c or E_xy)** is a measure of the **responsiveness of the quantity demanded of one commodity (X)** to a **change in the price of another commodity (Y)**, while all other factors (income, tastes, prices of other goods) are held constant (ceteris paribus).

It is a directional elasticity that establishes an **inter-commodity relationship**, allowing firms to predict how a change in the price of a related product will affect the demand for their own product. Mathematically, it expresses the ratio of the **percentage change in the quantity demanded of commodity X** to the **percentage change in the price of commodity Y**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a "see-saw between two products in a customer's mind."**

Imagine you walk into a canteen and always buy **Tea (X)**. Suddenly, the price of **Coffee (Y)** — your backup beverage — drops by ₹10. What do you do? You probably buy more **Coffee** and less **Tea**. This is because Tea and Coffee are **substitutes**. The Cross Elasticity quantifies exactly *how much* the demand for Tea will fall when Coffee becomes cheaper.

Now reverse the scenario: you buy a **Printer (X)** and also **Ink Cartridges (Y)**. If the price of ink cartridges skyrockets, you will print less, and consequently, you will demand fewer printers. The Printer and Ink are **complements** — they are used together. Cross Elasticity here is **negative**.

Finally, consider the price of **Petrol** rising. Will it change the demand for **Salt**? Almost certainly not. They are **unrelated goods**, and Cross Elasticity is **zero**.

- **Cross Elasticity is POSITIVE → Goods are SUBSTITUTES** (Tea ↔ Coffee)
- **Cross Elasticity is NEGATIVE → Goods are COMPLEMENTS** (Printer ↔ Ink)
- **Cross Elasticity is ZERO → Goods are UNRELATED** (Salt ↔ Petrol)

## 1.3 Boundary Conditions (Standard Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign** of Cross Elasticity, not its magnitude, is the primary diagnostic tool for classifying goods. The standard reference value is **0 (Zero)**.
> - **E_c > 0** → Substitutes (degree varies with value)
> - **E_c < 0** → Complements (degree varies with value)
> - **E_c = 0** → Unrelated / Independent goods

The **unit of Cross Elasticity** is a **dimensionless pure number** (it is a ratio of two percentages, so all units cancel out).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Logic Breakdown

The logic of Cross Elasticity rests on three pillars that students must internalize:

- **Pillar 1 — The Independent Variable is NOT your own price.** Unlike price elasticity of demand (PED), the denominator is the price of a *different* good. This is the most common conceptual trap for KTU students.
- **Pillar 2 — The Sign is the Story.** The numerical sign (+/−) tells you the *type* of relationship between two products, while the magnitude tells you the *strength* of that relationship.
- **Pillar 3 — Asymmetry Principle.** Cross Elasticity is generally **asymmetric**. E_xy is *not* necessarily equal to E_yx. For example, a 10% rise in coffee price may reduce tea demand by 8% (E_xy = 0.8), but a 10% rise in tea price may reduce coffee demand by only 5% (E_yx = 0.5) due to brand loyalty, habit, or market share.

## 2.2 Methods of Measuring Cross Elasticity

There are **three computational methods** that KTU examiners frequently test:

- **Method 1 — Percentage / Proportionate Method:** Used when absolute quantities and prices are given. The standard formula.
- **Method 2 — Point / Geometric Method:** Used when a demand function of the form Q_x = a − bP_y is given.
- **Method 3 — Arc / Midpoint Method:** Used when changes are large (e.g., "from ₹10 to ₹15") and the student must avoid the base-period bias.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas. They are 90% of any numerical problem in the ESE.**

| Method | Formula | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simple Percentage Method** | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_x}{\%\Delta P_y} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_x / Q_x}{\Delta P_y / P_y}$ | Direct percentage changes are given |
| **Proportionate Method** | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x}$ | Absolute changes (Δ) and original (Q_x, P_y) values are given |
| **Point / Geometric Method** | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{dQ_x}{dP_y} \times \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x}$ | A demand function $Q_x = a - bP_y$ is given; find derivative w.r.t. $P_y$ |
| **Arc / Midpoint Method** | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_x / (Q_1 + Q_2)}{\Delta P_y / (P_1 + P_2)} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \dfrac{(P_1 + P_2)}{(Q_1 + Q_2)}$ | Two price–quantity pairs given (e.g., "from ₹10 to ₹15"); eliminates base-year bias |

**Decision Table — Interpreting the Sign:**

| Sign of E_c | Classification | Business Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_c > 0$ | **Substitutes** (e.g., Coke & Pepsi) | If rival raises price, your sales rise — opportunity to capture market share |
| $E_c < 0$ | **Complements** (e.g., Cars & Petrol) | If complement's price rises, your sales fall — bundle pricing strategy needed |
| $E_c = 0$ | **Unrelated / Independent** (e.g., Rice & Laptops) | No pricing linkage; manage independently |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

- **Pricing Strategy:** Multinational firms (Apple, Samsung) use cross elasticity to predict cannibalisation within their own product lines (iPhone 15 vs. iPhone 16).
- **Market Definition & Antitrust Law:** The U.S. Department of Justice uses cross elasticity to define a "relevant market." High positive cross elasticity between two brands means they belong to the same market and a merger would reduce competition.
- **Joint Demand Forecasting:** A car manufacturer's revenue is highly sensitive to the price of petrol (complement). A 20% rise in petrol price may slash car demand by 30%.
- **Joint Supply Decisions:** A firm producing both shoes and shoe polish must consider the negative cross elasticity between shoes and cheap substitutes like slippers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Basic Identity to the Percentage Method

**Starting Definition:**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\text{Proportionate change in Quantity demanded of X}}{\text{Proportionate change in Price of Y}}$$

**Step 1:** Express the proportionate change in quantity of X as a ratio of the change to the original quantity.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x / Q_x}{\Delta P_y / P_y}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify by applying the algebraic rule for division of fractions (multiply by reciprocal of denominator).

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{Q_x} \times \frac{P_y}{\Delta P_y}$$

**Step 3:** Re-arrange the constants for computational clarity.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$$

This is the **Proportionate Method Formula** — the most frequently tested form in KTU numericals.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Point Method from a Linear Demand Function

Suppose the demand function for commodity X, in terms of the price of Y, is given by:

$$Q_x = a - b \cdot P_y$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate both sides of the equation with respect to $P_y$ (treat a as a constant).

$$\frac{dQ_x}{dP_y} = 0 - b = -b$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this slope into the Point Elasticity formula. The negative sign appears naturally.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{dQ_x}{dP_y} \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x} = (-b) \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$$

**Step 3:** Final Point Elasticity Equation.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{-b \cdot P_y}{Q_x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** is critical. Since $b > 0$ (the slope of a typical demand curve is negative in usual form, but here it represents responsiveness), this yields a **negative cross elasticity → complements**. If the demand function is $Q_x = a + b \cdot P_y$ (with positive slope), the goods are **substitutes**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arc Method (Eliminating Base-Year Bias)

The Percentage Method has a flaw: if you measure from P1 to P2, you get one answer; if you measure from P2 to P1, you get a different answer (base-year bias).

**Step 1:** Average the base and new quantities to remove bias.

$$\overline{Q_x} = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Average the base and new prices of Y.

$$\overline{P_y} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Express the proportionate change using these averages.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x / \overline{Q_x}}{\Delta P_y / \overline{P_y}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the average expressions back in.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2}} \times \frac{\frac{P_1 + P_2}{2}}{\Delta P_y}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the fractions by multiplying numerator and denominator by 2.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{2 \, \Delta Q_x}{Q_1 + Q_2} \times \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2 \, \Delta P_y}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel the 2s and present the final compact form.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_1 + P_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$$

This is the **Arc Cross Elasticity Formula** — use it whenever two distinct price–quantity pairs are given.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** When the price of commodity Y rises from ₹20 to ₹25, the quantity demanded of commodity X falls from 100 units to 80 units. Calculate the Cross Elasticity of Demand of X for Y. Identify the relationship.

**Solution (Proportionate Method):**

**Step 1:** Identify and compute the changes.

$$\Delta Q_x = 80 - 100 = -20 \text{ units}$$

$$\Delta P_y = 25 - 20 = +5 \text{ rupees}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the original/base values.

$$Q_x = 100, \quad P_y = 20$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the Proportionate Method Formula.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$$

**Step 4:** Plug in the numbers.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{-20}{+5} \times \frac{20}{100}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the first fraction.

$$E_{xy} = (-4) \times \frac{20}{100}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the second fraction and finalize.

$$E_{xy} = (-4) \times (0.2) = -0.8$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **E_c = −0.8 (Negative).** Since the cross elasticity is **negative**, X and Y are **Complementary Goods**. For every 1% rise in the price of Y, the demand for X falls by 0.8%.

**Solution (Arc Method — for cross-check):**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_1 + P_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$$

$$E_{xy} = \frac{-20}{+5} \times \frac{20 + 25}{100 + 80}$$

$$E_{xy} = (-4) \times \frac{45}{180}$$

$$E_{xy} = (-4) \times (0.25) = -1.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The arc method gives **E_c = −1.0** because it accounts for the average of the two positions, eliminating base-period bias. In KTU examinations, **state clearly which method you are using**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Substitutes vs. Complements vs. Unrelated

| Feature | Substitutes (E_c > 0) | Complements (E_c < 0) | Unrelated (E_c = 0) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sign of E_c** | Positive | Negative | Zero |
| **Demand Response** | Price up of Y → Demand up of X | Price up of Y → Demand down of X | No response |
| **Consumer Behaviour** | "Switching" effect | "Joint consumption" effect | No linkage |
| **Real Example** | Tea & Coffee | Cars & Petrol | Salt & Smartphone |
| **Firm Strategy** | Competitive pricing, advertising | Bundling, joint promotion | Independent decisions |
| **Cross-Demand Curve Slope** | Positive (upward sloping) | Negative (downward sloping) | Vertical (no change) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Classification Tree

The following Mermaid block renders the **decision logic** a student must follow when classifying two goods based on the sign of their Cross Elasticity.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Start: Calculate Cross Elasticity E_c] --> nodeB{What is the Sign of E_c?}
    nodeB -- E_c greater than 0 --> nodeC[E_c greater than 0: SUBSTITUTES]
    nodeB -- E_c less than 0 --> nodeD[E_c less than 0: COMPLEMENTS]
    nodeB -- E_c equals 0 --> nodeE[E_c equals 0: UNRELATED GOODS]

    nodeC --> nodeF[Examples: Tea vs Coffee, Coke vs Pepsi]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Demand Response: Price up of Y causes demand up of X]

    nodeD --> nodeH[Examples: Printer vs Ink, Car vs Petrol]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Demand Response: Price up of Y causes demand down of X]

    nodeE --> nodeJ[Examples: Salt vs Laptop, Rice vs Pen]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Demand Response: No change in X for any change in P_y]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How a Firm Uses Cross Elasticity

This diagram maps the **decision-making flow** inside a firm that uses cross elasticity to plan pricing, marketing, and product strategy.

```mermaid
graph TD
    firm[Managerial Decision Hub] --> data[Collect Price and Demand Data for X and Y]
    data --> calc[Apply Cross Elasticity Formula E_xy]
    calc --> sign{Interpret Sign of E_c}

    sign -- Positive --> sub[Identify Goods as Substitutes]
    sign -- Negative --> comp[Identify Goods as Complements]
    sign -- Zero --> unrel[Identify Goods as Unrelated]

    sub --> strat1[Strategy: Competitive Pricing to Capture Market Share]
    sub --> strat2[Strategy: Aggressive Advertising to Differentiate Brand]

    comp --> strat3[Strategy: Bundle Pricing to Encourage Joint Sales]
    comp --> strat4[Strategy: Joint Promotional Offers]

    unrel --> strat5[Strategy: Independent Pricing and Promotion]
    unrel --> strat6[Strategy: No Cross-Product Forecasting Required]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Cross-Demand Curve (Substitutes vs. Complements)

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisO[Origin] --> axisP[Price of Y Axis P_y]
    axisO --> axisQ[Quantity of X Axis Q_x]

    subCurve[Positive Slope: Substitutes - upward sloping line] -.-> axisP
    subCurve -.-> axisQ

    compCurve[Negative Slope: Complements - downward sloping line] -.-> axisP
    compCurve -.-> axisQ

    zeroLine[Vertical Line: Unrelated - no slope] -.-> axisP
    zeroLine -.-> axisQ
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Cross Elasticity of Demand. What does a negative value of cross elasticity indicate?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cross Elasticity of Demand is the measure of responsiveness of the quantity demanded of one commodity to a change in the price of another related commodity, other things being constant. **(1 Mark)** It is mathematically expressed as the ratio of the percentage change in quantity demanded of commodity X to the percentage change in the price of commodity Y. **(1 Mark)** A **negative value of cross elasticity** indicates that the two goods are **complementary goods** — they are consumed jointly (e.g., cars and petrol, printers and ink). A rise in the price of one leads to a fall in the demand for the other. **(1 Mark)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between substitutes and complements using the concept of cross elasticity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Substitutes | Complements |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sign of E_c** | Positive (E_c > 0) | Negative (E_c < 0) |
| **Nature** | Goods that can replace each other | Goods consumed together |
| **Price Effect** | Rise in price of Y → Rise in demand for X | Rise in price of Y → Fall in demand for X |
| **Examples** | Tea and Coffee, Coke and Pepsi | Cars and Petrol, Mobiles and Chargers |

**(1 Mark for correct definition of each; 1 Mark for table; 1 Mark for examples and conclusion.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Choice Rule:** Within a module, students answer EITHER the full OR alternative question. Both choices below are full 14-mark questions with 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Attempt any ONE of the following:**

**(a)** Define Cross Elasticity of Demand. Explain the various types of cross elasticity with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

Cross Elasticity of Demand is the percentage change in the quantity demanded of commodity X in response to a percentage change in the price of commodity Y, other factors remaining constant. **(1 Mark)**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_x}{\%\Delta P_y} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$$

**Types of Cross Elasticity: (6 Marks)**

1. **Positive Cross Elasticity — Substitutes:** When a rise in the price of Y leads to a rise in the demand for X. Example: Tea and Coffee. If the price of coffee rises, consumers switch to tea, increasing tea demand. The goods satisfy the same want. **(1.5 Marks)**

2. **Negative Cross Elasticity — Complements:** When a rise in the price of Y leads to a fall in the demand for X. Example: Cars and Petrol. If petrol prices rise, car usage becomes expensive, reducing demand for cars. The goods are jointly consumed. **(1.5 Marks)**

3. **Zero Cross Elasticity — Unrelated Goods:** When a change in the price of Y has no effect on the demand for X. Example: Salt and Smartphones. The goods have no functional relationship, so price changes in one do not affect the demand for the other. **(1.5 Marks)**

4. **Very High Positive Cross Elasticity — Perfect Substitutes:** When the cross elasticity is infinite. Example: Two brands of the same milk sold by the same shop. Any tiny price change makes consumers switch entirely. **(1.5 Marks)**

**(b)** When the price of commodity Y increases from ₹40 to ₹50, the quantity demanded of commodity X decreases from 200 units to 160 units. Calculate the cross elasticity of demand using (i) the Percentage Method and (ii) the Arc Method. Interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Given Data:** $P_{y1} = 40$, $P_{y2} = 50$, $Q_{x1} = 200$, $Q_{x2} = 160$.

**Step 1:** Compute changes.
- $\Delta Q_x = 160 - 200 = -40$ units
- $\Delta P_y = 50 - 40 = +10$ rupees **(0.5 Marks)**

**(i) Percentage Method: (3 Marks)**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$$

$$E_{xy} = \frac{-40}{+10} \times \frac{40}{200}$$

$$E_{xy} = (-4) \times (0.2) = -0.8$$

**(ii) Arc Method: (3 Marks)**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_1 + P_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$$

$$E_{xy} = \frac{-40}{+10} \times \frac{40 + 50}{200 + 160}$$

$$E_{xy} = (-4) \times \frac{90}{360} = (-4) \times 0.25 = -1.0$$

**Step 2: Interpretation (0.5 Marks)**
Both values are **negative**, confirming that X and Y are **complementary goods**. A 1% rise in the price of Y leads to a 0.8% (Percentage) or 1.0% (Arc) fall in the demand for X.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Attempt any ONE of the following:**

**(a)** What is the importance of Cross Elasticity of Demand in managerial decision-making? Discuss its role in pricing, product line, and competitive analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

Cross Elasticity of Demand is a critical managerial tool for inter-product strategic planning. **(0.5 Marks)** Its importance is discussed below:

1. **Pricing Decisions (2 Marks):** Firms use cross elasticity to set the right price. If a firm's product is a substitute for a rival's, the firm can track rival price hikes to estimate its own demand surge. For complements, the firm can use bundling (e.g., MS Office Suite) to push joint sales.

2. **Product Line Decisions (1.5 Marks):** A firm introducing a new variant (e.g., iPhone 16 alongside iPhone 15) must estimate cannibalisation — the loss of iPhone 15 sales to iPhone 16. Cross elasticity helps measure this internal cannibalisation.

3. **Competitive Analysis (1.5 Marks):** High positive cross elasticity between two brands means they are close competitors. Firms can identify their true rivals and benchmark pricing, advertising spend, and product features.

4. **Market Segmentation and Demand Forecasting (1 Mark):** Cross elasticity helps forecast demand when input prices (e.g., raw materials, fuel) change. A car manufacturer can use it to model the effect of petrol price hikes on car demand.

5. **Public Policy and Taxation (0.5 Marks):** Governments use cross elasticity to define markets for antitrust cases and to forecast the impact of taxing complements (e.g., fuel taxes indirectly affecting transport demand).

**(b)** The demand function of commodity X is given by $Q_x = 50 - 2P_x + 3P_y$, where $P_x$ and $P_y$ are the prices of X and Y respectively. If $P_x = 5$ and $P_y = 4$, calculate the cross elasticity of demand of X with respect to the price of Y. Identify the relationship. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Given:** $Q_x = 50 - 2P_x + 3P_y$, with $P_x = 5$ and $P_y = 4$.

**Step 1:** Compute $Q_x$ at the given prices.
- $Q_x = 50 - 2(5) + 3(4) = 50 - 10 + 12 = 52$ units **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** Differentiate $Q_x$ with respect to $P_y$ (treating $P_x$ as constant).
- $\dfrac{dQ_x}{dP_y} = 0 - 0 + 3 = +3$ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Apply the Point Cross Elasticity Formula.
- $E_{xy} = \dfrac{dQ_x}{dP_y} \times \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x}$ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4:** Substitute the values.
- $E_{xy} = (+3) \times \dfrac{4}{52}$ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5:** Compute the final numerical value.
- $E_{xy} = \dfrac{12}{52} = \dfrac{3}{13} \approx 0.23$ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 6: Interpretation (2 Marks)**
Since $E_{xy} = +0.23$ is **positive**, commodities X and Y are **substitutes** — a 1% rise in the price of Y will increase the demand for X by approximately 0.23%. However, the low magnitude (0.23 < 1) indicates that they are **weak substitutes** — the substitution effect is not very strong.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Inverted Formula Trap (Most Frequent Error):** Students often write $E_{xy} = \dfrac{\Delta P_y}{\Delta Q_x} \times \dfrac{Q_x}{P_y}$. This is WRONG. The denominator MUST be the change in the price of the OTHER good (Y), and the numerator MUST be the change in the quantity of YOUR good (X). A 1-mark penalty per such error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Sign in Interpretation:** Many students compute E_c = −0.8 but conclude "they are substitutes" because they ignore the negative sign. **The sign is mandatory in the final answer.**
>
> 3. **Percentage vs. Arc Method Confusion:** If the question gives two distinct price points (e.g., "from ₹40 to ₹50"), use the **Arc Method**. If it says "initially" and "after a 10% rise," use the **Percentage Method**. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for using the wrong formula.
>
> 4. **Derivative Sign Error in Point Method:** When differentiating $Q_x = a - bP_x + cP_y$ with respect to $P_y$, the derivative is simply $+c$. Do not carry a negative sign. Cross elasticity is calculated directly, not subtracted.
>
> 5. **Unit Confusion in Arc Method:** The 2s in $\frac{P_1 + P_2}{2}$ cancel out with the denominator. Do NOT write the 2 in the final answer; it is a midpoint formula, not a literal "2 times" multiplier on top.
>
> 6. **Not Specifying Substitutes vs. Complements:** A numerical answer like "E_c = −0.8" without the words "Complementary Goods" will receive only **half the interpretation marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition to Memorize:** Cross Elasticity = % change in Q_x ÷ % change in P_y. Always ceteris paribus.
- **Three Numerical Formulas:** (1) Percentage / Proportionate, (2) Point / Geometric (uses derivative), (3) Arc / Midpoint (eliminates base bias).
- **Sign is the Story:** E_c > 0 → Substitutes; E_c < 0 → Complements; E_c = 0 → Unrelated.
- **Asymmetry Rule:** E_xy is not necessarily equal to E_yx. Always specify which elasticity you are calculating.
- **Perfect Substitutes vs. Perfect Complements:** E_c = ∞ (perfect substitutes); E_c = −∞ (perfect complements). You only need to know the concept; specific numbers are rare.
- **Income vs. Cross Elasticity:** Don't confuse! Income elasticity uses ΔY/Y in the denominator; Cross elasticity uses ΔP_y/P_y.
- **Own Price vs. Cross Price:** PED uses your own price; Cross elasticity uses the price of the OTHER good. Mixing these up is the #1 error.
- **Managerial Use-Cases to Remember:** Pricing strategy, product line cannibalisation, antitrust market definition, bundling complements, demand forecasting for joint products.
- **Demand Function Shortcut:** If the demand function is $Q_x = a - bP_x + cP_y$, then $E_{xy} = \dfrac{c \cdot P_y}{Q_x}$. Substitute the given prices directly.
- **Exam Tip:** Always write the formula *first*, then substitute, then compute, then interpret. Examiners reward stepwise working, even if the final answer is slightly off.
- **Units:** Cross Elasticity is **dimensionless** — never write "units" or "rupees" in the answer box.
- **Numerical Practice:** Practise at least 3 problems each of Percentage, Arc, and Point methods. The KTU 2024 paper will likely include **at least one 7-mark cross elasticity numerical**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advertisement Elasticity, Applications of the Concept of Elasticity, Demand Forecasting: An Overview of Forecasting Techniques, Statistical and Non-Statistical Methods, Analysis of Consumer Equilibrium under Cardinal Utility Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advertisement Elasticity, Applications of the Concept of Elasticity, Demand Forecasting: An Overview of Forecasting Techniques, Statistical and Non-Statistical Methods, Analysis of Consumer Equilibrium under Cardinal Utility Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Advertisement Elasticity

### Formal Academic Definition
**Advertisement Elasticity of Demand (also called Promotional Elasticity)** is the measure of responsiveness of the quantity demanded of a good to a change in advertising expenditure on that good, while holding all other determinants of demand constant (ceteris paribus).

$$E_A = \frac{\text{Percentage Change in Quantity Demanded}}{\text{Percentage Change in Advertising Expenditure}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Advertisement elasticity measures the sensitivity of sales/demand to changes in promotional spending. It is a key concept under "Sales Elasticity" categories in the elasticity taxonomy.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you own a small tea stall in Kerala. You spend ₹500 on flex banners one week and sell 100 cups. The next week, you spend ₹1,000 on the same banners and sell 130 cups. The demand "stretched" because of your advertising push. The ratio of how much demand moved versus how much you spent on ads is the **Advertisement Elasticity**. If a small increase in ad spend causes a big jump in sales, the product is advertisement-elastic. If extra ads barely move sales, the product is advertisement-inelastic.

### Key Characteristics
- **Range:** $E_A$ typically lies between $0$ and $+\infty$ for normal goods.
- **$E_A > 1$:** Advertisement-elastic (high responsiveness — ads work very well).
- **$E_A < 1$:** Advertisement-inelastic (low responsiveness — ads are not very effective).
- **$E_A = 0$:** Completely advertisement-inelastic (ads have no effect).
- **$E_A = \infty$:** Completely advertisement-elastic (any tiny ad spend produces infinite demand).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Advertisement elasticity is **always positive** because both advertising and demand move in the same direction (more ads usually means more sales). This is unlike price elasticity which is usually negative for the **Law of Demand**.

### Sales-Maximising Advertising Budget
The optimum level of advertising expenditure occurs where:

$$E_A = 1$$

This is the point where the **marginal rupee spent on advertising yields exactly one rupee in additional revenue** — a profit-maximising condition. KTU frequently tests this equilibrium condition.

---

## 2. Applications of the Concept of Elasticity

### Formal Definition
The concept of elasticity has wide applications in managerial decision-making. It guides firms in pricing, output determination, government in taxation policy, and labour markets in wage bargaining. The **degree of responsiveness** of one economic variable to another is at the heart of nearly every business decision.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of elasticity as a **sensitivity dial** on a mixing console. The volume of demand (output) reacts differently when you tweak the price knob, the income knob, or the advertising knob. A smart manager (DJ) knows which knob has the biggest effect on the crowd and adjusts accordingly.

### Major Applications (KTU High-Yield List)
1. **Pricing Decisions:** Firms use price elasticity to decide whether to raise or lower price. Inelastic goods → raise price; elastic goods → lower price to boost revenue.
2. **Government Tax Policy:** Taxes are imposed more heavily on goods with **inelastic demand** (like petrol, cigarettes) because consumption won't fall much.
3. **Output Determination:** Producers estimate sales response to price changes to plan production volumes.
4. **Wage Negotiations:** Trade unions use elasticity of demand for labour to bargain for higher wages.
5. **Foreign Exchange & Devaluation:** A country devalues its currency only when demand for its exports is **elastic**.
6. **Advertising Decisions:** Firms use advertisement elasticity to decide ad budgets.
7. **Consumer Surplus & Welfare Analysis:** Used in cost-benefit studies of public projects.
8. **Market Segmentation:** Cross-elasticity helps identify substitute and complementary goods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 2 explicitly lists "Applications of Elasticity" as a separate learning outcome. Students must connect each elasticity type to **at least one real business application**.

---

## 3. Demand Forecasting: An Overview of Forecasting Techniques

### Formal Definition
**Demand Forecasting** is the systematic process of estimating the future demand for a product or service over a specified period, using historical data, market analysis, and statistical or judgmental techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** "Demand forecasting is a predictive analysis of the likely demand for a product or service over a specific period in the future, enabling managers to plan production, inventory, marketing, and capacity."

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you run a textile shop in Thrissur. Last Onam, you sold 500 sarees. Should you stock 700 this Onam? Demand forecasting is the **educated guess, backed by data**, that helps you answer this. Without it, you either overstock (loss) or understock (lost sales).

### Two Broad Categories of Forecasting Techniques
| **Type** | **Nature** | **Basis** |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative / Non-Statistical / Judgmental** | Subjective, opinion-based | Expert opinion, surveys, market research |
| **Quantitative / Statistical** | Objective, math-based | Historical data, statistical models |

### Need for Demand Forecasting
- **Production planning** and inventory control
- **Sales budgeting** and financial planning
- **Manpower planning**
- **Pricing and promotional decisions**
- **Capacity expansion** and capital investment decisions

---

## 4. Statistical and Non-Statistical Methods of Demand Forecasting

### Formal Definitions

**Non-Statistical (Qualitative / Judgmental) Methods** — These rely on the **judgement, intuition, experience, and opinions** of experts, customers, and sales personnel rather than numerical data.

**Statistical (Quantitative) Methods** — These use **mathematical models and historical numerical data** to project future demand.

### Non-Statistical Methods
1. **Survey of Buyers / Consumers' Intentions Method** — Direct questionnaire to potential customers about their future purchase plans.
2. **Sales Force Opinion Method** — Salesmen submit estimates of sales in their territories; aggregated to firm-level forecast.
3. **Delphi Method** — A panel of experts answers questionnaires in multiple rounds anonymously; consensus is reached.
4. **Market Experiment / Test Marketing** — Product launched in a small representative market to gauge response.
5. **Expert Opinion / Jury Method** — Top executives collectively forecast.
6. **Historical Analogy** — Compare product launch with similar past products.

### Statistical Methods
1. **Trend Projection / Time Series Analysis** — Fitting a trend line to historical data (linear, exponential).
2. **Barometric Method / Leading Indicators** — Uses indicators that historically lead changes in demand (e.g., building permits lead housing demand).
3. **Regression Analysis** — Models demand as a function of one or more independent variables (e.g., price, income).
4. **Econometric Models** — Multi-equation models combining several economic relationships.
5. **Moving Averages & Exponential Smoothing** — Smoothing time series to remove random fluctuations.
6. **Correlation Analysis** — Measures relationship strength between demand and influencing factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain" a method, students must give a **definition + working steps + merits + limitations**. Examiner awards marks for all four.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of demand forecasting methods as **two paths up the same mountain**. The non-statistical path is a **scenic trail** — slow, scenic, based on talking to locals (experts/customers). The statistical path is a **cable car** — fast, mechanical, based on engineering (math). Both reach the top, but for different conditions.

---

## 5. Analysis of Consumer Equilibrium under Cardinal Utility Analysis

### Formal Definition
**Cardinal Utility Analysis** assumes that utility (satisfaction) can be measured in cardinal numbers (utils). A consumer is in **equilibrium** when they maximise total utility subject to their budget constraint, given the prices of goods and the marginal utility of money.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you have ₹100 to spend on two items: tea and biscuits. You keep trying different combinations. Eventually, you find the **one combination** where you cannot feel any happier by swapping one rupee between tea and biscuits. That point of **maximum personal satisfaction within your budget** is consumer equilibrium.

### The Two Equivalence Conditions (Cardinal Utility)
A rational consumer maximises utility when:

1. **Marginal Utility Condition (Equimarginal Principle):**
$$\frac{MU_x}{P_x} = \frac{MU_y}{P_y} = \cdots = MU_m$$

Where $MU_m$ is the **marginal utility of money**. This is the "every last rupee gives the same satisfaction" rule.

2. **Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility Condition:**
As more of a good is consumed, its $MU$ must be **falling** ($MU$ curve downward-sloping), ensuring the second-order condition for maximum (not minimum) is satisfied.

### The Budget Constraint
The consumer must also satisfy:

$$P_x \cdot Q_x + P_y \cdot Q_y + \cdots = M$$

Where $M$ is the total money income. Equilibrium combines this constraint with the equimarginal condition.

### Derivation of the Consumer's Demand Curve
Cardinal utility analysis leads to the **Law of Demand**: as price of good X falls, with constant $MU_m$, the consumer buys more of X to restore the equality $\frac{MU_x}{P_x} = MU_m$. Since $MU$ falls with quantity, $P_x$ must fall to bring the ratio back. This is the **demand curve** sloping downward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Module 2 closes with consumer equilibrium under **cardinal** utility. The **ordinal** (indifference curve) approach is typically in Module 3. Do not confuse the two.

### Limitations of Cardinal Utility
- Utility is **subjective** and cannot be objectively measured in utils.
- Assumes **constant marginal utility of money** — unrealistic.
- Ignores the **income effect** and **substitution effect** separately.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## A. Advertisement Elasticity — Deep Dive

### 1. Why Advertisement Elasticity Matters
In the modern consumer goods market (FMCG, smartphones, fashion), the **sales response curve** is heavily shaped by advertising. Managers need to know:
- How much to spend on ads?
- Is the brand advertisement-elastic or inelastic?
- Does advertising shift the demand curve or move along it?

### 2. The Two Effects of Advertising
| **Effect Type** | **Description** | **Curve Movement** |
|---|---|---|
| **Informative Effect** | Ads inform consumers about the product, shifting demand curve to the right | Demand curve **shifts** |
| **Persuasive Effect** | Ads change consumer tastes, also shifting the curve | Demand curve **shifts** |

> [!NOTE]
> Advertising **shifts** the demand curve (changes consumer preferences, awareness, perceived value). It does **not** cause movement **along** the curve (that is what price changes do).

### 3. The Sales Response Function
The relationship between sales ($S$) and advertising ($A$) is often modelled as:

$$S = a + b \cdot \ln(A) \quad \text{or} \quad S = a \cdot A^b$$

Where $b$ is the **advertisement elasticity coefficient**. The logarithmic and power forms reflect the **diminishing returns** to advertising — initially ads work well, then each extra rupee gives less lift.

### 4. Concept of Diminishing Returns to Advertising
Just like the law of diminishing marginal utility, advertising exhibits **diminishing marginal returns**:
- First ₹10,000 spent: huge sales lift.
- Next ₹10,000: smaller lift.
- Eventually: negligible additional sales.

This implies the **average advertising cost per rupee of sales keeps rising**, capping the optimal ad budget.

---

## B. Applications of Elasticity — Deep Dive

### 1. Pricing Decisions — The Total Revenue Test
| **Elasticity** | **Price Increase** | **Price Decrease** | **Total Revenue** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ (Elastic) | Sales fall sharply | Sales rise sharply | Move opposite to price |
| $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ (Inelastic) | Sales fall slightly | Sales rise slightly | Move same as price |
| $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ (Unit) | Sales fall proportionally | Sales rise proportionally | Revenue **constant** |

### 2. Government Tax Policy
- Imposing **excise duty** on goods with **inelastic demand** raises significant revenue with minimal consumption reduction → favoured for revenue generation.
- For **public health** (cigarettes, alcohol), governments may also tax inelastic goods to **discourage consumption** while maintaining tax revenue (Pigovian logic).

### 3. Foreign Exchange & Devaluation
The **Marshall-Lerner Condition** states that devaluation improves trade balance only if the sum of export and import demand elasticities (in absolute value) exceeds **1**:

$$\vert E_x \vert + \vert E_m \vert > 1$$

### 4. Labour Markets
- **Inelastic demand for labour** → unions can push for higher wages.
- **Elastic demand for labour** → wage hikes cause employment losses, weakening union bargaining power.

### 5. Joint Product Pricing
When products are jointly produced (e.g., petrol and diesel from crude), cross-elasticity guides pricing — raise price of the good whose demand is **inelastic** and lower the price of the substitute.

---

## C. Demand Forecasting — Deep Dive

### 1. The Forecasting Process (Step-by-Step)
1. **Define the objective** — what to forecast and at what level of detail.
2. **Collect data** — historical sales, market data, macro variables.
3. **Choose the method** — based on data availability, time horizon, cost.
4. **Run the forecast** — apply the model/method.
5. **Validate & refine** — compare forecast with actual, refine model.
6. **Use the forecast** — feed into planning decisions.

### 2. Time Horizon Classification
| **Horizon** | **Type** | **Typical Method** |
|---|---|---|
| **Short-term** (≤ 1 year) | Operational | Moving averages, exponential smoothing |
| **Medium-term** (1–5 years) | Tactical | Regression, econometric |
| **Long-term** (> 5 years) | Strategic | Delphi, scenario planning, market experiments |

### 3. Factors Affecting Demand Forecast Accuracy
- **Data quality** and availability
- **Market stability** and predictability
- **Competitor actions**
- **Macroeconomic conditions** (recession, inflation)
- **Product life cycle stage** (introduction vs. maturity)

---

## D. Non-Statistical Methods — Deep Dive

### 1. Survey of Buyers' Method
- **Steps:** Design questionnaire → sample selection → collect responses → tabulate → forecast.
- **Merits:** Direct, customer-focussed, useful for new products.
- **Limitations:** Expensive, customers may not know their own future plans, response bias.

### 2. Sales Force Composite Method
- **Steps:** Each salesperson estimates sales in their territory → sales manager aggregates and adjusts.
- **Merits:** Ground-level knowledge, salesperson accountability.
- **Limitations:** Salespeople may be biased (pessimistic for bonus reasons, optimistic for budget reasons).

### 3. Delphi Method
- **Steps:** Select expert panel → first round questionnaire → anonymous feedback → refined questionnaire → consensus.
- **Merits:** No groupthink, iterative refinement, no geographical constraints.
- **Limitations:** Slow (multiple rounds), expert selection bias.

### 4. Market Experiment / Test Marketing
- Launch product in a representative sample market, observe real response, project to full market.
- **Merits:** Real consumer behaviour data, robust.
- **Limitations:** Costly, competitors may copy or disrupt, time-consuming.

---

## E. Statistical Methods — Deep Dive

### 1. Time Series Analysis
A time series has four components:
- **Secular Trend (T)** — long-term movement
- **Seasonal Variation (S)** — regular short-term patterns
- **Cyclical Variation (C)** — business cycle effects
- **Irregular / Random Variation (I)** — unpredictable shocks

Decomposition models:
- **Additive:** $Y = T + S + C + I$
- **Multiplicative:** $Y = T \times S \times C \times I$

### 2. Linear Trend Equation (Most Tested in KTU)
$$Y = a + bX$$

Where:
- $Y$ = forecasted demand
- $X$ = time period
- $a$ = intercept
- $b$ = slope (trend per period)

Normal equations:
$$\sum Y = na + b \sum X$$
$$\sum XY = a \sum X + b \sum X^2$$

### 3. Moving Average Method
$$MA_n = \frac{Y_1 + Y_2 + \cdots + Y_n}{n}$$

Used to smooth fluctuations. A **3-year moving average** uses three years of data; **5-year moving average** uses five years.

### 4. Exponential Smoothing
$$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$

Where:
- $F_{t+1}$ = forecast for next period
- $Y_t$ = actual value in current period
- $F_t$ = forecast made for current period
- $\alpha$ = smoothing constant ($0 < \alpha < 1$)

Higher $\alpha$ → more weight on recent actuals → more responsive forecast.

### 5. Regression Analysis
$$Y = a + bX + e$$

Where $X$ is an independent variable (e.g., price, income, advertising) and $e$ is the error term. Used to quantify elasticity directly.

---

## F. Consumer Equilibrium — Deep Dive

### 1. Total Utility and Marginal Utility
- **Total Utility (TU):** Total satisfaction from consuming all units of a good.
- **Marginal Utility (MU):** Additional utility from consuming one more unit.
$$MU_n = TU_n - TU_{n-1}$$

### 2. The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility (LDMU)
As consumption of a good increases (holding consumption of other goods constant), the marginal utility derived from each additional unit **decreases**.

### 3. The Cardinal Utility Assumption
- Utility is measurable in cardinal numbers (1, 2, 3 utils).
- Utility is comparable between individuals.
- Marginal utility of money is constant.

### 4. Why Both Conditions?
The first condition ($\frac{MU_x}{P_x} = \frac{MU_y}{P_y}$) ensures **equimarginal utility per rupee** — a first-order necessary condition.

The second condition (LDMU/diminishing $MU$) ensures this is a **maximum**, not a minimum. If $MU$ were increasing, the consumer would be at a minimum, not a maximum.

### 5. Deriving the Demand Curve from Cardinal Utility

| **Step** | **Logic** |
|---|---|
| 1 | $P_x$ falls, $M$ and $MU_m$ constant. |
| 2 | $\frac{MU_x}{P_x}$ rises above $MU_m$ for current $Q_x$. |
| 3 | Consumer buys more $X$ to restore equilibrium. |
| 4 | $MU_x$ falls as $Q_x$ rises (LDMU). |
| 5 | $\frac{MU_x}{P_x}$ falls back to $MU_m$ at new equilibrium. |
| 6 | Plot $(Q_x, P_x)$ pairs → downward-sloping demand curve. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The slope of the demand curve arises from the LDMU. Without diminishing marginal utility, there would be no downward-sloping demand curve in cardinal theory.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **Advertisement Elasticity** | $E_A = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta A}$ | Always positive |
| **Price Elasticity** | $E_d = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$ | Usually negative; $\vert E_d \vert$ used in analysis |
| **Income Elasticity** | $E_y = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta M}$ | Normal good: $E_y > 0$; Inferior: $E_y < 0$ |
| **Cross Elasticity** | $E_{xy} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_x}{\%\Delta P_y}$ | Substitutes: $> 0$; Complements: $< 0$ |
| **Sales-Maximising Ad Budget** | $E_A = 1$ | Equilibrium condition |
| **Marshall-Lerner Condition** | $\vert E_x \vert + \vert E_m \vert > 1$ | Devaluation improves trade balance |
| **Linear Trend** | $Y = a + bX$ | $a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$, $b = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$ |
| **Moving Average** | $MA_n = \frac{\sum Y_i}{n}$ | Smooths fluctuations |
| **Exponential Smoothing** | $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| **Consumer Equilibrium (Cardinal)** | $\frac{MU_x}{P_x} = \frac{MU_y}{P_y} = MU_m$ | Subject to $P_x Q_x + P_y Q_y = M$ |
| **Budget Line** | $M = P_x Q_x + P_y Q_y$ | $P_x$ and $P_y$ are prices |
| **Marginal Utility** | $MU_n = TU_n - TU_{n-1}$ | Discrete case |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, every formula must be **derived or applied**, not just stated. The examiner checks the **logic** behind the formula. Use arrows and short justifications.

---

## Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

- **Advertisement Elasticity:** Used in marketing analytics dashboards (Google Ads, Meta Ads use CTR and conversion lift to compute elasticity).
- **Demand Forecasting:** Used in supply chain management for JIT (Just-in-Time) production planning.
- **Consumer Equilibrium Logic:** Underpins modern recommendation systems — matching utility to budget constraints.
- **Elasticity Concepts:** Used in dynamic pricing algorithms of Uber, Amazon, airlines.
- **Statistical Methods:** Time series ARIMA models forecast demand in retail, energy, and telecom sectors.
