> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oligopoly; In-depth Analysis of Profits in Perfectly Competitive and Monopolistic Markets; Exploring Sources of Monopoly Power and the Concept of the Multi-Product Firm; Investigating Various Models of Oligopoly and Understanding Market Failures; Introduction to Game Theory for a Strategic Understanding of Market Interactions. Practical/Assessment Component: Assessment of Demand Elasticity - Price

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Oligopoly — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oligopoly** is a market structure dominated by a **small number of large, interdependent firms** producing either **homogeneous** (pure oligopoly, e.g., steel, crude oil) or **differentiated** (differentiated oligopoly, e.g., automobiles, soft drinks) products, where each firm's pricing and output decisions **directly influence** and are **influenced by** the decisions of its rivals.

**Key identifying features:**
- **Few sellers** (typically 2 to 10 major players)
- **High barriers to entry** (capital, technology, brand equity)
- **Strategic interdependence** — the defining trait
- **Significant non-price competition** (advertising, branding, R&D)
- Price rigidity / kinked demand behaviour

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Picture three giant airlines — Air India, Emirates, and Singapore Airlines — operating the Delhi–Dubai corridor. If Emirates suddenly slashes fares by 20%, Air India cannot remain passive; it must *react*, perhaps by matching the cut or by bundling free meals. Each airline's profit depends on what the *others* do. This mutual watching-and-responding is the heart of oligopoly.

---

## 1.2 Profit Behaviour in Perfect Competition vs Monopoly

| Aspect | Perfect Competition | Monopoly |
|---|---|---|
| Number of firms | Very large | One |
| Price taker / maker | Price **taker** | Price **maker** |
| Demand curve faced | Horizontal (perfectly elastic) | Downward sloping (market demand) |
| Profit rule | **P = MR = MC** | **MR = MC**, then P read off demand curve |
| Long-run economic profit | **Zero** (normal profit only) | Can persist due to **strong barriers** |
| Allocative efficiency | Achieved (P = MC) | Not achieved (P > MC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core economic law:** Profit is maximised where **MR = MC**. The difference lies in *what the price is* once that quantity is chosen. A perfect competitor accepts the market price; a monopolist chooses the highest price the market will bear along the demand curve.

---

## 1.3 Sources of Monopoly Power

> [!NOTE]
> Monopoly power refers to a firm's ability to **set price above marginal cost** without losing all its customers. The strength of this power is measured by the **Lerner Index**: $L = (P - MC)/P$.

**Primary sources (KTU high-yield):**
1. **Economies of scale** — natural monopoly (e.g., railways, power grids)
2. **Patents and intellectual property** — 20-year legal exclusion (pharma)
3. **Control of essential inputs** — De Beers (diamonds), OPEC (oil)
4. **Government licences and regulations** — telecom spectrum, liquor
5. **Brand loyalty and switching costs** — Apple, Coca-Cola
6. **Network effects** — Windows, Facebook
7. **Strategic barriers** — aggressive capacity expansion, predatory pricing

---

## 1.4 The Multi-Product Firm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **multi-product firm** produces two or more distinct products, often sharing common inputs, production facilities, distribution channels, or branding. Examples: Hindustan Unilever (soaps, detergents, shampoos), Tata (cars, steel, software, hotels).

**Why this matters for managerial economics:**
- **Joint costs** must be allocated across products
- **Cross-subsidisation** may occur (loss-leader pricing)
- Pricing decisions for one product affect demand for others (**interdependence via demand**)
- Cost curves are *not* separable for individual products

---

## 1.5 Market Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Market failure** occurs when the free-market price mechanism fails to achieve **allocative efficiency** (P = MC) or **productive efficiency** (minimum AC), leading to welfare loss.

**Standard KTU categories:**
- **Externalities** (positive: education; negative: pollution)
- **Public goods** (non-rival, non-excludable — streetlights, national defence)
- **Information asymmetry** (used-car market — Akerlof's "lemons")
- **Monopoly power** (the very topic above!)
- **Common-pool resources** (overfishing, groundwater depletion)
- **Factor immobility** (labour cannot move freely across regions)

---

## 1.6 Introduction to Game Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Game theory** is the analytical framework for studying **strategic interactions** where each decision-maker's payoff depends on the choices of others. In oligopoly, every firm is a "player" and every pricing/output decision is a "move."

**Three building blocks:**
1. **Players** — the decision-makers (firms)
2. **Strategies** — the available actions (set high price, set low price)
3. **Payoffs** — profits resulting from the *combination* of all players' strategies

The most celebrated solution concept is the **Nash Equilibrium**: a state where no player can improve his payoff by unilaterally changing his strategy, given the others' choices.

---

## 1.7 Practical/Assessment Component: Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)

> [!NOTE]
> **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)** measures the **responsiveness** of quantity demanded to a change in the product's own price, holding all else constant.

**Formula preview:**

$$E_d = \frac{\%\ \Delta Q_d}{\%\ \Delta P}$$

**Interpretation thresholds:**
- $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ → **Elastic** (luxuries, substitutes available)
- $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ → **Unit elastic**
- $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ → **Inelastic** (necessities, no substitutes)

This metric is the **practical workhorse** in Module 4: it connects directly to monopoly power (Lerner Index) and to oligopolistic pricing models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Profit Maximisation — Universal First-Order Condition

A firm's total profit is the gap between total revenue and total cost:

$$\pi(Q) = TR(Q) - TC(Q)$$

The first-order condition (FOC) for an interior maximum:

$$\frac{d\pi}{dQ} = \frac{dTR}{dQ} - \frac{dTC}{dQ} = 0$$

This gives the universal rule:

$$MR = MC$$

The second-order condition (SOC) requires the profit function to be concave:

$$\frac{d^2\pi}{dQ^2} = \frac{dMR}{dQ} - \frac{dMC}{dQ} < 0$$

**Why this matters in every market structure:** The MR = MC rule never changes. What changes is *what MR looks like* and *what price* the firm can then charge.

---

## 2.2 Profits in Perfect Competition — Deep Mechanics

In perfect competition, the firm is a price taker. Demand faced by the firm is **perfectly elastic** at the market price $P^*$. Therefore:

$$MR = P$$

Substituting into the FOC:

$$P = MC$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **short run**, a perfectly competitive firm can earn **supernormal profit** (P > AC) or incur a **loss** (P < AC). In the **long run**, free entry and exit drive **P = min AC**, leaving firms with **normal profit** only — the **zero economic profit** condition.

**Long-run equilibrium signatures (write these down for KTU):**
- $P = MR = MC = AR = \min AC$
- The firm operates at the **minimum point of the long-run average cost curve** (productive efficiency)
- **Allocative efficiency** is achieved because the price consumers pay equals the marginal cost of production

---

## 2.3 Profits in Monopoly — Deep Mechanics

The monopolist faces the entire **downward-sloping market demand curve**. For a linear demand $P = a - bQ$:

$$TR = P \cdot Q = aQ - bQ^2$$

$$MR = \frac{dTR}{dQ} = a - 2bQ$$

Notice that the MR curve has **the same intercept but twice the slope** of the demand curve. The monopolist:

1. Sets **MR = MC** to find the profit-maximising quantity $Q^*$
2. Reads the **highest acceptable price** $P^*$ off the demand curve at $Q^*$
3. Computes profit: $\pi^* = (P^* - AC^*) \cdot Q^*$

Because barriers to entry shield the monopolist, **supernormal profits can persist indefinitely** — a stark contrast to perfect competition.

---

## 2.4 Measuring Monopoly Power — The Lerner Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Lerner Index** quantifies the degree of monopoly power as the proportional gap between price and marginal cost:
> $$L = \frac{P - MC}{P}, \quad 0 \le L \le 1$$

A brilliant result, derived in the next section, links $L$ directly to demand elasticity:

$$L = -\frac{1}{E_d}$$

**Implication:** The more *inelastic* the demand, the *greater* the monopoly power. This is why monopolists target goods with few substitutes (insulin, salt).

---

## 2.5 Multi-Product Firm — Allocation of Joint Costs

When products share common inputs, the **incremental cost** approach is preferred over full allocation:

- **Stand-alone cost** of product $i$: cost if produced *alone*
- **Incremental cost** of product $i$: extra cost incurred by adding product $i$ to the firm's existing product line
- **Common cost**: cost that remains unchanged regardless of product mix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU testable point:** Common costs should *not* be allocated arbitrarily to determine price. Prices should at least cover **incremental costs**, and total revenue across the product portfolio should cover total cost.

---

## 2.6 Oligopoly — The Models

Because of strategic interdependence, there is **no single model** of oligopoly. The leading ones (all KTU-favourite) are summarised below.

### 2.6.1 Cournot Duopoly (Quantity Competition)
- Firms choose **quantities simultaneously**
- Each firm treats the rival's output as given
- Solve via **reaction functions**

### 2.6.2 Bertrand Duopoly (Price Competition)
- Firms choose **prices simultaneously**
- With identical products and constant MC, equilibrium collapses to **P = MC** (Bertrand Paradox) — same outcome as perfect competition!

### 2.6.3 Stackelberg Model (Leader–Follower)
- One firm (the **leader**) commits to output *first*; the other (the **follower**) observes and reacts
- The leader enjoys a **first-mover advantage**

### 2.6.4 Kinked Demand Curve (Sweezy)
- Explains **price rigidity** in oligopoly
- Rivals match price *cuts* (asymmetric reaction)

### 2.6.5 Cartel / Collusion
- Firms coordinate to act like a monopolist (OPEC + oil)
- Maximum joint profit, but unstable due to cheating incentive

### 2.6.6 Price Leadership
- A dominant firm sets price; others follow (barometric or dominant-firm model)

---

## 2.7 Market Failure — Remedies

> [!NOTE]
> For each source of failure, KTU expects the corresponding policy remedy:

| Source of Failure | Standard Remedy |
|---|---|
| Negative externality (pollution) | **Pigouvian tax** equal to marginal external cost |
| Positive externality (vaccination) | **Pigouvian subsidy** |
| Public good | **Government provision** (free-rider problem defeats markets) |
| Monopoly | **Antitrust regulation** (MRTPC, Competition Commission of India) |
| Information asymmetry | **Mandatory disclosure, warranties, certification** |
| Common-pool resource | **Coase theorem**, tradable permits, regulation |

---

## 2.8 Game Theory — Key Concepts

**Normal-form (matrix) game** with two players A and B, each with two strategies (C = Cooperate, D = Defect). Payoffs shown as (A, B):

|  | B: C | B: D |
|---|---|---|
| **A: C** | 3, 3 | 0, 5 |
| **A: D** | 5, 0 | 1, 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Prisoner's Dilemma**. The unique **Nash Equilibrium** is (D, D) with payoffs (1,1) — *both defect*. Although mutual cooperation (3,3) Pareto-dominates this outcome, individual rationality drives players to the inferior equilibrium. This single game explains price wars, arms races, and advertising escalation in oligopolies.

**Dominant strategy:** A strategy that is *best* regardless of what the opponent does. In the matrix above, *Defect* is a dominant strategy for both players.

---

## 2.9 Price Elasticity of Demand — Full KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **practical assessment component** of Module 4 — expect at least one numerical problem.

**Point (arc) elasticity formula:**

$$E_d = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$$

**Mid-point (arc) elasticity — used when there are large changes:**

$$E_d = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{(Q_2 + Q_1)/2} \cdot \frac{(P_1 + P_2)/2}{P_2 - P_1}$$

**Total revenue test (extremely high-yield):**
- $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ → price cut **increases** TR
- $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ → TR is at maximum
- $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ → price cut **decreases** TR

**Other elasticities (mention in 2-mark questions if asked):**
- Income elasticity: $E_Y = (\Delta Q / Q) / (\Delta Y / Y)$
- Cross elasticity: $E_{xy} = (\Delta Q_x / Q_x) / (\Delta P_y / P_y)$

---

## 2.10 Complete KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Profit max universal rule | $MR = MC$ | Plus SOC: $dMR < dMC$ |
| 2 | Perfect competition long run | $P = MR = MC = \min AC$ | Zero economic profit |
| 3 | Monopoly price | Read $P^*$ off demand at $Q^*$ where $MR=MC$ | $P^* > MC$ |
| 4 | Lerner Index | $L = (P - MC)/P$ | $0 \le L \le 1$ |
| 5 | Lerner–elasticity link | $L = -1/E_d$ | Higher $\vert E_d \vert$ → less power |
| 6 | Cournot equilibrium (duopoly) | Solve reaction functions simultaneously | See derivation below |
| 7 | Stackelberg leader output | $Q_L = (a - c)/(2b)$ for linear case | First-mover advantage |
| 8 | Nash Equilibrium | No player benefits from unilateral deviation | Stable in strategies |
| 9 | PED point formula | $E_d = (\Delta Q/\Delta P) \cdot (P/Q)$ | Sign convention: usually negative |
| 10 | PED arc (mid-point) | $E_d = [(Q_2-Q_1)/(Q_2+Q_1)] \cdot [(P_2+P_1)/(P_2-P_1)]$ | Use for $\Delta P > 5\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table reading tip:** The symbol $\vert E_d \vert$ denotes absolute value. Note that in the table above the vertical bar appears as $\vert$ — this is intentional to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Telecom operators** (Jio, Airtel, Vi): use **Bertrand-style** price wars and **Lerner Index** to assess market power
- **Pharma companies**: **patent-based monopoly** for 20 years → use PED to set launch prices
- **Automobile manufacturers** (Tata, Maruti): **kinked demand** and **Stackelberg leadership**
- **Energy markets** (OPEC): **cartel behaviour** with game-theoretic instability
- **E-commerce platforms** (Amazon, Flipkart): **cross-elasticity** between product lines; multi-product pricing

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **mathematically exhaustive**. Every step from the previous section is now derived in full. Read line by line; do not skip.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Universal Profit-Maximisation Rule

**Step 1: Define profit function.**
Profit is revenue minus cost. Both are functions of output $Q$:

$$\pi(Q) = TR(Q) - TC(Q)$$

**Step 2: Take the first derivative with respect to $Q$.**
Apply the subtraction rule of differentiation:

$$\frac{d\pi}{dQ} = \frac{d(TR)}{dQ} - \frac{d(TC)}{dQ}$$

**Step 3: Recognise the marginal concepts.**
By definition, $MR = dTR/dQ$ and $MC = dTC/dQ$:

$$\frac{d\pi}{dQ} = MR(Q) - MC(Q)$$

**Step 4: Set the derivative to zero at the optimum.**
At an interior maximum, the slope of the profit function is zero:

$$MR(Q^*) - MC(Q^*) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad MR = MC$$

**Step 5: Apply the second-order condition (concavity).**
The second derivative must be negative to confirm a maximum:

$$\frac{d^2\pi}{dQ^2} = \frac{dMR}{dQ} - \frac{dMC}{dQ} < 0$$

**Interpretation:** Marginal revenue must be rising *more slowly* than marginal cost at the optimum. Economically, the profit curve must be *concave* (∩-shaped).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Profit in Monopoly (Linear Demand Case)

**Step 1: Specify the inverse demand function.**
A linear demand with intercept $a$ and slope $-b$ ($b > 0$):

$$P = a - bQ$$

**Step 2: Construct total revenue.**

$$TR = P \cdot Q = (a - bQ) \cdot Q = aQ - bQ^2$$

**Step 3: Compute marginal revenue (derivative of TR).**

$$MR = \frac{dTR}{dQ} = a - 2bQ$$

**Step 4: Specify marginal cost (assumed constant for simplicity).**
Let $MC = c$ (a constant).

**Step 5: Apply $MR = MC$.**

$$a - 2bQ^* = c \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q^* = \frac{a - c}{2b}$$

**Step 6: Read the monopoly price from the demand curve.**

$$P^* = a - b \cdot \frac{a - c}{2b} = a - \frac{a - c}{2} = \frac{a + c}{2}$$

**Step 7: Compute maximum profit.**
If average cost is also $c$ (constant MC = AC), then:

$$\pi^* = (P^* - c) \cdot Q^* = \left(\frac{a + c}{2} - c\right) \cdot \frac{a - c}{2b} = \frac{(a - c)^2}{4b}$$

**Interpretation:** The monopolist's profit grows with the square of the demand–cost wedge $(a-c)$ and shrinks as the demand slope $b$ steepens (more elastic demand).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Lerner Index and Its Link to Elasticity

**Step 1: Start from the optimality condition $MR = MC$.**
For any firm facing a downward-sloping demand, the marginal revenue can be written in terms of price and elasticity (a standard result in managerial economics):

$$MR = P \left(1 + \frac{1}{E_d}\right)$$

where $E_d < 0$ (price and quantity move oppositely).

**Step 2: Substitute into $MR = MC$.**

$$P \left(1 + \frac{1}{E_d}\right) = MC$$

**Step 3: Expand and rearrange.**

$$P + \frac{P}{E_d} = MC \quad\Rightarrow\quad P - MC = -\frac{P}{E_d}$$

**Step 4: Divide both sides by $P$.**

$$\frac{P - MC}{P} = -\frac{1}{E_d}$$

**Step 5: Recognise the left-hand side as the Lerner Index.**

$$L = -\frac{1}{E_d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** Monopoly power is *inversely* related to demand elasticity. A firm facing perfectly elastic demand ($E_d = -\infty$) has **zero** market power ($L = 0$). A firm facing very inelastic demand (e.g., $E_d = -2$) has $L = 0.5$, meaning price is **50%** above marginal cost.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cournot Duopoly Equilibrium

**Step 1: Set up the market.**
Two firms, 1 and 2, produce a homogeneous good with identical constant marginal cost $c$. The market price is set by the demand curve:

$$P = a - b(Q_1 + Q_2)$$

**Step 2: Write firm 1's profit.**

$$\pi_1 = [a - b(Q_1 + Q_2)] Q_1 - c Q_1 = aQ_1 - bQ_1^2 - bQ_1Q_2 - cQ_1$$

**Step 3: Differentiate $\pi_1$ with respect to $Q_1$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial \pi_1}{\partial Q_1} = a - 2bQ_1 - bQ_2 - c = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for $Q_1$ as a function of $Q_2$ — this is firm 1's reaction function.**

$$Q_1 = \frac{a - c}{2b} - \frac{Q_2}{2}$$

**Step 5: By symmetry, firm 2's reaction function is the same in $Q_1$:**

$$Q_2 = \frac{a - c}{2b} - \frac{Q_1}{2}$$

**Step 6: Solve the two equations simultaneously.** Substitute Step 5 into Step 4:

$$Q_1 = \frac{a - c}{2b} - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a - c}{2b} - \frac{Q_1}{2}\right)$$

$$Q_1 = \frac{a - c}{2b} - \frac{a - c}{4b} + \frac{Q_1}{4}$$

$$Q_1 - \frac{Q_1}{4} = \frac{a - c}{4b}$$

$$\frac{3Q_1}{4} = \frac{a - c}{4b} \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q_1^* = \frac{a - c}{3b}$$

**Step 7: By symmetry, $Q_2^* = (a-c)/(3b)$.**

**Step 8: Aggregate output and price.**

$$Q_{\text{total}}^* = \frac{2(a - c)}{3b}, \quad P^* = a - b \cdot \frac{2(a-c)}{3b} = \frac{a + 2c}{3}$$

**Step 9: Compare to monopoly.**
Monopoly output is $(a-c)/(2b)$ and price is $(a+c)/2$. Cournot price lies *between* competitive price ($c$) and monopoly price, illustrating that **duopoly is more competitive than monopoly but less than perfect competition**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stackelberg Leader Output

**Step 1: Re-use the setup from Cournot** — same demand, same costs.

**Step 2: The follower (firm 2) chooses $Q_2$ *after* observing $Q_1$.** Firm 2's reaction function is unchanged:

$$Q_2 = \frac{a - c}{2b} - \frac{Q_1}{2}$$

**Step 3: The leader (firm 1) anticipates this and substitutes firm 2's reaction into its own profit.**

$$\pi_1 = [a - b(Q_1 + Q_2)]Q_1 - cQ_1 \quad\text{with}\quad Q_2(Q_1) = \frac{a-c}{2b} - \frac{Q_1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and differentiate.**

$$\pi_1 = \left[a - bQ_1 - b\left(\frac{a-c}{2b} - \frac{Q_1}{2}\right) - c\right]Q_1 = \left[\frac{a - c}{2} - \frac{bQ_1}{2}\right]Q_1$$

$$\frac{d\pi_1}{dQ_1} = \frac{a - c}{2} - bQ_1 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q_1^{\text{Leader}} = \frac{a - c}{2b}$$

**Step 5: Find follower's output and price.**

$$Q_2^{\text{Follower}} = \frac{a-c}{2b} - \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{a-c}{2b} = \frac{a - c}{4b}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \frac{3(a - c)}{4b}, \quad P = a - b \cdot \frac{3(a-c)}{4b} = \frac{a + 3c}{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stackelberg first-mover advantage:** The leader produces *twice* the follower's output and earns a *higher* profit. The leader's output $(a-c)/(2b)$ equals the **monopoly output** — the leader fully captures the monopoly profit by being decisive.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Price Elasticity of Demand (Worked Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Practical / Assessment Component** required by KTU Module 4.

**Problem:** A retailer's data shows that when the price of a commodity rose from ₹40 to ₹50, monthly sales fell from 1,200 units to 1,000 units. Calculate the price elasticity of demand using (a) the point formula and (b) the arc (mid-point) formula. Comment on the result.

**Part (a): Point formula at the lower price $(P_1, Q_1)$.**

$$E_d = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{P_1}{Q_1}$$

$\Delta Q = 1000 - 1200 = -200$
$\Delta P = 50 - 40 = 10$
$P_1 = 40, Q_1 = 1200$

$$E_d = \frac{-200}{10} \cdot \frac{40}{1200} = -20 \cdot 0.0333 = -0.667$$

**Part (b): Arc (mid-point) formula.**

$$E_d = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{(Q_2 + Q_1)/2} \cdot \frac{(P_1 + P_2)/2}{P_2 - P_1}$$

$$E_d = \frac{1000 - 1200}{(1000 + 1200)/2} \cdot \frac{(40 + 50)/2}{50 - 40} = \frac{-200}{1100} \cdot \frac{45}{10}$$

$$E_d = (-0.1818)(4.5) = -0.818$$

**Comment:** $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ in both cases, so demand is **inelastic** in this price range. The retailer could *raise* price to **increase** total revenue (a classic TR-test result). The arc formula gives a more accurate average estimate because it uses the mid-points of $P$ and $Q$.

**Total revenue check:**
$TR_1 = 40 \times 1200 = 48{,}000$
$TR_2 = 50 \times 1000 = 50{,}000$
Indeed, revenue rose with the price increase, confirming inelastic demand. ✓

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Key Equation | Optimality | Welfare Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perfect competition | $P = MR = MC$ | $P = \min AC$ in LR | Allocatively efficient |
| Monopoly | $P > MC$ | $MR = MC$ | Welfare loss (deadweight) |
| Monopolistic comp. | $P > MC$ (in SR) | Tangent to demand in LR | Excess capacity |
| Oligopoly — Cournot | $Q_i = (a-c)/(3b)$ | Reaction functions | P between monopoly and competition |
| Oligopoly — Bertrand | $P = MC$ | Nash = competitive | No monopoly profit (paradox) |
| Oligopoly — Stackelberg | $Q_L = (a-c)/(2b)$ | Leader anticipates follower | Leader earns more than Cournot |
| Cartel | Acts as monopoly | Joint profit max | Unstable (cheating incentive) |

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]**
**Define oligopoly. List any four of its distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Oligopoly is a market structure in which a small number of large firms dominate the market, producing either homogeneous or differentiated products, and each firm's actions significantly affect the others.

**Four distinguishing features:**
1. **Few sellers** with a large share of total market output
2. **Mutual interdependence** in decision-making
3. **High barriers to entry** (technology, capital, brand)
4. **Predominance of non-price competition** (advertising, product differentiation, customer service)
5. (Any one of the following may also be cited:) Price rigidity, existence of price wars, possibility of collusion.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the formula for the Lerner Index. Explain its economic significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:** $L = (P - MC)/P = -1/E_d$, where $0 \le L \le 1$.

**Economic significance:**
- **Measures market power:** the larger the value of $L$, the greater the firm's ability to set price above marginal cost.
- **Inverse relation with elasticity:** A firm facing highly elastic demand (many substitutes) has very little market power; a firm with inelastic demand (necessity, no substitute) has substantial market power.
- **Policy tool:** Competition authorities use $L$ to identify firms with excessive pricing power and to design regulatory interventions.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 14-mark questions always carry **internal choice** (either–or). Two alternatives are provided below; each is divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Cournot model of duopoly** with the help of reaction functions. Derive the equilibrium output for each firm and the market price. Assume a linear demand $P = a - b(Q_1 + Q_2)$ and constant marginal cost $c$.
> **(b)** Discuss the **sources of monopoly power** and explain how the **Lerner Index** links market power to price elasticity of demand.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Setting.** Two firms produce an identical (homogeneous) product. They choose **output levels simultaneously**, treating the rival's output as given. Market price clears the market via the linear demand curve.

**Step 2: Profit function of firm 1.**

$$\pi_1 = P \cdot Q_1 - c \cdot Q_1 = [a - b(Q_1 + Q_2)]Q_1 - cQ_1$$

**Step 3: First-order condition.**

$$\frac{\partial \pi_1}{\partial Q_1} = a - 2bQ_1 - bQ_2 - c = 0$$

**Step 4: Reaction function of firm 1.**

$$Q_1 = \frac{a - c}{2b} - \frac{1}{2}Q_2$$

**Step 5: By symmetry**, the reaction function of firm 2 is $Q_2 = (a - c)/(2b) - Q_1/2$.

**Step 6: Simultaneous solution.** Substitute the two reaction functions into each other:

$$Q_1^* = \frac{a - c}{3b}, \quad Q_2^* = \frac{a - c}{3b}$$

**Step 7: Market price and profit.**

$$P^* = \frac{a + 2c}{3}, \quad \pi_1^* = \pi_2^* = \frac{(a - c)^2}{9b}$$

**Step 8: Comparative remarks (high-mark earning sentences).**
- Total Cournot output $(2(a-c))/(3b)$ is **greater** than monopoly output $(a-c)/(2b)$.
- Cournot price is **lower** than monopoly price but **higher** than competitive price ($c$).
- Cournot equilibrium is a **Nash equilibrium**: given the rival's output, no firm wishes to deviate.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Sources of monopoly power:**
1. **Economies of scale** — large fixed costs create a natural monopoly.
2. **Patents and IPR** — 20-year legal exclusion.
3. **Control of essential inputs** — single supplier of a critical resource.
4. **Government regulation** — licences, spectrum allocation.
5. **Brand loyalty and switching costs** — customers reluctant to change.
6. **Network effects** — value of the product rises with the number of users.

**Lerner Index derivation:**

Starting from the optimality condition $MR = MC$ and the identity $MR = P(1 + 1/E_d)$:

$$P\left(1 + \frac{1}{E_d}\right) = MC \quad\Rightarrow\quad P - MC = -\frac{P}{E_d} \quad\Rightarrow\quad L = \frac{P - MC}{P} = -\frac{1}{E_d}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $L = 0$ under perfect competition ($E_d = -\infty$).
- $L \rightarrow 1$ as demand becomes very inelastic (e.g., life-saving drugs).
- Higher $L$ signals greater welfare loss (deadweight loss) and justifies regulatory action.

---

### Question B (14 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Kinked Demand Curve model** of oligopoly. How does it explain price rigidity?
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Nash Equilibrium** using the **Prisoner's Dilemma** as an illustration. Why is this relevant to understanding oligopolistic behaviour?

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Assumptions of Sweezy's kinked demand model:**
1. An oligopolistic firm faces a **kinked demand curve** at the prevailing market price.
2. **Above the kink**, demand is **highly elastic** — if the firm raises price, rivals do *not* follow, so the firm loses substantial market share.
3. **Below the kink**, demand is **highly inelastic** — if the firm cuts price, rivals *match* the cut to avoid losing share, so the firm's gain is limited.
4. The kink point represents the current price-quantity combination $(P_0, Q_0)$.

**Consequence — Price Rigidity:**

The corresponding **marginal revenue curve has a vertical discontinuity** at $Q_0$ (it falls sharply). As long as the marginal cost curve intersects the MR curve *within this discontinuity*, the profit-maximising price remains at $P_0$. Even with cost changes, the firm does not adjust price — explaining the **stickiness of prices** observed in many oligopolistic industries (e.g., petrol, cement, automobiles).

**Critical evaluation (for top marks):**
- Explains price rigidity but does not explain *how* the original price was determined.
- Assumes asymmetric rival reactions (match cuts, ignore hikes), which is empirically questionable.
- The model is essentially *static*; it does not address dynamic competition.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Nash Equilibrium:**
A set of strategies $(s_1^*, s_2^*, \ldots, s_n^*)$ such that no player $i$ can improve his payoff by unilaterally deviating from $s_i^*$, given the strategies chosen by all other players.

Formally: $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \ge u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)$ for all $s_i$, for all $i$.

**Prisoner's Dilemma (PD) Illustration:**

Two suspects, A and B, are arrested. Each can either **Cooperate** (remain silent) or **Defect** (confess). Payoffs (years in prison, with **lower** = better):

|  | B: Cooperate | B: Defect |
|---|---|---|
| **A: Cooperate** | 1, 1 | 10, 0 |
| **A: Defect** | 0, 10 | 5, 5 |

**Best-response analysis:**
- If B cooperates, A is better off *defecting* (0 < 1).
- If B defects, A is better off *defecting* (5 < 10).
- *Defect* is a **dominant strategy** for A. By symmetry, *Defect* is dominant for B as well.

**Nash Equilibrium:** (Defect, Defect) → payoffs (5, 5).

**Why this matters for oligopoly:**
- The PD explains **price wars, advertising escalation, and R&D races** — situations where all firms would be better off *cooperating* (high prices, low ad spend, low R&D) but individual incentives drive them to compete aggressively.
- It justifies **cartel instability**: even when colluding on a high price, each member has an incentive to secretly undercut the others.
- It also motivates **repeated-game strategies** (tit-for-tat) that *can* sustain cooperation in the long run.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss zones in Module 4 questions:**
> 1. **Writing $MR = MC$ without the second-order condition.** Examiners deduct at least 1 mark for omitting the SOC. Always state $d^2\pi/dQ^2 < 0$.
> 2. **Confusing Cournot with Bertrand.** Cournot is **quantity** competition; Bertrand is **price** competition. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting that the Stackelberg leader anticipates the follower's reaction function.** Many students write the follower's reaction as if it were Cournot — the leader's output is *not* the Cournot quantity.
> 4. **Mishandling elasticity signs.** $E_d$ is *negative* (law of demand), but most KTU questions ask for the **absolute value** when classifying. State both $\vert E_d \vert$ and the numerical sign.
> 5. **Nash Equilibrium identification in matrix games.** A common error is to mark the *Pareto-optimal* outcome as the Nash equilibrium. Only the cell where *no player can improve unilaterally* is the NE.
> 6. **Treating multi-product common costs as separable.** They are *not* — failing to mention "joint/common costs" loses marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final glance before the exam. Memorise the starred items.**

- ⭐ **Oligopoly:** Few interdependent firms; high barriers; non-price competition dominant.
- ⭐ **Profit max rule:** $MR = MC$ **+** $d^2\pi/dQ^2 < 0$ (in **every** market structure).
- ⭐ **Perfect competition LR:** $P = MR = MC = \min AC$; zero economic profit; allocative efficiency.
- ⭐ **Monopoly price:** Read $P^*$ off demand at $Q^*$ where $MR = MC$; $P^* > MC$ always.
- ⭐ **Lerner Index:** $L = (P-MC)/P = -1/E_d$; higher inelasticity → greater market power.
- ⭐ **Cournot:** Simultaneous quantity choice; reaction functions; $Q_i^* = (a-c)/(3b)$; $P^* = (a+2c)/3$.
- ⭐ **Bertrand:** Simultaneous price choice; $P = MC$ (paradox — competitive outcome with 2 firms).
- ⭐ **Stackelberg:** Leader moves first; $Q_L = (a-c)/(2b) = $ monopoly output; first-mover advantage.
- ⭐ **Kinked demand:** Asymmetric reaction; explains **price rigidity**; discontinuous MR.
- ⭐ **Cartel:** Maximises joint profit like a monopolist; unstable due to cheating incentive.
- ⭐ **Market failure sources:** Externalities, public goods, information asymmetry, monopoly, common-pool resources.
- ⭐ **Pigouvian tax:** Set equal to **marginal external cost** to internalise a negative externality.
- ⭐ **Game theory basics:** Players, strategies, payoffs; Nash Equilibrium = no unilateral gain from deviation; dominant strategy = best regardless of rival's action.
- ⭐ **Prisoner's Dilemma:** Both defect → both worse off than mutual cooperation; explains price wars.
- ⭐ **PED point formula:** $E_d = (\Delta Q/\Delta P) \cdot (P/Q)$.
- ⭐ **PED arc formula:** Use mid-points of $P$ and $Q$.
- ⭐ **TR test:** Elastic → price cut raises TR; Inelastic → price cut lowers TR.
- ⭐ **Multi-product firm:** Common costs **not** allocated arbitrarily; incremental-cost pricing.

**Last-minute numerical template (memorise this pattern):**
1. Read off $\Delta Q$ and $\Delta P$.
2. Choose formula: point (small change) or arc (large change).
3. Compute $E_d$, then take absolute value.
4. Classify: $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ elastic, $= 1$ unit, $< 1$ inelastic.
5. Verify using total revenue comparison (KTU examiners love this confirmation step).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Income
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Income

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Income

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Income in Managerial Economics):**
> In managerial economics, **Income** refers to the flow of earnings that a firm, individual, or economy receives over a specific period of time. It is fundamentally distinct from **Wealth** (which is a stock concept). Income encompasses wages, rent, interest, profit, and the aggregate monetary value of goods and services produced in an economy (National Income).

The concept of income is analyzed at three distinct levels in KTU economics:

1. **Individual / Personal Level:** Money received by a person (wages, salaries, dividends, rent, interest).
2. **Firm / Business Level:** Revenue minus explicit costs (accounting profit) or revenue minus explicit + implicit costs (economic profit).
3. **National Level:** The total monetary value of all final goods and services produced within a country during a financial year — popularly known as **National Income**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Tap vs. The Tank":**
> Imagine you have a large overhead water tank (this is your **Wealth** — a stock). The tap below it constantly delivers water into your bucket (this is your **Income** — a flow). If the tap runs faster, your tank refills quickly; if it slows, the level drops. **Income is the rate at which wealth is generated per unit of time.**
> 
> Similarly, a country's **National Income** is the rate at which the nation's economic well-being is produced each year, while the cumulative **Wealth** is the stock of all assets it has built up over the decades.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Economic Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard National Income Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **GDP (Gross Domestic Product):** Market value of all final goods and services produced **within** the domestic territory in a year.
> - **GNP (Gross National Product):** Market value of all final goods and services produced by **residents** of a country (whether inside or outside the territory) in a year.
> - **NNP (Net National Product):** GNP minus depreciation (consumption of fixed capital).
> - **NDP (Net Domestic Product):** GDP minus depreciation.
> - **Per Capita Income:** National Income ÷ Total Population.
> - **Personal Income (PI):** Total income received by individuals before personal taxes.
> - **Disposable Income (DI):** Personal Income minus personal taxes (income available for spending or saving).

---

## 1.4 Stock vs. Flow — The Fundamental Distinction

| Concept | Type | Example | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wealth** | Stock | Total deposits in your bank | Rupees (₹) |
| **Income** | Flow | Monthly salary credited | Rupees per month (₹/month) |
| **Capital** | Stock | Machinery in a factory | Units of capital |
| **Investment** | Flow | New machines bought this year | Units per year |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Treating Income and Wealth as interchangeable. Always remember: **Income is per unit time; Wealth is at a point in time.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of National Income (The Circular Flow Logic)

National Income is the sum of all factor incomes generated in the production of goods and services. The four factors of production earn the following incomes:

1. **Land → Rent**
2. **Labour → Wages and Salaries**
3. **Capital → Interest**
4. **Entrepreneurship → Profit**

Therefore, at the national level:
$$\text{National Income} = \text{Rent} + \text{Wages} + \text{Interest} + \text{Profit}$$

This identity arises because every rupee spent on a product becomes a rupee of income for someone in the economy (a fundamental equilibrium condition of the **Product Market** and **Factor Market**).

---

## 2.2 The National Income Identity Chain (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the conversion chain from GDP to Personal Disposable Income. Each formula in the chain has a specific economic meaning:

| # | Metric | Formula | Economic Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GDP at Market Price (GDP_MP)** | $C + I + G + (X - M)$ | Aggregate demand for domestic output |
| 2 | **NDP at Market Price (NDP_MP)** | $\text{GDP}_{MP} - \text{Depreciation}$ | Net output after capital wear-and-tear |
| 3 | **NDP at Factor Cost (NDP_FC)** | $\text{NDP}_{MP} - \text{Net Indirect Taxes}$ | Income earned by factors of production |
| 4 | **NNP at Factor Cost (NNP_FC) = National Income** | $\text{NDP}_{FC} + \text{Net Factor Income from Abroad (NFIA)}$ | True national income (cost-based) |
| 5 | **Personal Income (PI)** | $\text{NNP}_{FC} - \text{Undistributed Corporate Profits} - \text{Corporate Taxes} - \text{Social Security Contributions} + \text{Transfer Payments}$ | Income actually received by households |
| 6 | **Personal Disposable Income (PDI)** | $\text{PI} - \text{Personal Taxes}$ | Income households can spend or save |

---

## 2.3 Methods of Measuring National Income (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of three methods. Each method is valid but yields the same total when correctly measured.

### **Method 1: Product / Output Method (Value Added Method)**
$$\text{GDP}_{MP} = \sum (\text{Value Added by each firm})$$
where Value Added = Sales − Intermediate Consumption (cost of raw materials, etc.)

> **Logic:** We sum only the **value added** at each stage to avoid the **problem of double counting**.

### **Method 2: Income Method (Factor Income Method)**
$$\text{NNP}_{FC} = \text{Compensation of Employees} + \text{Operating Surplus} + \text{Mixed Income} + \text{NFIA}$$
This sums all factor incomes (rent, wages, interest, profit) earned by residents.

### **Method 3: Expenditure Method**
$$\text{GDP}_{MP} = C + I + G + (X - M)$$
where C = Private Final Consumption, I = Investment, G = Government Spending, X = Exports, M = Imports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three methods?** Each captures the same economy from a different angle: **production**, **distribution**, and **spending**. Theoretically, the three should yield identical results.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

Understanding national income is critical for:
- **Macroeconomic Policy Making:** Government uses GDP growth to frame fiscal and monetary policies.
- **Investment Decisions:** Firms analyze per capita income to identify market size and consumer purchasing power.
- **Inflation Targeting:** RBI uses nominal vs. real income gap to design interest rate policy.
- **International Comparisons:** World Bank uses **GNI (Gross National Income)** to rank economies.
- **Engineering Project Viability:** When engineers assess demand for a new product, national income statistics determine the addressable market.

---

## 2.5 Real vs. Nominal Income (Inflation Adjustment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{Real Income} = \frac{\text{Nominal Income}}{\text{GDP Deflator}} \times 100$$
> 
> Real income strips out the effect of inflation, showing the **actual purchasing power** of money earned. KTU frequently tests the distinction between Money (Nominal) Income and Real Income.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From GDP_MP to NNP_FC (The National Income Chain)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A KTU-style problem gives us raw data — we need to derive National Income step-by-step.

**Given Data (Hypothetical KTU 2024 Question Setup):**
- Private Final Consumption Expenditure (C) = ₹ 800 crores
- Gross Domestic Capital Formation (I) = ₹ 300 crores
- Government Final Consumption Expenditure (G) = ₹ 200 crores
- Exports (X) = ₹ 150 crores
- Imports (M) = ₹ 100 crores
- Depreciation = ₹ 70 crores
- Net Indirect Taxes = ₹ 50 crores
- Net Factor Income from Abroad (NFIA) = ₹ 20 crores
- Undistributed Corporate Profits = ₹ 30 crores
- Corporate Taxes = ₹ 40 crores
- Social Security Contributions = ₹ 20 crores
- Transfer Payments = ₹ 60 crores
- Personal Taxes = ₹ 50 crores

**Step 1 — Calculate GDP at Market Price (Expenditure Method):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GDP}_{MP} &= C + I + G + (X - M) \\
&= 800 + 300 + 200 + (150 - 100) \\
&= 800 + 300 + 200 + 50 \\
&= 1350 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to NDP at Market Price (subtract depreciation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NDP}_{MP} &= \text{GDP}_{MP} - \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 1350 - 70 \\
&= 1280 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to NDP at Factor Cost (subtract Net Indirect Taxes):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NDP}_{FC} &= \text{NDP}_{MP} - \text{Net Indirect Taxes} \\
&= 1280 - 50 \\
&= 1230 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to NNP at Factor Cost (add NFIA → this is NATIONAL INCOME):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NNP}_{FC} \ (\text{National Income}) &= \text{NDP}_{FC} + \text{NFIA} \\
&= 1230 + 20 \\
&= 1250 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Derive Personal Income (PI):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PI} &= \text{NNP}_{FC} - \text{Undistributed Profits} - \text{Corporate Taxes} - \text{Social Security} + \text{Transfers} \\
&= 1250 - 30 - 40 - 20 + 60 \\
&= 1220 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Derive Personal Disposable Income (PDI):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PDI} &= \text{PI} - \text{Personal Taxes} \\
&= 1220 - 50 \\
&= 1170 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Value Added Method (Anti-Double Counting)

> **Scenario:** A timber merchant sells wood to a furniture maker for ₹ 200, who sells a chair to a customer for ₹ 350. The customer's total payment is ₹ 350. Without adjustment, summing both transactions would falsely give ₹ 550.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Value Added by Timber Merchant} &= 200 - 0 = 200 \\
\text{Value Added by Furniture Maker} &= 350 - 200 = 150 \\
\text{Total Value Added (GDP)} &= 200 + 150 = 350
\end{aligned}$$

This correctly equals the final sale price — confirming the **Value Added = Final Product Price** identity.

---

## 3.3 Real vs. Nominal Income Derivation

> **Problem:** Last year a worker earned ₹ 50,000 nominal. The price index rose from 100 to 120 this year. What is the percentage change in real income?

**Step 1 — Compute Real Income of Last Year:**

$$\text{Real Income} = \frac{50000}{100} \times 100 = 50000$$

**Step 2 — Apply New Price Index to Convert:**

$$\text{Real Income (This Year Equivalent)} = \frac{50000}{120} \times 100 = 41666.67$$

**Step 3 — Compute Percentage Change:**

$$\% \Delta = \frac{41666.67 - 50000}{50000} \times 100 = -16.67\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Many KTU students forget to divide by the deflator and instead multiply. **Always divide by the index** (base year 100). The result is a *loss* in purchasing power even though nominal income did not change.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: KTU Frequently Confused Pairs

| Frequently Confused | Correct Distinction | Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| GDP vs. GNP | GDP = inside territory; GNP = residents anywhere | **D**omestic = **D**omain; **N**ational = **N**atives |
| NNP vs. NDP | NN*P* = N + NFIA; ND*P* = no NFIA | Net means minus Depreciation |
| PI vs. PDI | PI = before personal tax; PDI = after | **D**isposable = **D**educted |
| Nominal vs. Real | Nominal = current prices; Real = constant prices | Real = adjusted for **R**eal purchasing power |
| Factor Cost vs. Market Price | FC excludes indirect taxes; MP includes them | **F**actor Cost = **F**actory gate price |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: National Income Identity Chain (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GDP at Market Price GDP_MP"] --> B["Minus Depreciation"]
    B --> C["NDP at Market Price NDP_MP"]
    C --> D["Minus Net Indirect Taxes"]
    D --> E["NDP at Factor Cost NDP_FC"]
    E --> F["Plus Net Factor Income from Abroad NFIA"]
    F --> G["NNP at Factor Cost NATIONAL INCOME NNP_FC"]
    G --> H["Minus Undistributed Corporate Profits"]
    G --> I["Minus Corporate Taxes"]
    G --> J["Minus Social Security Contributions"]
    K["Plus Transfer Payments"] --> L["Personal Income PI"]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    L --> M["Minus Personal Taxes"]
    M --> N["Personal Disposable Income PDI"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Circular Flow of Income (Two-Sector Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["FIRMS SECTOR Producers"] -->|"Goods and Services Y = C + I"| H1["HOUSEHOLDS SECTOR Owners of Factors"]
    H1 -->|"Factor Services Land Labour Capital"| F1
    F1 -->|"Factor Incomes Rent Wages Interest Profit"| H1
    H1 -->|"Consumption Expenditure C"| F1
    F1 -->|"Investment Expenditure I"| H1
```

**Interpretation:** The upper loop shows the **real flow** (goods and factor services), and the lower loop shows the **money flow** (income and expenditure). In equilibrium, the two flows are equal in value.

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Three Methods of Measuring National Income

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["National Income Measurement"] --> B["Method 1 Product or Value Added Method"]
    A --> C["Method 2 Income or Factor Income Method"]
    A --> D["Method 3 Expenditure Method"]
    B --> B1["Sum of Value Added by all Producers"]
    C --> C1["Sum of Rent Wages Interest Profit"]
    D --> D1["C plus I plus G plus X minus M"]
    B1 --> E["Equals NATIONAL INCOME"]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Stock vs. Flow (Income vs. Wealth)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INCOME Flow per unit time"] --> A1["Salary per month"]
    A --> A2["Rent per year"]
    A --> A3["Interest per quarter"]
    B["WEALTH Stock at a point in time"] --> B1["Bank balance today"]
    B --> B2["Property value today"]
    B --> B3["Total assets today"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Distinguish between Nominal Income and Real Income. Why is Real Income considered a better indicator of economic welfare?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Nominal Income | Real Income |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Income measured in current (prevailing) money terms | Income adjusted for changes in price level (inflation) |
| **Price Basis** | Current year prices | Constant (base year) prices |
| **Formula** | Given directly | $\frac{\text{Nominal Income}}{\text{Price Index}} \times 100$ |
| **Reflects** | Money earned | Actual purchasing power |

> Real Income is a **better indicator of welfare** because it shows the actual quantity of goods and services a person can buy. A rise in nominal income accompanied by higher inflation does not improve welfare — only real income growth does. Hence real income is used for international comparisons and policy framing.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Personal Disposable Income". How is it different from Personal Income?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Personal Income (PI)** is the total income **actually received** by individuals from all sources before payment of personal (direct) taxes. It is derived by adjusting National Income for retained corporate earnings, corporate taxes, social security contributions, and adding transfer payments.
- **Personal Disposable Income (PDI)** is what remains of Personal Income **after** deducting personal taxes. It is the amount households are free to either **spend** (on consumption) or **save**.

$$\text{PDI} = \text{PI} - \text{Personal Taxes}$$

> The critical difference is the **deduction of personal taxes**. PI is gross-of-tax; PDI is net-of-tax. PDI is the relevant variable for consumption and saving behavior in the economy.

---

## **Part B Question — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — National Income Problems:**
> - **Do NOT** skip the conversion from Market Price to Factor Cost (you lose 2 marks).
> - **Do NOT** forget NFIA when computing National Income from NDP (you lose 1 mark).
> - **Do NOT** treat Depreciation as a recurring operating cost — it is a stock reduction (you lose 1 mark).
> - **Show all 6 steps** explicitly — partial marking is awarded step-wise, not as a lump sum.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**The following data relate to the economy of a country for the year 2023-24:**

| Item | ₹ in crores |
|---|---|
| Private Final Consumption Expenditure (C) | 1000 |
| Gross Domestic Capital Formation (I) | 400 |
| Government Final Consumption Expenditure (G) | 250 |
| Exports (X) | 200 |
| Imports (M) | 150 |
| Depreciation | 100 |
| Net Indirect Taxes | 80 |
| Net Factor Income from Abroad (NFIA) | 30 |
| Undistributed Corporate Profits | 50 |
| Corporate Taxes | 60 |
| Social Security Contributions | 30 |
| Transfer Payments | 80 |
| Personal Taxes | 70 |

**Calculate:**
**(a)** National Income (NNP at Factor Cost) — **7 marks**
**(b)** Personal Disposable Income (PDI) — **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — National Income (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate GDP at Market Price using the Expenditure Method.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GDP}_{MP} &= C + I + G + (X - M) \\
&= 1000 + 400 + 250 + (200 - 150) \\
&= 1000 + 400 + 250 + 50 \\
&= 1700 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Subtract Depreciation to obtain NDP at Market Price.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NDP}_{MP} &= \text{GDP}_{MP} - \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 1700 - 100 \\
&= 1600 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract Net Indirect Taxes to obtain NDP at Factor Cost.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NDP}_{FC} &= \text{NDP}_{MP} - \text{Net Indirect Taxes} \\
&= 1600 - 80 \\
&= 1520 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Add NFIA to obtain National Income (NNP at Factor Cost).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NNP}_{FC} \ (\text{National Income}) &= \text{NDP}_{FC} + \text{NFIA} \\
&= 1520 + 30 \\
&= \boxed{1550 \text{ crores}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Part (b) — Personal Disposable Income (7 marks):**

**Step 5:** Calculate Personal Income by adjusting National Income.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PI} &= \text{NNP}_{FC} - \text{Undistributed Profits} - \text{Corporate Taxes} - \text{Social Security} + \text{Transfers} \\
&= 1550 - 50 - 60 - 30 + 80 \\
&= 1490 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Subtract Personal Taxes to obtain Personal Disposable Income.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PDI} &= \text{PI} - \text{Personal Taxes} \\
&= 1490 - 70 \\
&= \boxed{1420 \text{ crores}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **National Income = ₹ 1550 crores**
- **Personal Disposable Income = ₹ 1420 crores**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the three methods of measuring National Income. Discuss the difficulties encountered in measuring National Income of a developing country like India. — **7 marks**

**(b)** From the following data, calculate **(i)** GDP at Market Price, **(ii)** NDP at Factor Cost, and **(iii)** Personal Income. — **7 marks**

| Item | ₹ in crores |
|---|---|
| Compensation of Employees | 600 |
| Operating Surplus | 350 |
| Mixed Income of Self-Employed | 100 |
| Depreciation | 80 |
| Net Indirect Taxes | 60 |
| Net Factor Income from Abroad | 20 |
| Undistributed Corporate Profits | 40 |
| Corporate Profit Tax | 50 |
| Social Security Contributions | 25 |
| Transfer Payments | 70 |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Three Methods & Difficulties (7 marks):**

**Three Methods:**

1. **Product / Value Added Method:** The value of all final goods and services produced in an economy during a year is summed up. Intermediate goods are excluded to avoid **double counting**. Value Added = Sales − Intermediate Consumption.

2. **Income Method:** The total of all factor incomes (rent, wages, interest, profit) earned by residents of a country in a year is aggregated. This includes compensation of employees, operating surplus, and mixed income.

3. **Expenditure Method:** Total expenditure incurred by all economic agents on final goods and services is summed: $\text{GDP} = C + I + G + (X - M)$.

**Difficulties in Developing Countries like India:**

- **Existence of Non-Monetized Sector:** A large share of production (e.g., subsistence farming, household work) is never sold in markets and goes unrecorded.
- **Inadequate Statistical Data:** Reliable data on output, employment, and prices is often missing or outdated.
- **Informal Economy:** Hawkers, domestic help, and small artisans often evade official records.
- **Double Counting Risk:** In multi-stage production, separating intermediate from final goods is difficult.
- **Valuation of Services:** Many services (government, household) are hard to value at market prices.
- **Underemployment and Barter:** In rural areas, barter transactions are common, complicating monetary valuation.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) GDP at Market Price:**

By the Income Method, the sum of factor incomes gives us NDP at Factor Cost; we then reverse-engineer to obtain GDP at MP.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NDP}_{FC} &= \text{Compensation of Employees} + \text{Operating Surplus} + \text{Mixed Income} \\
&= 600 + 350 + 100 \\
&= 1050 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NDP}_{MP} &= \text{NDP}_{FC} + \text{Net Indirect Taxes} \\
&= 1050 + 60 \\
&= 1110 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GDP}_{MP} &= \text{NDP}_{MP} + \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 1110 + 80 \\
&= \boxed{1190 \text{ crores}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) NDP at Factor Cost:** Already computed above: **₹ 1050 crores**

**(iii) Personal Income:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PI} &= \text{NNP}_{FC} - \text{Undistributed Profits} - \text{Corporate Taxes} - \text{Social Security} + \text{Transfers} \\
\text{NNP}_{FC} &= \text{NDP}_{FC} + \text{NFIA} = 1050 + 20 = 1070 \\
\text{PI} &= 1070 - 40 - 50 - 25 + 70 \\
&= 1025 \text{ crores}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Personal Income} = \boxed{1025 \text{ crores}}$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullets):**
> 
> - **Income is a Flow**; Wealth is a Stock. Never confuse them in exam answers.
> - **GDP_MP** = $C + I + G + (X - M)$ — the master formula for the Expenditure Method.
> - **NNP_FC = National Income** is the gold standard measure. It = NDP_FC + NFIA.
> - **Conversion Rule:** Market Price → Factor Cost requires **subtracting Net Indirect Taxes**.
> - **Net vs. Gross:** Net always means **subtracting Depreciation** (consumption of fixed capital).
> - **Personal Income adjustments:** Start from NNP_FC → deduct corporate retentions & taxes → add transfers.
> - **PDI = PI − Personal Taxes** — this is what households can actually spend or save.
> - **Real Income = Nominal Income ÷ Price Index × 100** — divides, never multiplies.
> - **Three Methods of NI Measurement** (Product, Income, Expenditure) all yield the same total in theory.
> - **Double Counting** is avoided by using the **Value Added** method, not by summing gross sales.
> - **India's challenge:** large informal/unorganized sector means actual NI is underestimated.
> - **GNP includes NFIA; GDP does not.** Domestic vs. National — remember the boundary.
> - **Factor Cost** = cost to the producer (factory gate); **Market Price** = cost to the buyer (includes taxes, subsidies).
> - **Per Capita Income** is a misleading welfare indicator — it ignores income distribution inequality.
> - For KTU 14-mark problems, **always show all 6 steps** of the GDP→NNP→PI→PDI chain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cross Elasticity of Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cross Elasticity of Demand (XED)** — also known as *Cross-Price Elasticity of Demand* — is a measure of the **responsiveness (sensitivity)** of the quantity demanded of one good to a change in the price of *another* related good, while holding all other factors (income, tastes, prices of other goods) constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Cross Elasticity of Demand measures the percentage change in quantity demanded of commodity X as a result of a percentage change in the price of commodity Y, other things being equal."*

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of **percentage change in quantity demanded of good X** to the **percentage change in price of good Y**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea & Coffee" Intuition

Imagine you are a tea-drinker who occasionally switches to coffee when prices fluctuate.

- ☕ **Scenario A:** The price of *Coffee* rises sharply. You think, *"Coffee is now too expensive, let me drink more tea."* → Your demand for **Tea increases**.
- ☕ **Scenario B:** The price of *Sugar* rises. You cut back on both tea and coffee equally. → Your demand for **Tea decreases**, but the trigger is the sugar price, not the tea price.

In Scenario A, **Tea and Coffee are *substitute goods*** — Cross Elasticity is **positive**.
In Scenario B, **Tea and Sugar are *complementary goods*** — Cross Elasticity is **negative**.

This real-world behavioral reaction — *how a price change in one product shifts demand for a related product* — is exactly what Cross Elasticity of Demand quantifies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why XED matters in Managerial Economics:**
> 1. **Pricing Strategy** — Firms use XED to set prices without losing customers to competitors.
> 2. **Product Line Decisions** — Helps decide whether to bundle, differentiate, or segment products.
> 3. **Market Definition** — Used by antitrust regulators to identify if two products belong to the same market.
> 4. **Demand Forecasting** — Predicts spillover effects of price changes across product portfolios.

---

## 1.3 Standard Sign Convention & Classification

| Relationship Type | Sign of XED | Magnitude | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Substitute Goods** | Positive (+) | XED > 0 | Tea & Coffee, Pepsi & Coca-Cola |
| **Complementary Goods** | Negative (−) | XED < 0 | Cars & Petrol, Printers & Cartridges |
| **Unrelated Goods** | Zero (0) | XED = 0 | Apples & Shoes, Salt & Mobile Phones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong vs. Weak Relationship:**
> - **Strong Substitutes:** XED > 1 (e.g., two brands of the same product)
> - **Weak Substitutes:** 0 < XED < 1
> - **Strong Complements:** XED < −1 (e.g., smartphones and earphones)
> - **Weak Complements:** −1 < XED < 0
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Cross Elasticity

**Step 1 — Identify the Two Goods in Question**
- Good X (the *affected* good — whose demand we measure)
- Good Y (the *trigger* good — whose price changes)

**Step 2 — Measure the Percentage Change in Quantity Demanded of Good X**
This is the *response variable*.

**Step 3 — Measure the Percentage Change in Price of Good Y**
This is the *causal variable* (the stimulus).

**Step 4 — Compute the Ratio**
Divide the response by the stimulus. The result is a dimensionless number (a pure ratio with **no units**).

**Step 5 — Interpret the Sign and Magnitude**
- **Positive sign** → Goods are *substitutes*.
- **Negative sign** → Goods are *complements*.
- **Zero** → Goods are *unrelated* (independent demand).

---

## 2.2 The Three Methods of Measurement

### Method 1: Percentage Method (Arc Method)
Used when there is a *finite* change in price (between two price points).

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\%\ \text{Change in Quantity Demanded of X}}{\%\ \text{Change in Price of Y}} = \frac{\frac{\Delta Q_x}{Q_x} \times 100}{\frac{\Delta P_y}{P_y} \times 100} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{Q_x} \div \frac{\Delta P_y}{P_y}$$

Simplifying:

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$$

### Method 2: Geometric Method (Point Method)
Used at a *specific point* on the demand curve.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{P_y}{Q_x} \times \frac{dQ_x}{dP_y}$$

(Where $\frac{dQ_x}{dP_y}$ is the partial derivative of the demand function of X with respect to the price of Y.)

### Method 3: Total Outlay / Expenditure Method
Used to *interpret* the sign of XED based on how total revenue moves when price of Y changes.

| Change in P_y | Change in Total Expenditure on X | Type of Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| Rise | Falls | Substitutes (XED > 0) |
| Rise | Rises | Complements (XED < 0) |
| Rise | No change | Unrelated (XED = 0) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-ready. Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Method / Use Case** | **Key Feature** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x}$ | Arc / Percentage Method | Finite change, between two points |
| 2 | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x} \times \dfrac{dQ_x}{dP_y}$ | Point / Geometric Method | Infinitesimal change at a point |
| 3 | $E_{xy} = \dfrac{P_y}{Q_x} \times \dfrac{1}{Slope_{xy}}$ | Slope-based shortcut | $Slope_{xy} = \dfrac{\Delta P_y}{\Delta Q_x}$ |
| 4 | $E_{xy} > 0$ | Substitution | Direct relationship |
| 5 | $E_{xy} < 0$ | Complementarity | Inverse relationship |
| 6 | $E_{xy} = 0$ | Independence | No demand linkage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner's Note on Units:**
> Cross Elasticity is a **dimensionless pure number** — it carries **no units** (e.g., not "per rupee" or "per percent"). Always present your final answer as a pure numeric value. Writing "2.5 per unit" will be marked wrong in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Applications

- **Automobile Industry:** Pricing of EVs vs. petrol cars — manufacturers track XED to forecast EV demand when fuel prices fluctuate.
- **Telecom Sector:** Tariff changes of Jio vs. Airtel — cross elasticity drives *predatory pricing wars*.
- **Software Industry:** Pricing of printers (low margin) vs. cartridges (high margin) — classic negative XED bundling strategy (Razor & Blade model).
- **Agriculture Policy:** MSP (Minimum Support Price) changes for one crop affect sowing decisions for substitute crops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Arc Method Formula

We start from the fundamental definition:

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\text{Percentage Change in } Q_x}{\text{Percentage Change in } P_y}$$

**Step 1:** Write the numerator (percentage change in quantity demanded of X).

$$\%\Delta Q_x = \frac{Q_{x2} - Q_{x1}}{Q_{x1}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Write the denominator (percentage change in price of Y).

$$\%\Delta P_y = \frac{P_{y2} - P_{y1}}{P_{y1}} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio by dividing Step 1 by Step 2.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\frac{Q_{x2} - Q_{x1}}{Q_{x1}} \times 100}{\frac{P_{y2} - P_{y1}}{P_{y1}} \times 100}$$

**Step 4:** The factor of 100 cancels from numerator and denominator.

$$E_{xy} = \frac{Q_{x2} - Q_{x1}}{Q_{x1}} \times \frac{P_{y1}}{P_{y2} - P_{y1}}$$

**Step 5:** Use $\Delta Q_x = Q_{x2} - Q_{x1}$ and $\Delta P_y = P_{y2} - P_{y1}$ for compactness.

$$\boxed{E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{Q_{x1}} \times \frac{P_{y1}}{\Delta P_y}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In the Arc Method, the *base values* ($Q_{x1}$ and $P_{y1}$) refer to the **initial** values before the price change. Do not use average values unless the question explicitly says so.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example 1 (Substitutes)

**Problem:** The price of Coffee (Good Y) rises from ₹200 to ₹250 per kg. As a result, the quantity demanded of Tea (Good X) increases from 100 kg to 130 kg per month. Calculate the Cross Elasticity of Demand of Tea with respect to the price of Coffee, and interpret the result.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract the given data.**

$$P_{y1} = 200,\quad P_{y2} = 250,\quad Q_{x1} = 100,\quad Q_{x2} = 130$$

**Step 2 — Compute the changes.**

$$\Delta P_y = P_{y2} - P_{y1} = 250 - 200 = 50$$

$$\Delta Q_x = Q_{x2} - Q_{x1} = 130 - 100 = 30$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Arc Method formula.**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{Q_{x1}} \times \frac{P_{y1}}{\Delta P_y}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{30}{100} \times \frac{200}{50}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify fraction by fraction.**

$$E_{xy} = 0.30 \times 4.0$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{E_{xy} = +1.2}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
- The sign is **positive** → Tea and Coffee are **substitute goods**.
- The magnitude is **1.2 > 1** → They are **strong substitutes**.
- *Business insight:* A 1% rise in coffee price leads to a 1.2% rise in tea demand.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example 2 (Complements)

**Problem:** The price of Petrol (Good Y) rises from ₹100 to ₹120 per litre. The demand for Cars (Good X) falls from 500 units to 460 units per month. Compute XED and identify the relationship.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract data.**

$$P_{y1} = 100,\quad P_{y2} = 120,\quad Q_{x1} = 500,\quad Q_{x2} = 460$$

**Step 2 — Compute changes.**

$$\Delta P_y = 120 - 100 = 20$$

$$\Delta Q_x = 460 - 500 = -40$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into formula.**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{-40}{500} \times \frac{100}{20}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$E_{xy} = -0.08 \times 5 = \boxed{-0.4}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- **Negative sign** → Cars and Petrol are **complementary goods**.
- Magnitude **0.4 < 1** → **Weak complement**.
- A 1% rise in petrol price reduces car demand by only 0.4% — they are not strongly tied in this market segment.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example 3 (Point Method)

**Problem:** The demand function for Good X is given by:

$$Q_x = 200 - 2P_x + 3P_y$$

At the current price $P_x = 50$ and $P_y = 30$, find the cross elasticity of demand of X with respect to the price of Y.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find $Q_x$ at given prices.**

$$Q_x = 200 - 2(50) + 3(30) = 200 - 100 + 90 = 190$$

**Step 2 — Find the partial derivative $\frac{\partial Q_x}{\partial P_y}$.**

$$\frac{\partial Q_x}{\partial P_y} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Point Method formula.**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{P_y}{Q_x} \times \frac{\partial Q_x}{\partial P_y}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{30}{190} \times 3 = \frac{90}{190}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{E_{xy} \approx +0.474}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** Positive but less than 1 → Goods X and Y are **weak substitutes** at this price point.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision-Making Application

| **XED Value** | **Goods Type** | **Managerial Decision** |
|---|---|---|
| XED = +2.0 | Strong Substitutes | Price competition is fierce; avoid simultaneous price hikes |
| XED = +0.5 | Weak Substitutes | Mild competition; brand loyalty is strong |
| XED = −1.5 | Strong Complements | Bundle pricing recommended (e.g., printer + ink) |
| XED = −0.3 | Weak Complements | Decouple pricing; joint promotion unnecessary |
| XED = 0 | Independent | Price changes have no spillover; independent demand planning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross Elasticity — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Price of Good Y Changes] --> B{What is the relationship?}
    B -- Substitute Goods --> C[Positive XED: Sign is +]
    B -- Complementary Goods --> D[Negative XED: Sign is -]
    B -- Unrelated Goods --> E[Zero XED: Sign is 0]
    C --> F{Compare Magnitude}
    D --> F
    F -- XED > 1 or XED < -1 --> G[Strong Relationship]
    F -- 0 < XED < 1 or -1 < XED < 0 --> H[Weak Relationship]
    G --> I[Managerial Action: Strategic Pricing or Bundling]
    H --> I
    E --> J[Managerial Action: Independent Pricing Policy]
```

## 4.2 Measurement Method Selection Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Problem Given] --> Q{Type of Data?}
    Q -- Two price points finite change --> R1[Use Arc Percentage Method]
    Q -- Single point with demand function --> R2[Use Point Geometric Method]
    Q -- Only expenditure data given --> R3[Use Total Outlay Method]
    R1 --> S[Compute Ratio and Interpret Sign]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    S --> T{Final Output}
    T -- Positive --> U1[Substitutes]
    T -- Negative --> U2[Complements]
    T -- Zero --> U3[Independent Goods]
```

## 4.3 Goods Classification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[All Goods in Market] --> Sub[Substitutes: XED greater than 0]
    Root --> Comp[Complements: XED less than 0]
    Root --> Unrel[Unrelated: XED equals 0]
    Sub --> Sub1[Perfect Substitutes: XED approaches infinity]
    Sub --> Sub2[Imperfect Substitutes: 0 less than XED less than infinity]
    Comp --> Comp1[Perfect Complements: XED approaches minus infinity]
    Comp --> Comp2[Imperfect Complements: minus infinity less than XED less than 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** The arrows indicate *logical flow* from input (price change) → classification → managerial action. In KTU descriptive answers, sketching a similar flow diagram (even in plain text) earns **evaluation credit** for structured thinking.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define Cross Elasticity of Demand. State the formula used to compute it using the Arc Method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 90-second write-up):**

Cross Elasticity of Demand measures the responsiveness of the quantity demanded of one good (X) to a change in the price of another good (Y), other factors remaining constant. It is given by the formula:

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{Q_x} \times \frac{P_y}{\Delta P_y}$$

A **positive** value indicates substitutes; a **negative** value indicates complementary goods; and **zero** indicates unrelated goods. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for interpretation of sign.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between substitute goods and complementary goods with reference to the sign of Cross Elasticity of Demand. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Substitute Goods | Complementary Goods |
|---|---|---|
| Sign of XED | Positive | Negative |
| Consumption | Used *in place of* each other | Used *together* |
| Example | Tea & Coffee | Car & Petrol |

When the price of one good rises, the demand for a **substitute** rises (switch effect), while the demand for a **complement** falls (joint-use effect). (1 mark per row + 1 mark for explanation.)

---

---

## 📕 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY:**
> - Students frequently **confuse the base value** ($Q_{x1}$ vs. average $Q_x$). The KTU convention is to use the **initial (before-change)** value. Using average values without instruction will be penalized.
> - Forgetting the **sign interpretation** loses 2 marks. Always end with: *"The result shows the goods are substitutes / complements / independent."*
> - Writing the formula without the **full derivation** (especially the ratio cancellation of 100) loses 1 mark in derivation sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Cross Elasticity of Demand. Discuss its significance in managerial decision-making with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The demand for Brand A soap rises from 800 units to 1,000 units per day when the price of Brand B soap (a competitor) increases from ₹40 to ₹50. Calculate the Cross Elasticity of Demand and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition (2 Marks):**
Cross Elasticity of Demand of X with respect to price of Y is the ratio of the percentage change in quantity demanded of X to the percentage change in price of Y.

**Step 2 — Formula (1 Mark):**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{\Delta Q_x}{\Delta P_y} \times \frac{P_y}{Q_x}$$

**Step 3 — Managerial Significance (4 Marks — point-wise):**

1. **Pricing Strategy:** A firm selling good X can anticipate the impact of a rival's price change. If XED is highly positive, raising one's own price slightly may be feasible without losing customers.
2. **Product Line Decisions:** High negative XED justifies *bundling* (e.g., printer + cartridge). High positive XED suggests *diversification* away from competitor's segment.
3. **Market Segmentation:** Firms use XED to identify substitute product clusters and design targeted marketing.
4. **Government Policy:** Taxing complementary goods (e.g., fuel + car) can be coordinated; taxing substitutes requires caution to avoid demand surges in untaxed alternatives.
5. **Demand Forecasting:** Predicts spillover — e.g., a smartphone launch affects demand for earphones, covers, chargers.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Given Data (1 Mark):**

$$Q_{x1} = 800,\quad Q_{x2} = 1000,\quad P_{y1} = 40,\quad P_{y2} = 50$$

**Step 2 — Compute Changes (1 Mark):**

$$\Delta Q_x = 1000 - 800 = 200$$

$$\Delta P_y = 50 - 40 = 10$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Arc Method Formula (2 Marks):**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{200}{800} \times \frac{40}{10}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify (1 Mark):**

$$E_{xy} = 0.25 \times 4 = +1.0$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 Marks):**
- $E_{xy} = +1$ → Brand A and Brand B are **substitute goods** (positive sign confirms substitution).
- Magnitude **equals 1** → **Unitary cross elasticity** — a 1% rise in Brand B's price leads to exactly a 1% rise in Brand A's demand.
- *Managerial insight:* Brand A's managers can confidently raise prices by 1% whenever Brand B raises theirs by 1% without losing market share.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** Using the Total Outlay Method, explain how you would determine whether two goods are substitutes, complements, or independent based on Cross Elasticity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The demand function for good X is $Q_x = 500 - 4P_x + 2P_y$. Given $P_x = 20$ and $P_y = 50$, calculate the cross elasticity of demand of X with respect to Y using the Point Method. Interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Concept of Total Outlay Method (2 Marks):**
Under this method, we observe how the *total expenditure* (price × quantity) on Good X changes when the price of Good Y changes. The direction of change in expenditure reveals the sign of XED.

**Step 2 — Three Cases (4 Marks — Tabular):**

| Price of Y | Expenditure on X | Relationship | XED |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rises | Falls | Substitutes (switch to X) | Positive |
| Rises | Rises | Complements (joint-use) | Negative |
| Rises | Unchanged | Independent | Zero |

**Step 3 — Example (1 Mark):**
If the price of petrol rises and total expenditure on cars *falls* (because car demand drops), then cars and petrol are **complements** — XED is negative.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $Q_x$ at given prices (2 Marks):**

$$Q_x = 500 - 4(20) + 2(50) = 500 - 80 + 100 = 520$$

**Step 2 — Find partial derivative (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{\partial Q_x}{\partial P_y} = 2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Point Method Formula (2 Marks):**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{P_y}{Q_x} \times \frac{\partial Q_x}{\partial P_y} = \frac{50}{520} \times 2$$

**Step 4 — Compute result (1 Mark):**

$$E_{xy} = \frac{100}{520} \approx +0.192$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**
- Positive sign → Goods X and Y are **weak substitutes**.
- Magnitude less than 1 → A 1% rise in Y's price increases X's demand by only 0.192% — competition is mild.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Cross Elasticity = % Change in $Q_x$ ÷ % Change in $P_y$** — always dimensionless.
- ✅ **Positive XED → Substitutes** (Tea-Coffee, Pepsi-Coke).
- ✅ **Negative XED → Complements** (Cars-Petrol, Phones-Earphones).
- ✅ **Zero XED → Independent goods** (Salt-Shoes, Butter-Pencils).
- ✅ **Arc Method** uses *initial* $Q_{x1}$ and $P_{y1}$ as base values (KTU convention).
- ✅ **Point Method** uses derivatives — used for *infinitesimally small* changes.
- ✅ **Total Outlay Method** is a *qualitative* check — does not yield a numeric XED value.
- ✅ **Business Use:** Pricing strategy, product bundling, market definition, demand forecasting.
- ✅ **Always end your answer with a sign interpretation** — this is where most marks are lost.
- ✅ **Do not confuse** Cross Elasticity with Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) or Income Elasticity of Demand (YED) — they have different denominators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:**
> *"Write the formula → plug in the data → show the cancellation → state the numeric value → interpret the sign and magnitude → link to a managerial insight."*
> This 6-step ritual guarantees full marks in KTU 2024 Scheme valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advertising. Demand Forecasting: Application of Qualitative and Quantitative Methods of Demand Forecasting to Various Sectors (Automobile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT525 - MANAGERIAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
